<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET501 - HYDROLOGY AND WATER RESOURCES ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Runoff - Components of Runoff, Hydrograph Analysis - Hydrograph from Isolated Storm, Base Flow Separation, Unit Hydrograph - Uses, Assumptions and Limitations of Unit Hydrograph Theory, Computation of Storm/Flood Hydrograph of Different Duration by Method of Superposition and by Development of S-Hydrograph

# Module 2: Runoff and Hydrograph Analysis

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Runoff – The Fundamental Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Runoff** is defined as the portion of precipitation that flows over the land surface towards streams, rivers, and eventually to the ocean. It is the water that appears in surface streams after precipitation, representing the integrated response of a drainage basin to a storm event.

In quantitative hydrology, runoff is measured as the volume (or depth in mm) of water discharged from a catchment through its outlet per unit time, expressed in **cubic metres per second (m³/s)** or **cumecs**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a sponge (your watershed) placed under a running tap (rainfall). The sponge absorbs some water (infiltration), holds some (depression storage), and the excess water trickles out from the bottom (runoff). The water coming out of the sponge is exactly what hydrologists call **runoff** — the water that *refuses* to stay and *travels* towards the stream outlet. Just as the rate at which water leaves the sponge depends on how much you squeezed it, runoff depends on rainfall intensity, soil type, slope, vegetation, and catchment area.

### 1.2 The Three Components of Runoff

The total runoff reaching the stream is divided into three distinct components based on the pathway the water takes to reach the stream channel:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Focus:** Questions on identifying and explaining the three components of runoff are repeatedly asked. Memorize the definitions, the time-scale of travel, and a representative real-world example for each.

| Component | Pathway | Travel Time | Typical Example |
|-----------|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Surface Runoff (Overland Flow)** | Flows over the land surface | Minutes to hours | Water rushing across paved roads during heavy rain |
| **Interflow (Subsurface Flow / Throughflow)** | Moves laterally through the upper soil layers | Hours to days | Water seeping sideways just below the grass roots |
| **Baseflow (Groundwater Flow)** | Travels through deep aquifers | Days to years | Spring water feeding a stream long after the rain stops |

#### 1.2.1 Surface Runoff (Overland Flow)
This is the water that does not infiltrate into the soil and travels over the land surface under the influence of gravity. It is the **fastest** component and is the primary contributor to flood peaks in small to medium-sized catchments. Horton (1933) described overland flow as occurring when rainfall intensity exceeds the infiltration capacity of the soil.

#### 1.2.2 Interflow (Throughflow)
A portion of infiltrated water moves laterally through the unsaturated upper soil layers (the *vadose zone*) until it re-emerges into a stream channel. Interflow is particularly significant in catchments with shallow, permeable soils overlying an impermeable layer.

#### 1.2.3 Baseflow (Groundwater Flow)
Water that percolates deep into the ground, enters the saturated zone (the *phreatic zone* or aquifer), and reaches the stream slowly through springs and seepage. It sustains the stream during dry periods and is the slowest of the three components.

### 1.3 Hydrograph – Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> A **hydrograph** is a graphical plot of the discharge (runoff) at a stream gauging station versus time. It represents the integrated response of a watershed to a precipitation event, showing how the streamflow rises, peaks, and recedes.

A hydrograph plotted for an **isolated storm** (a single, well-defined rainfall event) over a small, dry catchment exhibits a characteristic shape with three distinct regions:
1. **Rising Limb (Concentration Curve):** The ascending portion where discharge increases rapidly.
2. **Crest (Peak Segment):** The flat, near-horizontal portion containing the peak discharge.
3. **Recession Limb (Depletion Curve):** The descending portion where discharge gradually returns to baseflow.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a hydrograph as an **EKG (electrocardiogram) of a river**. Just as a doctor reads the heartbeat of a patient, a hydrologist reads the "heartbeat" of a watershed. A sharp, narrow hydrograph indicates a flashy, urbanized catchment (heart beating fast), while a broad, flat hydrograph indicates a well-vegetated, permeable catchment (heart beating steadily).

### 1.4 Unit Hydrograph – The Cornerstone Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Sherman, 1932):**
> A **Unit Hydrograph (UH)** is defined as the hydrograph of **direct runoff** resulting from **one unit (e.g., 1 cm or 1 inch) of effective rainfall** (rainfall excess) of **uniform intensity** occurring over the **entire watershed area** for a **specified duration (D)**.

Key elements of the definition:
- **Unit depth** of effective rainfall (1 cm by convention in SI).
- **Uniform intensity** throughout the storm duration.
- **Spatially uniform** — rainfall must cover the whole basin.
- **Specified duration (D)** — the UH applies only to a particular storm duration.
- **Direct runoff only** — baseflow must be separated out first.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A Unit Hydrograph is like a **fingerprint of the watershed**. Just as each person has a unique fingerprint, each watershed has a unique UH that depends on its shape, slope, area, and drainage density. The UH tells you "If 1 cm of rain falls uniformly on me for D hours, this is exactly how I will respond." Once you have the fingerprint, you can scale it up for any storm depth (linearity), or shift it for different storm durations (superposition and S-curve).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Components of Runoff

### 2.1.1 Flow Path Classification

The hydrologic cycle delivers precipitation to a stream through three primary pathways:

1. **Surface Runoff** (Overland Flow / Hortonian Flow)
   - Occurs when rainfall intensity $i$ exceeds the infiltration capacity $f_p$ of the soil.
   - Time of concentration: typically 0.5 to 3 hours.
   - Major contributor to **flood peaks**.

2. **Interflow** (Throughflow / Lateral Subsurface Flow)
   - Lateral movement in the unsaturated zone above the water table.
   - Significant in forested catchments with macropores and high permeability topsoil over low-permeability subsoil.
   - Travel time: hours to weeks.

3. **Groundwater Flow** (Baseflow)
   - Movement through saturated porous media (aquifer).
   - Sustains the stream during dry weather.
   - Travel time: days to centuries (in deep aquifers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Younger Catchments (Mountainous, Steep):** Surface runoff dominates.
> **Older, Flat Catchments (Plains, Alluvial):** Baseflow and interflow dominate.

## 2.2 The Hydrograph from an Isolated Storm

A hydrograph plotted from an **isolated storm** (a single, well-defined rainfall event that produced a distinct, well-separated flood wave) over a small, dry catchment has a characteristic shape with the following components:

### 2.2.1 Structural Elements of a Hydrograph

- **Base Flow:** The normal, sustained flow in the stream before the storm arrives. Represented as $Q_b$ in cumecs.
- **Direct Runoff Hydrograph (DRH):** The portion of the hydrograph that results directly from the storm rainfall. It is the total hydrograph minus the base flow.
- **Peak Discharge ($Q_p$):** The maximum discharge observed during the flood event.
- **Time to Peak ($t_p$):** The time from the start of effective rainfall to the occurrence of the peak.
- **Time Base ($T_b$):** The total duration of the direct runoff hydrograph, from the point of rise to the point of return to base flow.
- **Rising Limb (Concentration Curve):** Steeply rising portion reflecting increasing contribution from various parts of the catchment.
- **Crest Segment:** The relatively flat region near the peak.
- **Recession Limb (Depletion Curve):** Gradually decreasing portion after the peak.

### 2.2.2 Recession Curve Mathematical Form

The recession limb typically follows an exponential decay (Boussinesq/Maillet equation):

$$Q_t = Q_0 \cdot e^{-K \cdot t}$$

where:
- $Q_t$ = discharge at time $t$ after the peak.
- $Q_0$ = discharge at the start of recession (at peak time, or end of crest).
- $K$ = recession constant (day⁻¹ or hour⁻¹).
- $t$ = time elapsed since the start of recession.

The recession constant $K$ is related to the storage coefficient $K_s$ of the basin by:

$$K = \frac{1}{K_s}$$

A large $K$ (fast decay) implies a flashy catchment; a small $K$ (slow decay) implies a well-buffered catchment with significant groundwater storage.

## 2.3 Base Flow Separation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why separate base flow?**
> Unit Hydrograph theory applies ONLY to the **direct runoff** caused by the storm. The base flow, which existed before the storm, must be removed from the total hydrograph to obtain the **Direct Runoff Hydrograph (DRH)**. This is essential because the UH must be derived from and applied to direct runoff only.

### 2.3.1 Straight-Line Method (Simplest & Most Common in KTU Exams)

In this method:
1. Identify the **point A** = the start of the rising limb (where the hydrograph begins to rise sharply above the pre-storm base flow).
2. Identify the **point B** = the end of the recession limb (where the recession curve flattens and rejoins the normal base flow). This typically occurs at time $N$ days after the peak, where:

$$N = A^{0.2}$$

where $A$ is the catchment area in square kilometres. The value of $N$ is approximately 2 to 5 days for most catchments.

3. **Direct Runoff End Point (point C):** A vertical line dropped from the peak to the time axis defines a point. The horizontal line at the base-flow level $Q_b$ intersects the time axis at point C. Connect point A to point C with a **straight line** — this is the base flow separation line.

**Volume of Direct Runoff:**

$$V_{DRO} = \int_{A}^{C} (Q_{total} - Q_{base}) \cdot dt$$

or, equivalently, the **depth of direct runoff** (effective rainfall) over the basin:

$$R = \frac{V_{DRO}}{A_{basin}}$$

where $R$ is the effective rainfall depth in mm (used in UH derivation).

## 2.4 Unit Hydrograph — Theory, Uses, Assumptions & Limitations

### 2.4.1 Uses of Unit Hydrograph

The Unit Hydrograph is one of the most important tools in engineering hydrology:

1. **Flood Forecasting:** Predict the flood hydrograph from forecasted rainfall.
2. **Design Flood Estimation:** Compute the design flood (e.g., 100-year flood) for spillway design of dams and bridges.
3. **Simulation of Catchment Response:** Simulate runoff from complex, multi-peaked storms.
4. **Extension of Short Records:** Extend streamflow records using rainfall data.
5. **Catchment Comparison:** Compare the hydrological response of different basins.
6. **Derivation of Synthetic Unit Hydrographs:** Estimate UH for ungauged catchments (e.g., Snyder's, SCS methods).

### 2.4.2 Assumptions of Unit Hydrograph Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These assumptions define the **limits of validity** of the UH. The KTU Board Examiner often asks to "State the assumptions" — this is a **2-mark guaranteed question**.

1. **Time Invariance:** The UH of a basin is **constant in time**. The basin characteristics (slope, area, vegetation) do not change appreciably between the calibration event and the prediction event.

2. **Linear Response:** The direct runoff response is **linearly proportional** to the effective rainfall depth. If rainfall depth is doubled, the ordinates of the DRH are doubled, and the time base remains the same.

3. **Principle of Superposition:** The DRH from a sequence of storms of equal duration can be obtained by **superposing** (adding with appropriate time lags) the individual UHs.

4. **Time Invariance of Base Flow:** Base flow is assumed to remain **constant** during the UH duration (or is separated out cleanly).

5. **Uniform Spatial Distribution:** The effective rainfall is **uniformly distributed** over the entire catchment area.

6. **Constant Duration:** The UH is for a specific duration $D$. For a different duration, a new UH must be developed.

### 2.4.3 Limitations of Unit Hydrograph Theory

1. **Non-Uniform Rainfall:** Real storms rarely have uniform spatial distribution — intense rainfall in part of the basin and light rain elsewhere violates the assumption.
2. **Non-Linear Response:** For very large storms, the catchment response becomes non-linear. The UH derived from a small storm underestimates the peak of a large storm.
3. **Changing Catchment Conditions:** Land use changes (urbanization, deforestation, afforestation) change the UH over time, violating the time-invariance assumption.
4. **Snowmelt Events:** UH theory is not directly applicable to snowmelt-generated floods, which have fundamentally different physics.
5. **Multi-Peaked Storms:** Complex storm patterns produce hydrographs that may not be accurately decomposed into superposition of UHs.

## 2.5 Computation of Storm Hydrograph of Different Duration

### 2.5.1 Method of Superposition (Direct Method)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use:** To derive a UH of duration $nD$ from a UH of duration $D$ (where $n$ is an integer).

**Procedure:**
- Lag the $D$-hr UH by $D$ hours and add it to the original. Repeat $n$ times.
- The resulting hydrograph is for a storm of depth $n$ cm and duration $nD$ hours.
- Divide all ordinates by $n$ to obtain the **$nD$-hr UH** (depth of 1 cm).

**Mathematical representation:**

$$UH_{nD}(t) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} UH_D(t - kD)$$

### 2.5.2 Development of S-Hydrograph (S-Curve)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The S-hydrograph is the **continuous hydrograph** that would result from a **sustained effective rainfall of intensity 1 cm/hr** (in SI units) continued indefinitely over the basin.

**Derivation Steps:**
1. Place the $D$-hr UH ordinates end-to-end repeatedly, each block lagging the previous by $D$ hours.
2. Add the ordinates at each time ordinate to get the S-curve ordinates.
3. The S-curve asymptotically approaches a constant equilibrium discharge $Q_s$:

$$Q_s = \frac{2.778 \cdot A}{D}$$

where $A$ is the catchment area in km² and $D$ is the UH duration in hours, and the factor 2.778 converts (cm·km²/hr) to m³/s.

**Deriving a UH of Different Duration (T) from S-Curve:**

Let $S_D$ = S-curve from the $D$-hr UH.
Lag $S_D$ by $T$ hours to get $S_D'$.
The difference in ordinates (at corresponding times) gives a hydrograph of volume $T$ cm of excess rainfall in time $T$ hours.
To get the $T$-hr UH (1 cm depth), divide the ordinates by $T$:

$$UH_T(t) = \frac{S_D(t) - S_D(t - T)}{T}$$

**Modified Pulse Method for Non-Integer Ratios:**

If $T/D$ is not an integer, use the **modified S-curve approach**:
- Compute $S_D$ from the $D$-hr UH.
- Lag $S_D$ by $T$ hours.
- Compute the difference hydrograph.
- Multiply the ordinates by the ratio $(D/T)$ to obtain the $T$-hr UH.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE.** It contains every formula you need for the KTU Board Exam on this topic.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Recession curve | $Q_t = Q_0 \cdot e^{-K t}$ | $Q$ in m³/s, $K$ in 1/hr, $t$ in hr |
| 2 | Storage coefficient | $K_s = 1/K$ | $K_s$ in hr |
| 3 | Base flow separation, time of end | $N = A^{0.2}$ | $N$ in days, $A$ in km² |
| 4 | Effective rainfall depth | $R = V_{DRO} / A_{basin}$ | $R$ in cm, $V$ in m³, $A$ in m² |
| 5 | Volume of direct runoff (numerical) | $V_{DRO} = \sum (Q - Q_b) \cdot \Delta t$ | $V$ in m³, $Q$ in m³/s, $\Delta t$ in sec |
| 6 | Equilibrium S-curve discharge | $Q_s = 2.778 \cdot A / D$ | $Q_s$ in m³/s, $A$ in km², $D$ in hr |
| 7 | S-curve to UH of new duration $T$ | $UH_T(t) = [S_D(t) - S_D(t-T)] / T$ | All terms in consistent time units |
| 8 | Superposition for $nD$-hr UH | $UH_{nD}(t) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} UH_D(t - kD)$ | $n$ = integer multiplier |
| 9 | Unit conversion factor | $1 \, \text{cm over 1 km}^2 = 10^4 \, \text{m}^3 = 2.778 \times 10^3 \, \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$ | 2.778 = $10^4 / 3.6$ |
| 10 | Linear UH scaling (for $R$ cm rainfall) | $DRH(t) = R \times UH(t)$ | $R$ in cm (effective rainfall) |

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
- **Dam Spillway Design:** Engineers use UH to estimate the Probable Maximum Flood (PMF) and design spillway capacity.
- **Urban Stormwater Design:** Municipal engineers compute design storms using UH for storm sewer networks.
- **Bridge Hydraulics:** UH-based flood estimation prevents under-designed bridges that wash out during floods.
- **Floodplain Mapping:** UH-derived hydrographs feed 1D/2D flood models used by disaster management authorities.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Effective Rainfall Depth from a Hydrograph

**Statement:** Given the total hydrograph ordinates and the base flow level, derive the effective rainfall depth $R$ over the basin.

**Step 1 — Set up the integral:**

$$V_{DRO} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} [Q_{total}(t) - Q_{base}(t)] \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Discretize using the trapezoidal rule** (typical KTU problem format):

For a time step $\Delta t$ (in hours) and ordinates at times $t_0, t_1, \ldots, t_n$:

$$V_{DRO} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{(Q_i - Q_{b,i}) + (Q_{i-1} - Q_{b,i-1})}{2} \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Convert the volume to equivalent depth:**

$$R \, (\text{cm}) = \frac{V_{DRO} \, (\text{m}^3)}{A_{basin} \, (\text{m}^2)} \times 100$$

**Example Computation:**

Suppose the DRH ordinates (in m³/s) at 1-hour intervals are: 0, 30, 80, 130, 150, 130, 90, 50, 20, 0, and the basin area is 100 km². Then:

$$V_{DRO} = \left[\frac{0+30}{2} + \frac{30+80}{2} + \ldots + \frac{20+0}{2}\right] \times 3600 \, \text{sec}$$

$$V_{DRO} = 680 \times 3600 = 2.448 \times 10^6 \, \text{m}^3$$

$$R = \frac{2.448 \times 10^6}{100 \times 10^6} \times 100 = 2.448 \, \text{cm}$$

This is the **effective rainfall** over the basin.

## 3.2 Derivation of S-Hydrograph Equilibrium Discharge

**Statement:** Show that the S-curve asymptote $Q_s$ equals $\frac{2.778 \cdot A}{D}$ where $A$ is in km² and $D$ is in hours.

**Step 1 — Physical reasoning:**
After a long time, the basin "fills up" with water and reaches a steady state. In steady state, the rate of inflow of effective rainfall equals the rate of outflow at the basin outlet.

**Step 2 — Inflow rate (effective rainfall intensity):**

A $D$-hr UH corresponds to 1 cm of effective rainfall in $D$ hours.

The equivalent intensity of the *continuous* rainfall feeding the S-curve is:

$$i = \frac{1 \, \text{cm}}{D \, \text{hr}} = \frac{10 \, \text{mm}}{D \, \text{hr}}$$

**Step 3 — Inflow volume per unit time:**

For a basin of area $A$ km²:

$$\text{Inflow rate} = \frac{i \cdot A \cdot 10^6 \, \text{m}^2}{3600 \, \text{s/hr}} = \frac{10^{-2} \, \text{m} \cdot A \times 10^6}{D \times 3600}$$

**Step 4 — Simplification:**

$$Q_s = \frac{A \times 10^4}{D \times 3.6 \times 10^3} = \frac{A \times 10}{3.6 \cdot D} = \frac{2.778 \cdot A}{D} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

The factor 2.778 is the **unit conversion constant** from cm·km²/hr to m³/s.

**Step 5 — Alternative derivation using volume balance:**

The total volume of water under the S-curve equilibrium (a horizontal line at $Q_s$ extending from $t = 0$ to $t = \infty$) is infinite. However, the S-curve is built from individual UHs, and the volume of one UH is:

$$V_{UH} = 1 \, \text{cm} \times A \, \text{km}^2 = 10^4 \cdot A \, \text{m}^3$$

The volume discharged per hour at equilibrium:

$$\text{Rate} = \frac{V_{UH}}{D} = \frac{10^4 A}{D} \, \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$

Converting to m³/s:

$$Q_s = \frac{10^4 A}{D \times 3600} = \frac{2.778 A}{D} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation of a $T$-Hour UH from the S-Curve (Proof)

**Statement:** Show that $UH_T(t) = \frac{S_D(t) - S_D(t-T)}{T}$ gives the $T$-hr UH of unit depth (1 cm).

**Step 1 — Definition of S-curve:**

$$S_D(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} UH_D(t - kD)$$

where the sum is taken over all time-shifted $D$-hr UHs.

**Step 2 — Lag the S-curve by $T$ hours:**

$$S_D(t - T) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} UH_D(t - T - kD)$$

**Step 3 — Take the difference:**

$$S_D(t) - S_D(t - T) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} [UH_D(t - kD) - UH_D(t - T - kD)]$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the physical meaning:**

The right-hand side represents the response to a **storm of duration $T$** and depth $T/D$ cm (since it is the difference of two S-curves separated by $T$ hours). The depth of effective rainfall in this storm is:

$$R_{storm} = \frac{T \, \text{hr}}{D \, \text{hr/cm}} \times 1 \, \text{cm} = \frac{T}{D} \, \text{cm}$$

**Step 5 — Scale to unit depth (1 cm):**

Since the difference hydrograph has a depth of $T/D$ cm, dividing by $T/D$ converts it to a UH of unit depth:

$$UH_T(t) = \frac{S_D(t) - S_D(t - T)}{T/D} \cdot \frac{1}{D} = \frac{S_D(t) - S_D(t - T)}{T}$$

The factor $T/D$ is the depth, and the additional $1/D$ converts the unit correctly. Combined, we get division by $T$. $\blacksquare$

**Verification by dimensional analysis:**

- Numerator: m³/s (discharge)
- Denominator: hours
- Result: m³/s per cm of effective rainfall — **the correct units for a UH** ✓

## 3.4 Worked Example: Method of Superposition

**Problem (KTU Sample):**
A 3-hour UH of a basin has the following ordinates (in m³/s): 0, 25, 50, 75, 60, 40, 20, 5, 0. Determine the storm hydrograph for a 6-hour storm of intensity 2 cm/hr (i.e., 12 cm of effective rainfall).

**Step 1 — Identify duration and depth:**
- Duration of given UH: $D = 3$ hr.
- Required storm duration: $2D = 6$ hr.
- Effective rainfall: $2 \times 3 \times 1 = 6$ cm over 6 hours.

**Step 2 — Lag the 3-hr UH by 3 hours:**

| Time (hr) | $UH_3(t)$ | $UH_3(t-3)$ |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | 25 | 0 |
| 6 | 50 | 25 |
| 9 | 75 | 50 |
| 12 | 60 | 75 |
| 15 | 40 | 60 |
| 18 | 20 | 40 |
| 21 | 5 | 20 |
| 24 | 0 | 5 |
| 27 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 3 — Add ordinates (Superposition):**

$$DRH_{6hr}(t) = UH_3(t) + UH_3(t-3)$$

| Time (hr) | $UH_3(t)$ | $UH_3(t-3)$ | Sum (m³/s) |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|------------|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | 25 | 0 | 25 |
| 6 | 50 | 25 | 75 |
| 9 | 75 | 50 | 125 |
| 12 | 60 | 75 | 135 |
| 15 | 40 | 60 | 100 |
| 18 | 20 | 40 | 60 |
| 21 | 5 | 20 | 25 |
| 24 | 0 | 5 | 5 |
| 27 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

This is the DRH for **6 cm of effective rainfall in 6 hours**.

**Step 4 — Scale to the actual storm (12 cm effective rainfall):**

$$DRH_{actual}(t) = \frac{12}{6} \times DRH_{6hr}(t) = 2 \times DRH_{6hr}(t)$$

| Time (hr) | Actual DRH (m³/s) |
|-----------|-------------------|
| 0 | 0 |
| 3 | 50 |
| 6 | 150 |
| 9 | 250 |
| 12 | 270 |
| 15 | 200 |
| 18 | 120 |
| 21 | 50 |
| 24 | 10 |
| 27 | 0 |

This is the **Direct Runoff Hydrograph** for the 6-hr storm of 12 cm effective rainfall.

**Step 5 — Add base flow** (if given) to obtain the **Total Storm Hydrograph**:

$$Q_{total}(t) = Q_{base} + DRH(t)$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Runoff Pathway (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This block-level functional architecture shows how precipitation is partitioned into the three components of runoff as it travels through the watershed system to the stream outlet.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Precipitation Input] --> B[Canopy Interception]
    B --> C[Net Rainfall Reaching Ground]
    C --> D{Infiltration Capacity vs Rainfall Intensity}
    D -->|Rainfall exceeds capacity| E[Surface Runoff / Overland Flow]
    D -->|Rainfall less than capacity| F[Infiltration into Soil]
    E --> G[Rill and Gully Flow]
    G --> H[Stream Channel Network]
    F --> I[Upper Soil Zone - Unsaturated]
    I -->|Lateral movement| J[Interflow / Throughflow]
    I --> K[Deep Percolation]
    K --> L[Saturated Zone - Aquifer]
    L --> M[Groundwater Flow]
    J --> H
    M --> H
    H --> N[Stream Outlet - Basin Runoff]
    H --> O[Stream Gauging Station]
    O --> P[Total Streamflow Hydrograph]
    P --> Q[Base Flow Separation]
    Q --> R[Direct Runoff Hydrograph DRH]
    R --> S[Unit Hydrograph Derivation]
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- **Green path (A → E → H):** Rapid surface flow — the flood-peak contributor.
- **Blue path (F → I → J → H):** Intermediate subsurface flow — interflow.
- **Deep blue path (F → I → K → L → M → H):** Slow groundwater flow — baseflow sustainer.
- **Final output (P):** Total streamflow hydrograph at the gauging station, which is then split into base flow and DRH for UH analysis.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Anatomy of a Hydrograph from an Isolated Storm

This diagram shows the three cardinal regions of a hydrograph and how base flow separation is performed.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pre-storm Base Flow Qb] --> B[Point A - Start of Rise]
    B --> C[Rising Limb<br/>Concentration Curve]
    C --> D[Peak Discharge Qp<br/>at time tp]
    D --> E[Crest Segment]
    E --> F[Recession Limb<br/>Exponential Decay]
    F --> G[Point B - End of Recession]
    G --> H[Post-storm Base Flow]
    B -.Straight-line baseflow<br/>separation.-> I[Point C - End of DRH]
    I --> J[Direct Runoff Hydrograph DRH]
    J --> K[Volume of DRO / Basin Area<br/>equals Effective Rainfall R cm]
    K --> L[Unit Hydrograph of 1 cm depth]
```

**Reading notes:**
- The area between the **rising/recession limbs** and the **straight base flow line** is the DRH.
- The volume of DRH divided by basin area = **effective rainfall depth $R$**.
- To derive a UH, divide the DRH ordinates by $R$.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: S-Hydrograph Construction Workflow

This flow diagram illustrates how to develop the S-hydrograph and then derive a UH of any other duration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given D-hour Unit Hydrograph Ordinates] --> B[Step 1: Repeat UH block<br/>every D hours]
    B --> C[Step 2: Sum all ordinates<br/>at each time step]
    C --> D[Step 3: S-curve SD is generated]
    D --> E[Step 4: Asymptote reached<br/>at Qs = 2.778 A / D]
    E --> F{What duration T UH<br/>is required?}
    F -->|T is integer multiple<br/>of D| G[Use Method of Superposition]
    F -->|T is any value| H[Use S-curve Method]
    G --> I[Sum D-hour UHs lagged<br/>by D, 2D, 3D ... nD times]
    I --> J[Divide by n to get nD-hour UH]
    H --> K[Step 5: Lag SD by T hours to get SD prime]
    K --> L[Step 6: Compute difference SD - SD prime]
    L --> M[Step 7: Divide difference by T<br/>to get T-hour UH of unit depth]
    J --> N[Final Unit Hydrograph]
    M --> N
```

**Pedagogical note:** This is the **most important diagram** in this section. Master the S-curve workflow — KTU frequently tests the ability to construct an S-curve from a given UH and then derive a new-duration UH.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Components of Runoff — Time-Scale View

This diagram contrasts the three runoff components by travel time and storage volume.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Streamflow at Outlet] --> B[Surface Runoff]
    A --> C[Interflow]
    A --> D[Baseflow]
    B --> E[Travel Time<br/>Minutes to Hours]
    B --> F[Fastest response<br/>Dominant in flood peak]
    C --> G[Travel Time<br/>Hours to Days]
    C --> H[Intermediate response<br/>Significant in humid catchments]
    D --> I[Travel Time<br/>Days to Years]
    D --> J[Slowest response<br/>Sustains stream in dry season]
```

**Engineering interpretation:**
- In **steep, urbanized catchments** → surface runoff dominates.
- In **forested, humid catchments** → interflow is significant.
- In **alluvial/plain catchments** → baseflow is dominant, producing **perennial streams**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "Runoff" and list its three main components.**

**Model Answer:**
**Runoff** is the portion of precipitation that flows over the land surface towards streams, rivers, and eventually to the ocean. It is the water that appears in surface streams as the integrated response of a drainage basin to a storm event, measured in cumecs (m³/s) or mm depth.

The three main components of runoff are:
1. **Surface Runoff (Overland Flow):** Water flowing over the land surface, occurring when rainfall intensity exceeds infiltration capacity.
2. **Interflow (Throughflow):** Water moving laterally through the upper unsaturated soil layers before entering the stream.
3. **Baseflow (Groundwater Flow):** Water reaching the stream through deep aquifers, sustaining the stream during dry periods. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State any three assumptions of the Unit Hydrograph theory.**

**Model Answer:**
The three key assumptions of the Unit Hydrograph theory are:

1. **Time Invariance:** The unit hydrograph of a basin remains constant with time; the basin characteristics do not change.
2. **Linear Response:** The direct runoff ordinates are directly proportional to the effective rainfall depth. A 1 cm UH can be scaled linearly to a 2 cm storm DRH by multiplying ordinates by 2.
3. **Principle of Superposition:** The DRH from a multi-period storm can be obtained by adding the DRHs of the individual storm periods, each lagged by the appropriate time interval. (3 marks)

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Define a **Unit Hydrograph**. State its **four principal assumptions** and **three important uses** in engineering hydrology. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-hour Unit Hydrograph of a basin has the ordinates (in m³/s) given below:

| Time (hr) | 0 | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 | 18 | 21 | 24 |
|-----------|---|---|---|---|----|----|----|----|----|
| UH Ordinate | 0 | 30 | 90 | 150 | 120 | 80 | 50 | 20 | 0 |

Using the **method of superposition**, compute the ordinates of the **9-hour Unit Hydrograph**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Definition (3 marks):**
A Unit Hydrograph is the hydrograph of direct runoff resulting from **one unit depth (1 cm)** of effective rainfall of **uniform intensity** occurring over the **entire watershed area** for a **specified duration (D)**.

**Four Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The basin response is **time-invariant**.
2. The basin response is **linear** (linearity principle).
3. The basin response follows the **principle of superposition**.
4. The effective rainfall is **uniformly distributed** over the basin.

**Three Uses (2 marks):**
1. **Flood forecasting** from predicted rainfall events.
2. **Design flood estimation** for hydraulic structures (dams, bridges, culverts).
3. Derivation of **synthetic unit hydrographs** for ungauged catchments using empirical formulae (e.g., Snyder's).

---

### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**
- $D = 3$ hr.
- Required UH duration: $T = 9$ hr = $3D$.
- $n = 3$.

**Step 2 — Apply the superposition formula:**

$$UH_{3D}(t) = \frac{1}{3} \left[ UH_D(t) + UH_D(t - 3) + UH_D(t - 6) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Tabulate the lagged UHs and their sum:**

| Time (hr) | $UH_3(t)$ | $UH_3(t-3)$ | $UH_3(t-6)$ | Sum | $UH_9(t) = \text{Sum}/3$ |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|-------------|-----|--------------------------|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | 30 | 0 | 0 | 30 | 10 |
| 6 | 90 | 30 | 0 | 120 | 40 |
| 9 | 150 | 90 | 30 | 270 | 90 |
| 12 | 120 | 150 | 90 | 360 | 120 |
| 15 | 80 | 120 | 150 | 350 | 116.67 |
| 18 | 50 | 80 | 120 | 250 | 83.33 |
| 21 | 20 | 50 | 80 | 150 | 50 |
| 24 | 0 | 20 | 50 | 70 | 23.33 |
| 27 | 0 | 0 | 20 | 20 | 6.67 |
| 30 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 4 — Final answer (9-hr UH ordinates in m³/s):**
0, 10, 40, 90, 120, 116.67, 83.33, 50, 23.33, 6.67, 0

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q-A(b)):**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Forgetting to divide the final sum by $n$ (= 3). Many students write the **sum** as the answer, but that is the DRH for **3 cm** of effective rainfall, **not** the **9-hr UH of 1 cm**. Deduct **2 marks** if you forget the division.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Writing $UH_3(t-3)$ in the wrong column — the **lag** column is for the UH shifted to the **right** in time. If you shift the wrong way, your peak will be at the wrong time and your entire hydrograph will be incorrect.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Not extending the time range enough. The 9-hr UH has a longer time base than the 3-hr UH — extend to at least $3 \times 24 = 72$ hr in general, or until all ordinates become zero.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **components of a hydrograph** obtained from an isolated storm. Describe the **straight-line method** of base flow separation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-hour Unit Hydrograph of a basin has the ordinates (in m³/s) given below:

| Time (hr) | 0 | 6 | 12 | 18 | 24 | 30 | 36 | 42 | 48 | 54 | 60 |
|-----------|---|---|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|
| UH Ordinate | 0 | 20 | 60 | 110 | 130 | 110 | 80 | 55 | 30 | 10 | 0 |

The basin area is **200 km²**. Using the **S-curve method**, determine the **4-hour Unit Hydrograph** ordinates. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Sketch (3 marks) — to be drawn by the student:**

A labelled hydrograph showing the following on the discharge-time plot:
- Pre-storm base flow (horizontal line at $Q_b$).
- Point A — start of rising limb.
- Rising limb (steep, concave) up to peak.
- Peak discharge $Q_p$ at time $t_p$.
- Crest segment.
- Recession limb (exponential decay).
- Point B — end of recession where it rejoins base flow.
- Direct Runoff Hydrograph shaded between the storm hydrograph and the base flow line.

**Components of the hydrograph (2 marks):**
- **Rising Limb (Concentration Curve):** Rising portion.
- **Crest (Peak Segment):** Maximum discharge region.
- **Recession Limb (Depletion Curve):** Falling portion.
- **Base Flow:** Sustained low flow before/after the event.
- **Direct Runoff Hydrograph (DRH):** Volume above base flow.

**Straight-line method of base flow separation (2 marks):**
1. Identify Point A: where the hydrograph begins to rise sharply from the pre-storm base flow.
2. Identify Point B: where the recession curve flattens and rejoins the normal base flow. Time of end $N = A^{0.2}$ days (where $A$ is area in km²).
3. Connect Point A to a Point C (directly below the peak at the base flow level) with a **straight line**.
4. The area above this line is the **Direct Runoff Hydrograph**.

---

### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Step 1 — Compute the equilibrium S-curve discharge:**

$$Q_s = \frac{2.778 \cdot A}{D} = \frac{2.778 \times 200}{6} = 92.6 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Construct the S-curve by adding lagged UHs (every 6 hours):**

| Time (hr) | $UH_6(t)$ | Lagged 1 ($UH_6(t-6)$) | Lagged 2 ($UH_6(t-12)$) | ... | S-curve $S_D(t)$ |
|-----------|-----------|------------------------|--------------------------|-----|-------------------|
| 0 | 0 | - | - | - | 0 |
| 6 | 20 | 0 | - | - | 20 |
| 12 | 60 | 20 | 0 | - | 80 |
| 18 | 110 | 60 | 20 | - | 190 |
| 24 | 130 | 110 | 60 | - | 300 |
| 30 | 110 | 130 | 110 | - | 350 |
| 36 | 80 | 110 | 130 | - | 320 |
| 42 | 55 | 80 | 110 | - | 245 |
| 48 | 30 | 55 | 80 | - | 165 |
| 54 | 10 | 30 | 55 | - | 95 |
| 60 | 0 | 10 | 30 | - | 40 |
| 66 | - | 0 | 10 | - | 10 |
| 72 | - | - | 0 | - | 0 |

(Continue adding until the S-curve reaches equilibrium at $Q_s = 92.6$ m³/s.)

**Step 3 — Lag the S-curve by $T = 4$ hours:**

To get $S_D(t-4)$, shift the S-curve ordinates 4 hours to the right and interpolate at intermediate times. (For 6-hr steps, the 4-hr lag requires interpolation, which is standard KTU practice — use linear interpolation.)

**Step 4 — Compute the difference $S_D(t) - S_D(t-4)$:**

This difference represents the DRH for $T = 4$ hr of effective rainfall, with depth $T/D = 4/6$ cm.

**Step 5 — Divide by $T$ (= 4) to obtain the 4-hr UH of 1 cm depth:**

$$UH_4(t) = \frac{S_D(t) - S_D(t-4)}{4}$$

The resulting ordinates (in m³/s per cm) form the required 4-hour Unit Hydrograph.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q-B(b)):**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Dividing by $D$ (the original duration) instead of $T$ (the new duration). The correct denominator is $T = 4$ hr, not 6. Deduct **2 marks**.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Not computing the equilibrium S-curve discharge $Q_s$ — this is a **mandatory check** that your S-curve is correct. KTU examiners expect this verification.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Forgetting to add enough lagged UHs to reach equilibrium. If your S-curve is still rising at the end of your table, your answer is incomplete.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Confusing the S-curve (m³/s vs time) with a UH (m³/s per cm vs time). The S-curve does **not** need division by anything — it is in absolute discharge units.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-FREQUENCY KTU EXAM POINTS — Last-Minute Revision Checklist**

### Core Definitions
- **Runoff:** Portion of precipitation that flows to streams via surface, subsurface, and groundwater paths.
- **Three Components of Runoff:** Surface runoff, interflow, baseflow.
- **Hydrograph:** Plot of stream discharge vs time at a gauging station.
- **Unit Hydrograph (Sherman, 1932):** DRH from 1 cm of effective rainfall, uniform intensity, over the entire basin, for a specified duration $D$.

### Key Components of a Hydrograph
- **Rising Limb (Concentration Curve)** — steep rising portion.
- **Peak Discharge ($Q_p$)** — maximum ordinate at time $t_p$.
- **Crest Segment** — flat region near peak.
- **Recession Limb (Depletion Curve)** — exponential decay: $Q_t = Q_0 e^{-Kt}$.
- **Base Flow** — pre-storm and post-storm sustained flow.

### Base Flow Separation (Straight-Line Method)
- Point A = start of rise; Point C = end of DRH (under peak at base flow level).
- Connect A to C with a straight line; area above is DRH.
- $R = V_{DRO} / A_{basin}$ (in cm).

### Unit Hydrograph Assumptions
1. Time invariance.
2. Linear response.
3. Principle of superposition.
4. Uniform spatial distribution.
5. Constant base flow during event.

### Unit Hydrograph Limitations
- Non-uniform rainfall.
- Non-linear response for large storms.
- Land use changes violate time invariance.
- Not directly applicable to snowmelt events.

### Method of Superposition
- To get $nD$-hr UH from $D$-hr UH: sum $n$ lagged UHs, divide by $n$.
- Formula: $UH_{nD}(t) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} UH_D(t - kD)$.

### S-Hydrograph (S-Curve) Development
- Sustained 1 cm/hr (or unit intensity) storm response.
- Equilibrium discharge: $Q_s = 2.778 A / D$ m³/s, with $A$ in km², $D$ in hr.
- To get $T$-hr UH: $UH_T(t) = [S_D(t) - S_D(t-T)] / T$.

### Critical Conversion Factor
- **2.778** = (10 mm × 10⁶ m²/km²) / (3600 s/hr) — used to convert (cm·km²/hr) to (m³/s).

### Common KTU Exam Numerical Format
- A 2-part Part B question: (a) 7-mark theory (definition, assumptions, sketch); (b) 7-mark numerical (superposition or S-curve derivation).
- Always show **lagged tables** clearly.
- Always state **units** with every answer.
- Always **verify** the S-curve reaches equilibrium $Q_s$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Floods - Methods of Design Flood Estimation - Empirical Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET501 - HYDROLOGY AND WATER RESOURCES ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Floods - Methods of Design Flood Estimation - Empirical Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Flood?

A **flood** is an overflow of water that submerges land which is normally dry. In hydrological engineering terms, a flood is a high flow event in a stream or river, characterized by a peak discharge that exceeds the normal channel capacity and may cause damage to life, property, and infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** A flood is a relatively high flow of water, over the normal channel capacity, occurring at a particular point and time due to various causes such as heavy precipitation, dam break, or sudden snowmelt.

## 1.2 What is a Design Flood?

A **Design Flood** is the flood discharge (peak flow) that is adopted for the design of hydraulic structures such as dams, spillways, bridges, culverts, and levees. The selection of a design flood involves a balance between **safety** (avoiding catastrophic failure) and **economy** (avoiding over-design).

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Flood Estimation (DFE)** is the process of estimating the magnitude of the peak flood discharge that a structure must safely withstand during its operational life. The structure must be designed to safely pass the **Probable Maximum Flood (PMF)** or the **Standard Project Flood (SPF)** depending on the importance and hazard classification.

## 1.3 Classification of Flood Estimation Methods

The estimation of design flood discharge is carried out using different methods, broadly classified as:

| Sl. No. | Method Category | Data Requirement | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Empirical Methods** | Catchment area only | Small ungauged catchments |
| 2 | **Rational Method** | Area, rainfall intensity, runoff coefficient | Urban drainage, small catchments (< 50 km²) |
| 3 | **Unit Hydrograph Method** | Rainfall & runoff data | Medium catchments (gauged/ungauged) |
| 4 | **Flood Frequency Analysis** | Long-term annual peak discharge | Gauged catchments |
| 5 | **Regional Flood Frequency Analysis** | Data from similar regional catchments | Ungauged catchments |
| 6 | **PMF / SPF Estimation** | Hydrometeorological data | Major dams, spillways |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Focus of this Module:** **Empirical Methods** — these are the simplest methods and require **only the catchment area** (A) and regional empirical coefficients. They are highly useful for **preliminary estimates** and for **ungauged catchments** where detailed hydrological data is unavailable.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy: Understanding Empirical Flood Estimation

**Analogy — The "Tailor's Quick Estimate":**

Imagine a tailor needs to stitch a uniform for a new student but has no measurements. Based on the student's *age group* (a rough indicator of size), the tailor uses a **standard size chart** to estimate the uniform dimensions. This is fast and reasonably accurate, but not perfect.

Similarly, an engineer estimating a flood for an ungauged catchment has no actual flood records. By knowing the **catchment area** and the **regional setting** (which decides the empirical coefficient), the engineer can "look up" or "calculate" a quick, approximate peak discharge using **empirical formulas**. Just like the tailor's estimate, the result is approximate but useful for preliminary design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Empirical methods were developed by studying past floods in specific regions (e.g., Dickens studied Central India; Ryves studied South India). Each formula is a **statistical fit** through observed peak flood data, hence their coefficients vary by region.

## 1.5 Why Empirical Methods are Important in KTU Context

- They are **frequently asked in KTU examinations** (Part A — 3 mark questions, and short problems in Part B).
- They form the **basis of preliminary design** before detailed studies.
- They are useful for **ungauged catchments** where the engineer has only the area and a regional map.
- They appear in the KTU **syllabus under Module 2** as a sub-topic of floods.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often use the **wrong empirical formula** for a given region. Each formula has a specific region of applicability. Using Dickens formula in South India (where Ryves is applicable) will give **erroneous results** and cost marks in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Rational Method (Most Important Empirical Method)

The **Rational Method** is the most widely used empirical method for estimating the peak runoff from a small catchment (typically < **50 km²**) due to a specific rainfall event.

### 2.1.1 Underlying Assumptions

1. The rainfall occurs **uniformly** over the entire catchment.
2. The rainfall intensity is **constant** for a duration equal to or greater than the **time of concentration (t_c)**.
3. The peak runoff occurs when the **entire catchment contributes** to the outlet simultaneously (i.e., at time t = t_c).
4. The runoff coefficient **C** is constant during the storm.

### 2.1.2 The Rational Formula

The peak discharge is given by:

$$
Q_p = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{360}
$$

Where:
- $Q_p$ = Peak discharge in **m³/s**
- $C$ = Runoff coefficient (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
- $i$ = Rainfall intensity in **mm/hr** (corresponding to duration = t_c and the chosen return period)
- $A$ = Catchment area in **km²**
- The factor **360** is a unit conversion constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Origin of 360:**
> $Q = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{360}$
> - $A$ in km² = $A \times 10^6$ m²
> - $i$ in mm/hr = $\frac{i}{1000 \times 3600}$ m/s
> - $Q = C \times \frac{i}{1000 \times 3600} \times A \times 10^6 = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A \times 10^3}{3.6} = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{0.0036}$
> - Note: $1/0.0036 = 277.78 \approx 360$ when $C$, $i$, $A$ are used in their standard units (C dimensionless, i in mm/hr, A in hectares) — **a very common exam derivation question**.

### 2.1.3 Runoff Coefficient (C) — A Detailed Look

The runoff coefficient depends on:
- **Land use** (urban, forest, agricultural)
- **Soil type** (perviousness)
- **Slope** of catchment
- **Antecedent moisture** condition

**Standard KTU Table for C values:**

| Surface Type | C Value Range |
|---|---|
| Pavement (asphalt, concrete) | 0.70 – 0.95 |
| Roofs | 0.75 – 0.95 |
| Lawns (sandy soil, flat 2%) | 0.05 – 0.10 |
| Lawns (sandy soil, steep 7%) | 0.15 – 0.20 |
| Lawns (heavy soil, flat 2%) | 0.13 – 0.17 |
| Lawns (heavy soil, steep 7%) | 0.25 – 0.35 |
| Residential (single family) | 0.30 – 0.50 |
| Residential (multi-family, detached) | 0.40 – 0.60 |
| Residential (multi-family, attached) | 0.60 – 0.75 |
| Downtown areas | 0.70 – 0.95 |
| Forest (flat, heavy soil) | 0.10 – 0.20 |
| Cultivated land | 0.20 – 0.40 |

> [!NOTE]
> For composite catchments (mixed land use), use a **weighted average**:
> $C_{composite} = \frac{\sum C_i \cdot A_i}{\sum A_i}$

### 2.1.4 Time of Concentration (t_c)

The time taken for runoff from the **most remote point** of the catchment to reach the outlet. It is the sum of:
- **Overland flow time (t_o)**: Time for water to flow over the ground surface to the nearest channel.
- **Channel flow time (t_ch)**: Time for water to flow through the channel to the outlet.

$$
t_c = t_o + t_ch
$$

Common empirical formulas:
- **Kirpich formula:** $t_c = 0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77} / S^{0.385}$ (minutes; L in m, S in m/m)
- **Izzard's formula:** For overland flow on paved surfaces.
- **Kerby's formula:** $t_o = 0.83 \cdot (L \cdot n)^{0.467} / S^{0.235}$ (minutes)

## 2.2 Other Empirical Formulas (Region-Specific)

These formulas give the **peak flood discharge (Q_P)** as a function of the **catchment area (A)** in **km²** and a **regional coefficient (C)**.

### 2.2.1 Dickens' Formula (1865)

Developed from studies of floods in **Central India** (e.g., rivers like Narmada, Tapti, Son, etc.).

$$
Q_P = C_D \cdot A^{3/4}
$$

Where:
- $Q_P$ = Peak flood discharge in **m³/s**
- $A$ = Catchment area in **km²**
- $C_D$ = Dickens constant (typically **6 to 30**, with 11.4 common; depends on region)

| Region | $C_D$ Value |
|---|---|
| North Indian rivers | 6 – 10 |
| Central Indian rivers | 11 – 14 |
| Coastal Andhra and Orissa | 14 – 19 |
| Northern Bengal | 11 – 16 |
| Mahi, Tapti basins | 22 – 30 |

### 2.2.2 Ryves' Formula (1884)

Developed for **South Indian** catchments (e.g., Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, parts of Kerala).

$$
Q_P = C_R \cdot A^{2/3}
$$

Where:
- $C_R$ = Ryves constant (typically **6.8 to 40**, with 6.8 common for South India)
- For areas in **Madras Presidency (old)**: $C_R = 6.8$
- For areas in **Bengal**: $C_R = 40$ (much higher coefficient, indicating high rainfall)

### 2.2.3 Inglis Formula (1930s)

Developed for the **old Bombay Presidency** (now Maharashtra, parts of Gujarat and Karnataka).

$$
Q_P = \frac{124 \cdot A}{\sqrt{A + 10.4}}
$$

Where:
- $Q_P$ = Peak flood discharge in **m³/s**
- $A$ = Catchment area in **km²**
- **No regional coefficient** — implicit in the formula structure.

### 2.2.4 Nawab Jung Bahadur Formula (1880s)

One of the **oldest empirical formulas**, derived from South Indian (old Hyderabad state) catchments.

$$
Q_P = C_N \cdot A^{0.5}
$$

Where:
- $C_N$ = Coefficient (typically **38 to 1300**, depending on region)
- Rarely used now; primarily of historical interest.

### 2.2.5 Fannings Formula

Used for **small catchments in humid regions** of USA, sometimes adapted for similar Indian catchments.

$$
Q_P = C_F \cdot A^{0.5}
$$

Where:
- $C_F$ = Fannings constant (approximately **2.39** for general use)

### 2.2.6 Modified Formula (CSIRS / CWC)

The **Central Water Commission (CWC)** and other bodies have modified these to:

$$
Q_P = C \cdot A^{0.5}
$$

with $C$ values for different sub-zones of India published in flood estimation reports.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these formulas appear in almost every KTU exam question on this topic.**

| Sl. | Formula | Variables & Units | Region of Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_p = \dfrac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{360}$ | $Q_p$ m³/s; $i$ mm/hr; $A$ km²; $C$ dimensionless | Small urban/ rural catchments (< 50 km²) |
| 2 | $Q_P = C_D \cdot A^{3/4}$ | $Q_P$ m³/s; $A$ km²; $C_D$ = 6 to 30 | Central India |
| 3 | $Q_P = C_R \cdot A^{2/3}$ | $Q_P$ m³/s; $A$ km²; $C_R$ = 6.8 to 40 | South India (old Madras Presidency) |
| 4 | $Q_P = \dfrac{124 \cdot A}{\sqrt{A + 10.4}}$ | $Q_P$ m³/s; $A$ km² | Old Bombay Presidency (Western India) |
| 5 | $Q_P = C_N \cdot A^{0.5}$ | $Q_P$ m³/s; $A$ km²; $C_N$ = 38 to 1300 | Old Hyderabad State (historical) |
| 6 | $Q_P = 2.39 \cdot A^{0.5}$ | $Q_P$ m³/s; $A$ km² | Humid small catchments (Fannings) |
| 7 | $t_c = \dfrac{0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77}}{S^{0.385}}$ | $t_c$ minutes; $L$ m; $S$ dimensionless | Kirpich (small rural catchments) |
| 8 | $C_{composite} = \dfrac{\sum C_i A_i}{\sum A_i}$ | All dimensionless | Mixed land use catchments |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Most Suitable Method |
|---|---|
| **Storm drain design** in urban areas | Rational Method |
| **Culvert design** for highways | Rational Method |
| **Spillway design** for small check dams | Dickens / Ryves |
| **Bridge opening** design on regional roads | Inglis / Dickens |
| **Preliminary flood estimate** for a dam site in ungauged basin | Ryves (South India) or Dickens (Central/North India) |
| **Quick flood estimate** for a small reservoir | Fannings / Nawab Jung Bahadur |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Judgment:** Empirical methods give a **first-cut** estimate. The design engineer should always **cross-verify** with at least one more method (e.g., Unit Hydrograph or Flood Frequency Analysis) before finalizing the design flood for a major structure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Unit Conversion Constant in the Rational Method

The Rational formula is dimensionally derived as follows:

**Given:** $Q_p = C \cdot i \cdot A$ (basic form, dimensions not yet consistent)

**Step 1 — Express $A$ in m²:**
$$
A_{m^2} = A_{km^2} \times 10^6
$$

**Step 2 — Express $i$ in m/s:**
$$
i_{m/s} = \frac{i_{mm/hr}}{1000 \, mm/m \times 3600 \, s/hr} = \frac{i_{mm/hr}}{3.6 \times 10^6}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the basic equation:**
$$
Q = C \times i_{m/s} \times A_{m^2}
$$

$$
Q = C \times \frac{i}{3.6 \times 10^6} \times A \times 10^6
$$

$$
Q = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A \times 10^6}{3.6 \times 10^6}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the powers of 10:**
$$
Q = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{3.6} \times \frac{10^6}{10^6}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{3.6}
$$

**Step 5 — For area in hectares** ($1 \text{ ha} = 0.01 \text{ km}^2$):
$$
A_{ha} = A_{km^2} \times 100
$$

So with $A$ in hectares, $i$ in mm/hr:
$$
Q = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A_{ha} \times 100}{3.6 \times 10^6} = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A_{ha}}{36000} \quad \text{(in m³/s, not the 360 form)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU convention:** $A$ is generally taken in **km²** with the formula $Q = \dfrac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{3.6}$ giving $Q$ in m³/s. Some textbooks use $A$ in **hectares** with denominator **360**. Always check the unit of $A$ before solving — **this is a frequent KTU exam pitfall**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Rational Method (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A small urban catchment of area **2.0 km²** receives a uniform rainfall of intensity **50 mm/hr** for a duration equal to its time of concentration. The weighted runoff coefficient is **0.4**. Calculate the peak runoff.

**Step 1 — Identify the data:**
- $A = 2.0$ km²
- $i = 50$ mm/hr
- $C = 0.4$

**Step 2 — Apply the Rational formula:**
$$
Q_p = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{3.6}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values:**
$$
Q_p = \frac{0.4 \times 50 \times 2.0}{3.6}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator:**
$$
0.4 \times 50 = 20
$$

$$
20 \times 2.0 = 40
$$

**Step 5 — Divide by 3.6:**
$$
Q_p = \frac{40}{3.6} = 11.11 \, m^3/s
$$

**Answer:** $Q_p = 11.11$ m³/s ✓

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Dickens Formula

**Problem:** Estimate the peak flood discharge for a Central Indian catchment of area **250 km²** using Dickens formula. Take $C_D = 14$.

**Step 1 — Identify data:**
- $A = 250$ km²
- $C_D = 14$ (Central India, hilly region)

**Step 2 — Apply Dickens formula:**
$$
Q_P = C_D \cdot A^{3/4}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $A^{3/4}$:**
$$
A^{1/4} = 250^{0.25}
$$

Using logarithms: $\log(250) = 2.3979$
$\log(250^{0.25}) = 0.5995$
$250^{0.25} = 10^{0.5995} = 3.976$

$$
A^{3/4} = A \times A^{-1/4} = \frac{250}{3.976} = 62.88
$$

**Alternatively, direct computation:**
$$
A^{0.75} = e^{0.75 \times \ln(250)} = e^{0.75 \times 5.5215} = e^{4.1411} = 62.93
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $C_D$:**
$$
Q_P = 14 \times 62.93 = 881.0 \, m^3/s
$$

**Answer:** $Q_P = 881$ m³/s ✓

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Ryves Formula

**Problem:** A catchment in old Madras Presidency has an area of **640 km²**. Estimate the peak flood using Ryves formula with $C_R = 6.8$.

**Step 1 — Data:**
- $A = 640$ km²
- $C_R = 6.8$

**Step 2 — Apply Ryves formula:**
$$
Q_P = C_R \cdot A^{2/3}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $A^{2/3}$:**
$$
A^{2/3} = 640^{2/3} = (640^{1/3})^2
$$

$$
640^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{640} \approx 8.6177
$$

$$
(8.6177)^2 = 74.266
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $C_R$:**
$$
Q_P = 6.8 \times 74.266 = 505.0 \, m^3/s
$$

**Answer:** $Q_P = 505$ m³/s ✓

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Inglis Formula

**Problem:** A catchment in old Bombay Presidency has an area of **100 km²**. Estimate the peak flood by Inglis formula.

**Step 1 — Data:**
- $A = 100$ km²

**Step 2 — Apply Inglis formula:**
$$
Q_P = \frac{124 \cdot A}{\sqrt{A + 10.4}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**
$$
\sqrt{A + 10.4} = \sqrt{100 + 10.4} = \sqrt{110.4} = 10.508
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator:**
$$
124 \times 100 = 12400
$$

**Step 5 — Divide:**
$$
Q_P = \frac{12400}{10.508} = 1180.1 \, m^3/s
$$

**Answer:** $Q_P = 1180$ m³/s ✓

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Fannings Formula

**Problem:** A small catchment has an area of **5 km²**. Estimate peak flood using Fannings formula.

**Step 1 — Data:**
- $A = 5$ km²
- $C_F = 2.39$ (Fannings constant)

**Step 2 — Apply Fannings formula:**
$$
Q_P = 2.39 \cdot A^{0.5}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $A^{0.5}$:**
$$
A^{0.5} = \sqrt{5} = 2.2361
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**
$$
Q_P = 2.39 \times 2.2361 = 5.344 \, m^3/s
$$

**Answer:** $Q_P = 5.34$ m³/s ✓

## 3.7 Worked Example 6 — Composite Runoff Coefficient (Mixed Land Use)

**Problem:** A catchment consists of the following land uses. Calculate the weighted runoff coefficient.

| Land Use | Area (ha) | Runoff Coefficient C |
|---|---|---|
| Pavement | 2.0 | 0.90 |
| Lawn (heavy soil, flat) | 1.0 | 0.15 |
| Residential (single family) | 3.0 | 0.40 |

**Step 1 — Total area:**
$$
\sum A_i = 2.0 + 1.0 + 3.0 = 6.0 \, ha
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_i A_i$ for each:**
- Pavement: $0.90 \times 2.0 = 1.80$
- Lawn: $0.15 \times 1.0 = 0.15$
- Residential: $0.40 \times 3.0 = 1.20$

**Step 3 — Sum:**
$$
\sum C_i A_i = 1.80 + 0.15 + 1.20 = 3.15
$$

**Step 4 — Compute weighted C:**
$$
C_{composite} = \frac{\sum C_i A_i}{\sum A_i} = \frac{3.15}{6.0} = 0.525
$$

**Answer:** $C_{composite} = 0.525$ ✓

## 3.8 Comparative Analytical Matrix — All Formulas for the Same Catchment

**Catchment:** $A = 200$ km², Central India (assume appropriate coefficients)

| Method | Coefficient Used | Computation | Peak Flood (m³/s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dickens | $C_D = 14$ | $14 \times 200^{0.75} = 14 \times 53.18$ | **744.5** |
| Ryves | $C_R = 6.8$ | $6.8 \times 200^{2/3} = 6.8 \times 34.20$ | **232.6** |
| Inglis | — | $124 \times 200 / \sqrt{210.4}$ | **1709.5** |
| Rational | $C = 0.4, i = 50$ mm/hr | $0.4 \times 50 \times 200 / 3.6$ | **1111.1** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The wide variation in the above table is **expected** — each formula was calibrated for a specific region, rainfall pattern, and catchment size. The designer must select the formula most appropriate to the location. **Inglis** (Bombay region) gives a very high value because the Western Ghats receive extremely heavy rainfall. **Ryves** gives a lower value because old Madras Presidency generally had lower-intensity rainfall.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Design Flood Estimation Methods Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Design Flood Estimation"] --> nodeB["Empirical Methods"]
    nodeA["Design Flood Estimation"] --> nodeC["Statistical Methods"]
    nodeA["Design Flood Estimation"] --> nodeD["Deterministic Methods"]
    nodeA["Design Flood Estimation"] --> nodeE["Probabilistic Methods"]
    
    nodeB["Empirical Methods"] --> nodeF["Rational Method"]
    nodeB["Empirical Methods"] --> nodeG["Dickens Formula"]
    nodeB["Empirical Methods"] --> nodeH["Ryves Formula"]
    nodeB["Empirical Methods"] --> nodeI["Inglis Formula"]
    nodeB["Empirical Methods"] --> nodeJ["Fannings Formula"]
    nodeB["Empirical Methods"] --> nodeK["Nawab Jung Bahadur"]
    
    nodeF["Rational Method"] --> nodeL["Small Urban Catchments less than 50 km2"]
    nodeG["Dickens Formula"] --> nodeM["Central India Region"]
    nodeH["Ryves Formula"] --> nodeN["South India Region"]
    nodeI["Inglis Formula"] --> nodeO["Western India Old Bombay"]
    nodeJ["Fannings Formula"] --> nodeP["Small Humid Catchments"]
    
    nodeC["Statistical Methods"] --> nodeQ["Flood Frequency Analysis"]
    nodeD["Deterministic Methods"] --> nodeR["Unit Hydrograph Method"]
    nodeE["Probabilistic Methods"] --> nodeS["PMF and SPF Estimation"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Rational Method Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Start Catchment Data"] --> nodeB["Identify Catchment Area A in km2"]
    nodeB["Design Flood Estimation"] --> nodeC["Determine Runoff Coefficient C"]
    nodeC["Design Flood Estimation"] --> nodeD["Compute Time of Concentration tc"]
    nodeD["Design Flood Estimation"] --> nodeE["Select Rainfall Intensity i mm/hr for tc duration and chosen return period"]
    nodeE["Design Flood Estimation"] --> nodeF["Apply Rational Formula Qp equals C times i times A divided by 3.6"]
    nodeF["Design Flood Estimation"] --> nodeG["Check Catchment Area Limit less than 50 km2"]
    nodeG["Design Flood Estimation"] --> nodeH{"Within Limit?"}
    nodeH{"Within Limit?"} -->|Yes| nodeI["Acceptable Use Rational Method"]
    nodeH{"Within Limit?"} -->|No| nodeJ["Use Unit Hydrograph or Flood Frequency Analysis"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow for Empirical Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Select Empirical Method"] --> nodeB{"Catchment Region?"}
    nodeB{"Catchment Region?"} -->|Central India| nodeC["Use Dickens Formula CD times A to the power 3 divided by 4"]
    nodeB{"Catchment Region?"} -->|South India| nodeD["Use Ryves Formula CR times A to the power 2 divided by 3"]
    nodeB{"Catchment Region?"} -->|Western India Old Bombay| nodeE["Use Inglis Formula 124A divided by square root of A plus 10.4"]
    nodeB{"Catchment Region?"} -->|Small Urban less than 50 km2| nodeF["Use Rational Method"]
    nodeB{"Catchment Region?"} -->|Historical Estimate| nodeG["Use Nawab Jung Bahadur Formula CN times A to the power 0.5"]
    
    nodeC["Use Dickens Formula CD times A to the power 3 divided by 4"] --> nodeH["Compute Peak Flood QP in m3 per second"]
    nodeD["Use Ryves Formula CR times A to the power 2 divided by 3"] --> nodeH["Compute Peak Flood QP in m3 per second"]
    nodeE["Use Inglis Formula 124A divided by square root of A plus 10.4"] --> nodeH["Compute Peak Flood QP in m3 per second"]
    nodeF["Use Rational Method"] --> nodeH["Compute Peak Flood QP in m3 per second"]
    nodeG["Use Nawab Jung Bahadur Formula CN times A to the power 0.5"] --> nodeH["Compute Peak Flood QP in m3 per second"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Flood Estimation Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["INPUT BLOCK"]
        nodeA["Catchment Area A"]
        nodeB["Regional Coefficient C"]
        nodeC["Rainfall Intensity i"]
        nodeD["Time of Concentration tc"]
    end
    
    subgraph Processing["PROCESSING BLOCK"]
        nodeE["Empirical Formula Selection Engine"]
        nodeF["Rational Method Module"]
        nodeG["Dickens Ryves Inglis Module"]
    end
    
    subgraph Output["OUTPUT BLOCK"]
        nodeH["Peak Flood Discharge QP in m3 per second"]
        nodeI["Design Flood Recommendation"]
    end
    
    nodeA["Catchment Area A"] --> nodeE["Empirical Formula Selection Engine"]
    nodeB["Regional Coefficient C"] --> nodeE["Empirical Formula Selection Engine"]
    nodeC["Rainfall Intensity i"] --> nodeF["Rational Method Module"]
    nodeD["Time of Concentration tc"] --> nodeF["Rational Method Module"]
    
    nodeE["Empirical Formula Selection Engine"] --> nodeF["Rational Method Module"]
    nodeE["Empirical Formula Selection Engine"] --> nodeG["Dickens Ryves Inglis Module"]
    
    nodeF["Rational Method Module"] --> nodeH["Peak Flood Discharge QP in m3 per second"]
    nodeG["Dickens Ryves Inglis Module"] --> nodeH["Peak Flood Discharge QP in m3 per second"]
    nodeH["Peak Flood Discharge QP in m3 per second"] --> nodeI["Design Flood Recommendation"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**Q: List any three empirical formulas used for the estimation of peak flood discharge and mention the region of applicability of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. | Formula | Region |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dickens: $Q_P = C_D \cdot A^{3/4}$ | Central India (e.g., Narmada, Tapti basins) |
| 2 | Ryves: $Q_P = C_R \cdot A^{2/3}$ | South India (old Madras Presidency) |
| 3 | Inglis: $Q_P = \dfrac{124 \cdot A}{\sqrt{A + 10.4}}$ | Old Bombay Presidency (Western India) |

**(1 mark for each correct formula-region pair)**

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**Q: State the Rational formula for peak runoff and explain the meaning of each term with its units.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
Q_p = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{3.6}
$$

- $Q_p$ = Peak discharge in **m³/s** (1 mark)
- $C$ = Runoff coefficient (dimensionless, 0 to 1) (1 mark)
- $i$ = Rainfall intensity in **mm/hr** (for duration = $t_c$)
- $A$ = Catchment area in **km²**
- **3.6** = Unit conversion constant

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget the **3.6** denominator and just write $Q = C \cdot i \cdot A$, which is dimensionally incorrect. **Always include the conversion constant for full marks.**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Dickens & Rational Method Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q (a)** Describe in detail the Rational Method for the estimation of peak runoff. State its assumptions and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** A catchment in Central India has an area of **400 km²**. Estimate the peak flood discharge using **Dickens formula** with $C_D = 11$. Compare this with the **Ryves formula** using $C_R = 6.8$. Comment on the difference. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — Rational Method (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** The Rational Method is an empirical method used to estimate the peak rate of runoff from a small catchment (typically < 50 km²) due to a rainfall of uniform intensity.

**Rational Formula (1 mark):**
$$
Q_p = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{3.6}
$$

**Assumptions (3 marks):**
1. Rainfall is **uniform** in space over the catchment.
2. Rainfall intensity is **constant** for a duration at least equal to $t_c$.
3. Peak runoff occurs when **the entire catchment area** is contributing to the outlet (i.e., at time $t = t_c$).
4. The runoff coefficient $C$ is **constant** for the storm duration.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. Applicable only to **small catchments** (< 50 km²) — the assumption of uniform rainfall breaks down for larger areas.
2. The choice of $C$ is **subjective** and depends on the engineer's experience.
3. Does not account for **storm movement** over the catchment.
4. Assumes **steady-state** rainfall, which is rarely true in practice.
5. Does not directly account for **storage** in the catchment.

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — Dickens vs. Ryves (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Data (0.5 marks):**
- $A = 400$ km²
- $C_D = 11$ (Dickens, Central India)
- $C_R = 6.8$ (Ryves, South India)

**Step 2 — Apply Dickens formula (2 marks):**
$$
Q_P = C_D \cdot A^{3/4} = 11 \times 400^{0.75}
$$

$$
400^{0.75} = e^{0.75 \times \ln(400)} = e^{0.75 \times 5.9915} = e^{4.4936} = 89.45
$$

$$
Q_P = 11 \times 89.45 = 983.9 \, m^3/s
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Ryves formula (2 marks):**
$$
Q_P = C_R \cdot A^{2/3} = 6.8 \times 400^{2/3}
$$

$$
400^{2/3} = (400^{1/3})^2 = (7.368)^2 = 54.29
$$

$$
Q_P = 6.8 \times 54.29 = 369.2 \, m^3/s
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison and comment (2.5 marks):**

| Method | Peak Flood (m³/s) |
|---|---|
| Dickens | 983.9 |
| Ryves | 369.2 |
| **Difference** | **614.7 m³/s (≈ 2.66× higher for Dickens)** |

**Comment:** The Dickens formula gives a peak flood nearly **2.7 times higher** than the Ryves formula. This is because:
- Dickens was calibrated for **Central Indian rivers** which experience high-intensity rainfall (monsoon-driven) and have steeper catchments.
- Ryves was calibrated for **South Indian catchments** of old Madras Presidency, which generally have more **gentle slopes** and **lower-intensity** rainfall.
- Since the catchment is in **Central India** per the problem statement, the Dickens estimate is more appropriate. Ryves would underestimate the peak.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Inglis & Composite Rational Method Focus

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**Q (a)** A small urban catchment of area **1.5 km²** consists of the following:
- 30% paved area (C = 0.9)
- 40% residential area (C = 0.5)
- 30% lawn on heavy soil, flat (C = 0.15)

The time of concentration is **25 minutes** and the rainfall intensity for a 25-min duration storm of 10-year return period is **80 mm/hr**. Estimate the peak runoff using the **Rational Method**. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** A catchment in the old Bombay Presidency has an area of **625 km²**. Estimate the peak flood discharge using the **Inglis formula**. Compare with the result from the **Rational Method** assuming $C = 0.35$ and $i = 40$ mm/hr. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a) — Composite Rational Method (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute composite runoff coefficient (2 marks):**

$$
C_{comp} = \frac{\sum C_i A_i}{\sum A_i}
$$

| Land Use | Fraction of A | C | $C_i \times A_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Paved | 0.30 | 0.90 | 0.270 |
| Residential | 0.40 | 0.50 | 0.200 |
| Lawn | 0.30 | 0.15 | 0.045 |
| **Total** | **1.00** | — | **0.515** |

$$
C_{comp} = 0.515
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Rational formula (3 marks):**
$$
Q_p = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{3.6}
$$

$$
Q_p = \frac{0.515 \times 80 \times 1.5}{3.6}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**
$$
0.515 \times 80 = 41.2
$$

$$
41.2 \times 1.5 = 61.8
$$

**Step 4 — Final calculation:**
$$
Q_p = \frac{61.8}{3.6} = 17.17 \, m^3/s
$$

**Step 5 — Check applicability (1 mark):**
Area = 1.5 km² < 50 km² ✓ — Rational Method is applicable.

**Answer:** $Q_p = 17.17$ m³/s

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b) — Inglis vs. Rational Comparison (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Inglis formula (3 marks):**
$$
Q_P = \frac{124 \cdot A}{\sqrt{A + 10.4}}
$$

$$
Q_P = \frac{124 \times 625}{\sqrt{625 + 10.4}} = \frac{77500}{\sqrt{635.4}} = \frac{77500}{25.207}
$$

$$
Q_P = 3074.6 \, m^3/s
$$

**Step 2 — Rational Method (3 marks):**
$$
Q_p = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{3.6} = \frac{0.35 \times 40 \times 625}{3.6}
$$

$$
0.35 \times 40 = 14
$$

$$
14 \times 625 = 8750
$$

$$
Q_p = \frac{8750}{3.6} = 2430.6 \, m^3/s
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison (1 mark):**

| Method | Peak Flood (m³/s) |
|---|---|
| Inglis | 3074.6 |
| Rational | 2430.6 |
| **Difference** | **644 m³/s (Inglis ≈ 26% higher)** |

**Comment:** The Inglis formula is calibrated for Western Ghats catchments which receive very high rainfall, hence gives a higher peak. The Rational Method gives a comparable but lower estimate. For a structure of major importance, the higher (Inglis) value is recommended for design safety.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 KTU VALUATION PITFALLS — EMPIRICAL METHODS:**
>
> 1. **Wrong unit of A:** Using hectares in the $A/3.6$ version (should be km²) — the answer will be 100× off. **Always confirm the unit before applying the formula.**
>
> 2. **Missing the 3.6 denominator in Rational Method:** Writing $Q = C \cdot i \cdot A$ directly. This loses 1–2 marks even if the logic is correct.
>
> 3. **Wrong region formula:** Using Ryves for a North Indian catchment or Dickens for a South Indian catchment. **Always identify the region first.**
>
> 4. **Incorrect exponent evaluation:** $A^{3/4}$ or $A^{2/3}$ — students often compute $\sqrt{A}$ or $A^{0.5}$ by mistake. **Show the intermediate calculation steps.**
>
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** In Rational Method questions, failing to list the assumptions costs **at least 2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist and revise before any KTU exam on this topic:**

- [ ] A **flood** is a high flow exceeding the normal channel capacity; a **design flood** is the peak discharge adopted for the safe design of a hydraulic structure.
- [ ] **Empirical methods** require only the catchment area and a regional coefficient; they are used for **ungauged** catchments and **preliminary** estimates.
- [ ] The **Rational Method** $Q_p = C \cdot i \cdot A / 3.6$ is the **most important** empirical method in the KTU syllabus — applicable to **small catchments (< 50 km²)**.
- [ ] The **3.6** denominator arises from unit conversion: $A$ in km², $i$ in mm/hr, $Q$ in m³/s.
- [ ] **Rainfall intensity $i$** must be selected for a duration equal to **$t_c$** and the design **return period** (e.g., 25-year, 50-year).
- [ ] The **runoff coefficient C** depends on land use, soil, slope; for mixed land use, take a **weighted average** $C_{comp} = \sum C_i A_i / \sum A_i$.
- [ ] **Dickens** $Q_P = C_D A^{3/4}$ — **Central India**, $C_D$ = 6 to 30 (typically 11).
- [ ] **Ryves** $Q_P = C_R A^{2/3}$ — **South India**, $C_R$ = 6.8 (Madras Presidency) to 40 (Bengal).
- [ ] **Inglis** $Q_P = 124 A / \sqrt{A + 10.4}$ — **Old Bombay Presidency** (Western India).
- [ ] **Fannings** $Q_P = 2.39 \sqrt{A}$ — small humid catchments.
- [ ] **Nawab Jung Bahadur** $Q_P = C_N \sqrt{A}$ — historical, old Hyderabad state.
- [ ] Different methods give **different results** for the same area because they were calibrated for **different regions** with different rainfall regimes.
- [ ] For design safety, the **highest** estimate (after cross-verification) is generally adopted.
- [ ] **Time of concentration ($t_c$)** is the time for water from the most remote point to reach the outlet — sum of overland flow and channel flow times.
- [ ] Empirical methods do **not** consider storm movement, catchment storage, or detailed hydrograph shape — they give **only peak discharge**.
- [ ] For **major structures** (large dams), empirical methods are **only a first step**; full flood studies (Flood Frequency, Unit Hydrograph, PMF) follow.
- [ ] Always **state the region** and the **chosen coefficient value** before applying any empirical formula — examiners allocate marks for this.
- [ ] **Show all intermediate steps** in the calculation of $A^{3/4}$, $A^{2/3}$, etc. — examiners verify the exponent evaluation.
- [ ] In a **14-mark question**, the format is typically: (a) Theory 7 marks + (b) Numerical 7 marks. **Do not skip the theory part.**
- [ ] **Units must accompany every numerical answer**: e.g., $Q_p = 11.11$ **m³/s** (not just 11.11).
