<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Standard Project Flood (SPF) and Probable Maximum Flood (PMF)

### Standard Project Flood (SPF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Project Flood (SPF):** It is the flood discharge that may be expected from the most severe combination of meteorological and hydrological conditions that are reasonably characteristic of the region. It is **NOT** the maximum conceivable flood, but rather a practical, economically justifiable design flood.

The SPF is determined by studying the most critical combinations of:
- **Antecedent precipitation** (pre-storm moisture conditions)
- **Snowmelt** (if applicable)
- **Storm pattern** (depth, area, duration, direction)
- **Initial reservoir levels** (for dams)

It is generally used for the design of **spillways of large dams** where complete safety against the worst possible flood is not economically viable.

### Probable Maximum Flood (PMF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Probable Maximum Flood (PMF):** It is the **largest flood discharge** that can possibly occur at a given location under the most severe combinations of hydrological and meteorological conditions. It is essentially the **upper physical limit** of flood magnitude.

The PMF is derived from the **Probable Maximum Precipitation (PMP)**, which represents the theoretical maximum precipitation physically possible over a drainage basin. PMF is determined by:
- Maximizing the storm characteristics
- Applying the worst-case basin conditions (saturated soil, full reservoirs)
- Using unit hydrograph principles

It is used for designing **nuclear dams, high-hazard dams**, and structures where failure would result in catastrophic loss of life.

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (The "Dress for the Weather" Analogy):**
> 
> Imagine you are planning a road trip:
> - **SPF** is like packing for the **"worst weather you can reasonably expect"** — a heavy downpour in monsoon, say 3 inches of rain. You'd carry a good raincoat and umbrella.
> - **PMF** is like packing for the **"most extreme weather that has EVER occurred on Earth"** — a category 5 hurricane, a 100-year storm, or even a theoretical "max storm." You'd carry hazmat-grade waterproofing and a submarine.
> 
> In engineering: SPF is the "practical worst" used for most dams. PMF is the "absolute worst" used when human lives downstream are at catastrophic risk.

### Comparison Table

| Parameter | SPF | PMF |
|---|---|---|
| **Magnitude** | Severe but plausible | Theoretical maximum |
| **Basis** | Critical but reasonable combinations | Physical upper limit of precipitation |
| **Use** | Spillways of large dams | High-hazard dams, nuclear projects |
| **Economy** | Economically justifiable | Over-conservative, costly |
| **Derivation** | From historical severe storms | From PMP via maximization |

---

## 1.2 Return Period (Conceptual Ideas Only)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Return Period (T):** The average time interval, usually expressed in years, between occurrences of a hydrological event (flood, drought, rainfall) of a given magnitude or greater. Also known as **Recurrence Interval**.

The return period is **NOT** the time between two successive floods of the same magnitude, but rather the **AVERAGE** time between such events over a very long period.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (The "Lottery Ticket" Analogy):**
> 
> If someone says, *"This is a 100-year flood,"* it does NOT mean the flood happens once every 100 years like clockwork. Instead, it means:
> - In any given year, there is a **1 in 100 (1%) chance** of such a flood occurring.
> - Over a long period (say 10,000 years), you would expect roughly **100 such floods**.
> - You could even have two 100-year floods in consecutive years, and then none for 200 years!
> 
> **Think of it as:** The probability of a flood of magnitude Q being equalled or exceeded in any year = 1/T.

### Risk vs. Return Period

For a structure with design life of **n** years, the **risk (R)** of failure due to at least one occurrence of a flood with return period **T** is:

$$R = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)^n$$

Where:
- **R** = Risk of failure (probability of exceedance during design life)
- **T** = Return period (years)
- **n** = Design life of structure (years)

---

## 1.3 Streamflow Measurement — Area-Velocity Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stream Gauging:** The measurement of the discharge (volume of water flowing per unit time) of a stream/river. The **Area-Velocity Method** is the most fundamental and widely used direct method of stream gauging.

The fundamental continuity equation is:

$$Q = A \times \bar{V}$$

Where:
- **Q** = Discharge (m³/s or cumecs)
- **A** = Cross-sectional area of flow (m²)
- **V̄** = Mean velocity of flow (m/s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (The "Counting Cars on a Highway" Analogy):**
> 
> Imagine cars passing under a bridge:
> - **A (Area)** = Imagine a cross-section of the river as a "gate." The larger the gate, the more cars (water) can pass.
> - **V̄ (Velocity)** = How fast the cars are moving.
> - **Q (Discharge)** = Total cars per second passing the gate = gate area × car speed.
> 
> To measure Q, you need to (1) know the area of the "gate" and (2) measure how fast the cars move. That's the entire essence of the Area-Velocity Method!

---

## 1.4 Selection of Site for Stream Gauging Station

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A stream gauging station must be located at a site where the **stage-discharge relationship is stable, unique, and reliable** over time. The ideal site should minimize errors and represent the true discharge of the stream.

### Key Site Selection Criteria:

1. **Straight Reach:** A relatively long, straight stretch of the channel (preferably 2–3 times the channel width) both upstream and downstream of the gauge site.
2. **Stable Cross-Section:** The cross-section should be **stable** and not subject to significant scour or siltation.
3. **Stable Bed and Banks:** Bed and banks should be firm and non-erodible to maintain the stage-discharge relationship.
4. **Well-defined Channel:** The channel should be well-defined, with no braiding or splitting.
5. **Free from Backwater Effects:** No tidal influence, dam releases, or tributary confluences nearby.
6. **Accessibility:** The site should be easily accessible for installation, operation, and maintenance of equipment.
7. **Sufficient Range:** Capable of measuring the full range of flows (low to flood).
8. **Velocity Adequacy:** Mean velocity should be at least **0.3 m/s** for low flows to avoid errors.

---

## 1.5 Stage-Discharge Curve (Rating Curve)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stage-Discharge Curve (Rating Curve):** A graphical relationship between the **stage (water level/gage height)** and the **discharge (Q)** at a gauging station. It is used to determine the discharge from observed stage readings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (The "Bath Tub Water Level" Analogy):**
> 
> If you pour water into a bathtub at a constant rate, the water level rises. If you increase the flow, the level rises faster. If you record the (level, flow) pairs over many conditions and plot them, you get a unique curve for that bathtub.
> 
> Similarly, a river has its own "personality" — for a given water level (stage), a specific flow (discharge) exists. Plot stage vs. discharge → you get the **rating curve**. Now you only need to read the stage (easy), and the curve tells you the discharge (hard to measure directly).

The general form of the rating curve is:

$$Q = C \times (G - G_0)^n$$

Where:
- **Q** = Discharge
- **G** = Stage (gauge reading)
- **G₀** = Gauge reading at zero flow (datum)
- **C** = Constant
- **n** = Exponent (typically 1.5 to 2.5)

---

## 1.6 Flow Duration Curve (FDC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flow Duration Curve (FDC):** A cumulative frequency curve that shows the **percentage of time** a given discharge is equalled or exceeded during a specific period (usually a year, or the entire period of record).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (The "Salary Percentile" Analogy):**
> 
> Imagine a city's daily streamflow data over 10 years (3,650 days):
> - Sort all daily flow values from highest to lowest.
> - The flow equalled or exceeded on **10% of days** is the high flow.
> - The flow equalled or exceeded on **90% of days** is the low flow (often the driest period).
> - Plot % of time exceeded (x-axis) vs. discharge (y-axis) → **Flow Duration Curve**.
> 
> It's like saying, *"On a 'typical' day, you can count on at least X m³/s being available."*

### Key Characteristics:
- **Steep slope** → highly variable flow (e.g., flashy streams)
- **Flat slope** → steady flow (e.g., rivers fed by lakes/groundwater)
- Used for **water resources planning, hydropower assessment, water supply, and environmental flow studies**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SPF and PMF — Detailed Conceptual Analysis

### Standard Project Flood (SPF) — Operational Logic

The SPF is derived based on the following logical steps:

- **Step 1:** Identify the meteorological conditions that produce severe floods in the region (e.g., cyclones, monsoons, snowmelt).
- **Step 2:** Select the most critical **storm transposition** and **storm combination** scenarios.
- **Step 3:** Determine the **most probable maximum storm** (not the theoretical maximum, but a realistic upper bound).
- **Step 4:** Apply unit hydrograph or flood routing to convert rainfall excess to flood discharge.
- **Step 5:** Add base flow contributions from snowmelt or antecedent rainfall to get the SPF.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SPF?**
> 
> Using PMF for all structures would make engineering projects prohibitively expensive. SPF provides a balance between **safety** and **economy**, ensuring that spillways are designed to handle "rare but plausible" events.

### Probable Maximum Flood (PMF) — Operational Logic

- **Step 1:** Determine the **Probable Maximum Precipitation (PMP)** using statistical maximization of historical storms or meteorological models.
- **Step 2:** Compute rainfall excess by subtracting infiltration losses (assume minimum losses for PMF, i.e., saturated watershed).
- **Step 3:** Apply the unit hydrograph to derive the **PMF hydrograph**.
- **Step 4:** Add base flow and snowmelt contributions (maximized).
- **Step 5:** Route the PMF through the reservoir to determine the required spillway capacity and maximum water level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why PMF?**
> 
> PMF is reserved for **critical structures** where failure would cause catastrophic loss of life or property (e.g., nuclear facilities, dams above populated areas). The cost of over-design is justified by the value of human life.

---

## 2.2 Return Period — Detailed Conceptual Analysis

### Inverse Relationship with Probability

The probability of occurrence of an event of magnitude equal to or greater than the design event, in any given year, is:

$$P = \frac{1}{T}$$

Where:
- **P** = Probability of exceedance in any year (dimensionless)
- **T** = Return period (years)

### Risk of Failure Over Design Life

For a structure designed for a return period **T** with a design life of **n** years, the **risk (R)** of the design event being equalled or exceeded at least once during the design life is:

$$R = 1 - (1 - P)^n = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)^n$$

### KTU Formula Sheet — Master Table

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Variables** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Probability of exceedance in any year | P, T | dimensionless, years |
| $R = 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{T}\right)^n$ | Risk of failure over design life n | R, T, n | dimensionless, years, years |
| $Q = A \times \bar{V}$ | Continuity equation for discharge | Q, A, V̄ | m³/s, m², m/s |
| $Q = C(G - G_0)^n$ | Stage-discharge rating curve | Q, G, G₀, C, n | m³/s, m, m, —, — |
| $\bar{V} = \dfrac{V_{0.2} + 2V_{0.6} + V_{0.8}}{4}$ | Mean velocity (0.6-depth method adapted) | V̄, V at depths | m/s |
| $\bar{V} = \dfrac{\sum v_i d_i}{\sum d_i}$ | Mean velocity (mid-section method) | V̄, vᵢ, dᵢ | m/s, m/s, m |
| $q_i = v_i \times d_i$ | Unit-width discharge | qᵢ, vᵢ, dᵢ | m²/s, m/s, m |
| $Q = \sum q_i \cdot w_i$ | Total discharge (sum of sub-section flows) | Q, qᵢ, wᵢ | m³/s, m²/s, m |
| $P = \dfrac{m}{N+1} \times 100$ | Exceedance probability (% time equalled/exceeded) | P, m, N | %, rank, total |

> [!WARNING]
> **Important:** The "0.6-depth method" is for individual verticals. The mean velocity across a vertical is approximately equal to the velocity at **0.6 of the depth** below the water surface (for a logarithmic velocity profile). This is **different from** the mean velocity over the entire cross-section.

---

## 2.3 Streamflow Measurement — Area-Velocity Method Deep Dive

### Procedure Summary

The Area-Velocity method involves the following:

- **Step 1:** Select a cross-section (gauge site) that is straight, stable, and well-defined.
- **Step 2:** Divide the cross-section into **vertical strips** of equal or unequal width.
- **Step 3:** At each vertical, measure the **depth (dᵢ)** of flow.
- **Step 4:** Measure the **velocity (vᵢ)** at each vertical using a current meter (at 0.2d and 0.8d, or at 0.6d).
- **Step 5:** Compute the **mean velocity** for each vertical.
- **Step 6:** Compute the **discharge** through each sub-section.
- **Step 7:** Sum the discharges of all sub-sections to get the total discharge **Q**.

### Two Standard Methods for Sub-section Discharge:

**(a) Mid-Section Method:**

$$Q = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( v_i \cdot d_i \cdot w_i \right)$$

Where:
- **vᵢ** = Mean velocity at vertical i
- **dᵢ** = Depth at vertical i
- **wᵢ** = Width of sub-section centered on vertical i
- **n** = Number of verticals

**(b) Mean-Section Method:**

$$Q = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \left( \frac{v_i + v_{i+1}}{2} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{d_i + d_{i+1}}{2} \right) \cdot (x_{i+1} - x_i)$$

Where:
- **xᵢ** = Horizontal distance of vertical i from a reference
- Adjacent verticals define sub-section boundaries

### Mean Velocity at a Vertical — Methods:

**Two-Point Method (most common):**

$$\bar{v} = \frac{v_{0.2} + v_{0.8}}{2}$$

Where v₀.₂ and v₀.₈ are velocities at 0.2 and 0.8 of depth below the surface.

**Six-Tenths Method (for shallow streams):**

$$\bar{v} = v_{0.6}$$

**Three-Point Method (for deep streams):**

$$\bar{v} = \frac{v_{0.2} + v_{0.6} + v_{0.8}}{4}$$

---

## 2.4 Stage-Discharge Curve — Theoretical Framework

### Why is the Rating Curve Non-Linear?

The stage-discharge relationship is non-linear because:

- As stage increases, **both** the area and velocity increase.
- The area increases with depth (geometry).
- The velocity increases with hydraulic radius (Manning's equation: V = (1/n)·R^(2/3)·S^(1/2)).
- The discharge Q = A·V, so Q increases faster than linearly with stage.

### Assumptions of a Stable Rating Curve:

- The channel cross-section does not change with time.
- The bed slope is constant.
- The roughness coefficient (Manning's n) is constant.
- No variable backwater effects (e.g., tides, downstream obstructions).

### Limitations:

- **Shifting Control:** If the bed scours or silts, the rating curve shifts.
- **Loop Rating Curves:** During floods, the rising limb and falling limb produce different curves due to unsteady flow effects.

---

## 2.5 Flow Duration Curve — Uses and Characteristics

### Construction Procedure:

- **Step 1:** Collect daily (or weekly/monthly) discharge data for the period of interest.
- **Step 2:** Arrange the discharge values in **descending order** (highest to lowest).
- **Step 3:** Assign a rank **m** to each value (m = 1 for highest, m = N for lowest).
- **Step 4:** Calculate the **exceedance probability** (percent of time equalled or exceeded):

$$P = \frac{m}{N+1} \times 100\%$$

- **Step 5:** Plot **P** (x-axis) vs. **Q** (y-axis) on a suitable graph paper.

### Key Uses of FDC:

| **Use** | **Description** |
|---|---|
| **Water Supply Planning** | Determines the dependable flow available 90% or 95% of the time. |
| **Hydropower Assessment** | Calculates the available head and flow for power generation. |
| **Irrigation Planning** | Identifies reliable flows for irrigation requirements. |
| **Environmental Flows** | Establishes minimum flow requirements to sustain aquatic ecosystems. |
| **Water Quality Studies** | Correlates low flows with pollutant concentration levels. |
| **Reservoir Sizing** | Provides data for storage-yield analysis. |
| **Comparative Studies** | Compares flow characteristics of different rivers. |

### Characteristics of FDC:

- **Slope of Curve:** Steep slope = flashy stream (high variability); flat slope = perennial, steady stream.
- **FDC Position:** A curve to the right of another = greater flows (wetter basin).
- **Shape:** Inflection points indicate transitions between surface flow and groundwater contributions.
- **Long-term FDC:** Based on full period of record; useful for water resources planning.
- **Seasonal FDC:** Provides flow characteristics for specific seasons (monsoon, non-monsoon).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FDC vs. Hydrograph:**
> 
> - **Hydrograph:** Time on x-axis, shows the variation of flow with time for a specific event.
> - **Flow Duration Curve:** Probability on x-axis, shows the overall flow regime.
> 
> FDC eliminates the time sequence and gives a statistical summary of the flow regime.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Risk of Failure Over Design Life

We start with the probability of the design event **NOT** occurring in a given year:

$$P_{\text{safe, 1 year}} = 1 - P = 1 - \frac{1}{T}$$

For the design event **NOT** to occur in **n** consecutive years (assuming independence of events):

$$P_{\text{safe, n years}} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)^n$$

The risk **R** (probability of failure at least once in n years) is the complement of safety:

$$R = 1 - P_{\text{safe, n years}}$$

Substituting the expression for safe probability:

$$R = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)^n$$

**Explanation:**
- The first term in parentheses $(1 - 1/T)$ represents the chance of a "non-flood" year.
- Raising it to the power $n$ gives the chance of $n$ consecutive non-flood years.
- Subtracting from 1 gives the chance of at least one flood year in the $n$-year design life.

**Limiting Case Verification:**
- If $n = 1$ (one-year structure), then $R = 1 - (1 - 1/T)^1 = 1/T$. This correctly equals the single-year exceedance probability.
- If $T \to \infty$, then $R \to 0$. This makes sense: an infinitely rare event has zero risk.
- If $T = 1$, then $R = 1 - 0^n = 1$. A 1-year flood is expected every year, so risk is 100%.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean Velocity at a Vertical (Two-Point Method)

The velocity distribution in a vertical is approximately **logarithmic**:

$$v(y) = v_* \cdot \frac{1}{\kappa} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{y}{y_0}\right)$$

Where:
- $v_*$ is the shear velocity
- $\kappa$ is the von Kármán constant (≈ 0.41)
- $y$ is the distance from the bed
- $y_0$ is the zero-velocity depth (roughness length)

The mean velocity over the depth $d$ is the integral of velocity divided by depth:

$$\bar{v} = \frac{1}{d} \int_0^d v(y) \, dy$$

Substituting and integrating (with appropriate boundary conditions):

$$\bar{v} = \frac{v_{0.2d} + v_{0.8d}}{2}$$

**Explanation of Result:**
- The two-point method works because the velocity at 0.2d and 0.8d are symmetric around the mean for a logarithmic profile.
- The arithmetic average of these two readings is the **best estimate** of the depth-averaged velocity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Mid-Section Method for Total Discharge

The total discharge through a cross-section is the integral of point velocity over the cross-sectional area:

$$Q = \int_A v \, dA$$

Discretize the cross-section into **n vertical strips** of width $w_i$ each, with depth $d_i$ and mean velocity $v_i$ at the center of the strip.

For each strip, the discharge is:

$$\Delta Q_i = v_i \cdot d_i \cdot w_i$$

Summing all strips:

$$Q = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i \cdot d_i \cdot w_i$$

**Explanation of Logic:**
- The cross-section is approximated as a series of **rectangular sub-areas**.
- Each sub-area has area $A_i = d_i \cdot w_i$ and mean velocity $v_i$.
- The discharge through each sub-area is $v_i \times A_i$.
- Total discharge = sum of all sub-section discharges.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1: Risk Calculation

**Problem:** A bridge has a design life of 50 years. It is designed for a 100-year flood. Calculate the risk of failure during its design life.

**Given:**
- Design life, $n = 50$ years
- Return period, $T = 100$ years

**Solution:**

$$R = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)^n$$

Substituting values:

$$R = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{100}\right)^{50}$$

Computing the term inside:

$$1 - \frac{1}{100} = 0.99$$

Raising to the 50th power:

$$(0.99)^{50} = e^{50 \cdot \ln(0.99)} = e^{50 \cdot (-0.01005)} = e^{-0.5025} \approx 0.605$$

Therefore:

$$R = 1 - 0.605 = 0.395 = 39.5\%$$

**Interpretation:** There is a **39.5% chance** that the bridge will experience a flood equal to or greater than the 100-year flood during its 50-year life. This is a significant risk!

---

### Example 2: Stream Gauging Using Mid-Section Method

**Problem:** The following data are obtained at a stream gauging station using the mid-section method with a current meter. Calculate the total discharge.

| **Vertical No.** | **Distance from left bank (m)** | **Depth (m)** | **Velocity at 0.2d (m/s)** | **Velocity at 0.8d (m/s)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | — | — |
| 2 | 2 | 1.5 | 0.85 | 0.65 |
| 3 | 4 | 2.4 | 1.10 | 0.90 |
| 4 | 6 | 2.8 | 1.30 | 1.10 |
| 5 | 8 | 2.0 | 0.95 | 0.75 |
| 6 | 10 | 0 | — | — |

**Step 1: Compute Mean Velocity at each vertical**

Using the two-point method: $\bar{v}_i = (v_{0.2} + v_{0.8})/2$

For Vertical 2:
$$\bar{v}_2 = \frac{0.85 + 0.65}{2} = 0.75 \text{ m/s}$$

For Vertical 3:
$$\bar{v}_3 = \frac{1.10 + 0.90}{2} = 1.00 \text{ m/s}$$

For Vertical 4:
$$\bar{v}_4 = \frac{1.30 + 1.10}{2} = 1.20 \text{ m/s}$$

For Vertical 5:
$$\bar{v}_5 = \frac{0.95 + 0.75}{2} = 0.85 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute Sub-section Widths**

The width for vertical $i$ in mid-section method is half the distance to adjacent verticals.

- For Vertical 2: $w_2 = (4-0)/2 = 2$ m
- For Vertical 3: $w_3 = (6-2)/2 = 2$ m
- For Vertical 4: $w_4 = (8-4)/2 = 2$ m
- For Vertical 5: $w_5 = (10-6)/2 = 2$ m

**Step 3: Compute Sub-section Discharges**

For Vertical 2:
$$Q_2 = \bar{v}_2 \cdot d_2 \cdot w_2 = 0.75 \times 1.5 \times 2 = 2.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

For Vertical 3:
$$Q_3 = 1.00 \times 2.4 \times 2 = 4.80 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

For Vertical 4:
$$Q_4 = 1.20 \times 2.8 \times 2 = 6.72 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

For Vertical 5:
$$Q_5 = 0.85 \times 2.0 \times 2 = 3.40 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Total Discharge**

$$Q = Q_2 + Q_3 + Q_4 + Q_5$$

$$Q = 2.25 + 4.80 + 6.72 + 3.40$$

$$Q = 17.17 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Answer:** The total discharge at the gauging station is **17.17 m³/s**.

---

### Example 3: Flow Duration Curve Computation

**Problem:** The following daily discharge values (in m³/s) are recorded for 10 days: 50, 120, 80, 200, 30, 90, 150, 60, 110, 70. Construct the flow duration curve by computing the percentage of time each flow is equalled or exceeded.

**Step 1: Sort in Descending Order**

| **Rank (m)** | **Discharge (m³/s)** |
|---|---|
| 1 | 200 |
| 2 | 150 |
| 3 | 120 |
| 4 | 110 |
| 5 | 90 |
| 6 | 80 |
| 7 | 70 |
| 8 | 60 |
| 9 | 50 |
| 10 | 30 |

**Step 2: Compute Exceedance Probability**

Using Weibull's formula: $P = m/(N+1) \times 100\%$, where $N = 10$

| **Rank (m)** | **Discharge (m³/s)** | **P (%)** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 200 | 9.09% |
| 2 | 150 | 18.18% |
| 3 | 120 | 27.27% |
| 4 | 110 | 36.36% |
| 5 | 90 | 45.45% |
| 6 | 80 | 54.55% |
| 7 | 70 | 63.64% |
| 8 | 60 | 72.73% |
| 9 | 50 | 81.82% |
| 10 | 30 | 90.91% |

**Step 3: Interpretation**

- The flow of **50 m³/s is equalled or exceeded 81.82% of the time** (i.e., on ~300 days/year).
- The flow of **150 m³/s is equalled or exceeded only 18.18% of the time** (i.e., on ~66 days/year).
- The dependable flow for water supply (say 90% reliability) is between 30 and 50 m³/s.

---

### Example 4: Return Period for a Spillway Design

**Problem:** A dam spillway is designed to pass a flood of 2,500 m³/s. If the design life of the dam is 100 years, what return period should be adopted if the acceptable risk is 10%?

**Given:**
- Acceptable risk, $R = 0.10$
- Design life, $n = 100$ years

**Solution:**

Starting from the risk formula:

$$R = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)^n$$

Rearranging for $T$:

$$1 - R = \left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)^n$$

Taking the nth root:

$$(1 - R)^{1/n} = 1 - \frac{1}{T}$$

Solving for $T$:

$$\frac{1}{T} = 1 - (1 - R)^{1/n}$$

$$T = \frac{1}{1 - (1 - R)^{1/n}}$$

Substituting values:

$$T = \frac{1}{1 - (1 - 0.10)^{1/100}} = \frac{1}{1 - (0.90)^{0.01}}$$

Computing $(0.90)^{0.01}$:

$$(0.90)^{0.01} = e^{0.01 \cdot \ln(0.90)} = e^{0.01 \cdot (-0.1054)} = e^{-0.001054} \approx 0.99895$$

Therefore:

$$T = \frac{1}{1 - 0.99895} = \frac{1}{0.00105} \approx 952 \text{ years}$$

**Interpretation:** To limit the risk to 10% over 100 years, the spillway should be designed for approximately a **950-year flood**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram: Stream Gauging Procedure (Area-Velocity Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Select Gauging Site] --> B[Survey Cross-Section]
    B --> C[Divide Cross-Section into n Verticals]
    C --> D[At Each Vertical: Measure Depth d_i]
    D --> E[Measure Velocity at 0.2d and 0.8d]
    E --> F[Compute Mean Velocity v_i = avg of 0.2d and 0.8d]
    F --> G[Compute Sub-section Width w_i]
    G --> H[Compute Q_i = v_i x d_i x w_i]
    H --> I{All Verticals Done?}
    I -->|No| D
    I -->|Yes| J[Sum Q = Sum of all Q_i]
    J --> K[Record Stage G at Gauge]
    K --> L[Plot Point on Stage-Discharge Curve]
    L --> M[End]
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram above shows the step-by-step procedure for stream gauging using the Area-Velocity Method. The iterative loop ensures that all verticals are processed before the final discharge is computed.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Site Selection Criteria Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ideal Stream Gauging Site] --> B[Hydraulic Criteria]
    A --> C[Geometric Criteria]
    A --> D[Operational Criteria]
    
    B --> B1[Stable Bed Material]
    B --> B2[No Backwater Effects]
    B --> B3[Velocity at least 0.3 m/s]
    B --> B4[No Variable Control]
    
    C --> C1[Long Straight Reach]
    C --> C2[Stable Cross-Section]
    C --> C3[Well-Defined Channel]
    C --> C4[No Braiding]
    
    D --> D1[Accessibility All Year]
    D --> D2[Range Covers Low to Flood Flows]
    D --> D3[Site Security]
    D --> D4[Power and Communication]
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram classifies site selection criteria into three primary categories: hydraulic (flow behavior), geometric (channel shape), and operational (practical considerations). An ideal site satisfies all criteria.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Flood Magnitude Spectrum (SPF vs PMF)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Flood Events] --> B[Common Floods]
    A --> C[Design Flood]
    A --> D[Standard Project Flood SPF]
    A --> E[Probable Maximum Flood PMF]
    
    B --> B1[Return Period 2 to 10 years]
    C --> C1[Return Period 50 to 100 years]
    D --> D1[Most Severe Plausible Flood]
    E --> E1[Physical Upper Limit]
    
    D1 --> D2[Used for Spillways of Large Dams]
    E1 --> E2[Used for High Hazard Dams and Nuclear Projects]
```

**Interpretation:** This diagram visualizes the spectrum of flood magnitudes. PMF is at the extreme upper end, while SPF represents a severe but plausible flood. The choice between them depends on the structure's hazard classification.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Stage-Discharge Curve Development

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect Multiple Gauging Data] --> B[Record Stage G and Discharge Q]
    B --> C[Plot Q vs G on Graph Paper]
    C --> D[Fit Smooth Curve Through Points]
    D --> E{Is Curve Stable?}
    E -->|No| F[Identify Cause of Instability]
    F --> F1[Check for Bed Scour]
    F1 --> F2[Check for Vegetation Growth]
    F2 --> F3[Check for Backwater Effects]
    F3 --> G[Apply Correction]
    G --> A
    E -->|Yes| H[Establish Rating Curve Equation]
    H --> H1[Q = C times G minus G0 to power n]
    H --> I[Use Curve for Future Discharge Estimation]
```

**Interpretation:** The rating curve is developed iteratively. If the curve is unstable, the causes (scour, vegetation, backwater) must be addressed before the curve can be reliably used.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Flow Duration Curve Construction and Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect Daily Discharge Data] --> B[Sort in Descending Order]
    B --> C[Assign Rank m to Each Value]
    C --> D[Compute Exceedance Probability P]

    E --> F[Analyze Curve Characteristics]

    F --> H[Compute Firm Power for Hydropower]
    F --> I[Identify Low Flow Periods]

```

**Interpretation:** The FDC serves multiple downstream applications including water supply planning, hydropower assessment, and reservoir sizing. Its shape provides insight into the basin's hydrological character.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Standard Project Flood (SPF) and Probable Maximum Flood (PMF).**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **SPF** | **PMF** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Flood from most severe but reasonable meteorological conditions. | Flood from the most extreme physically possible conditions. |
| **Severity** | Severe but plausible. | Theoretical upper limit. |
| **Basis** | Historical critical storm combinations. | Probable Maximum Precipitation (PMP). |
| **Application** | Spillways of large dams, barrages. | High-hazard dams, nuclear projects. |
| **Economy** | Economically balanced. | Over-conservative, costly. |
| **Magnitude** | Less than PMF. | Greater than SPF. |

The SPF is generally **40% to 60%** of the PMF for the same watershed.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the concept of return period with a suitable example. Is a 100-year flood likely to occur exactly once in 100 years?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The return period (T) of a hydrological event is the average time interval between successive occurrences of an event of equal or greater magnitude.

**Example:** A 50-year flood has a 1/50 = 2% probability of being equalled or exceeded in any given year.

**No, a 100-year flood does not occur exactly once in 100 years.** It means:
- In any year, there is a 1% chance of such a flood.
- Over a long period (say 10,000 years), about 100 such floods are expected.
- It is possible to have two 100-year floods within 5 years, followed by no 100-year floods for 200 years.
- Return period is a **statistical concept** based on long-term averages, not a rigid time schedule.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **Area-Velocity Method** of stream gauging. List the various methods for measuring the mean velocity at a vertical.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A stream is gauged using the mid-section method. The following observations are made. Calculate the total discharge.

| **Vertical** | **Distance from left bank (m)** | **Depth (m)** | **Velocity at 0.2d (m/s)** | **Velocity at 0.8d (m/s)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0.0 | — | — |
| 2 | 1.5 | 1.2 | 0.60 | 0.40 |
| 3 | 3.0 | 2.0 | 0.80 | 0.60 |
| 4 | 4.5 | 2.4 | 1.00 | 0.80 |
| 5 | 6.0 | 1.8 | 0.70 | 0.50 |
| 6 | 7.5 | 0.0 | — | — |

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a):** [7 Marks]

**Area-Velocity Method — Detailed Procedure:**

The Area-Velocity method is based on the continuity equation: $Q = A \times \bar{V}$, where $Q$ is discharge, $A$ is cross-sectional area, and $\bar{V}$ is mean velocity.

**Steps:**

1. **Site Selection:** Choose a straight, stable, well-defined reach of the stream.
2. **Cross-Section Division:** Divide the cross-section into a number of vertical strips (typically 10–25).
3. **Depth Measurement:** At each vertical, measure the depth of flow using a sounding rod or echo sounder.
4. **Velocity Measurement:** At each vertical, measure the velocity using a current meter (Price AA, Price pygmy, or electromagnetic meter).
5. **Mean Velocity Computation:** Calculate the mean velocity for each vertical using one of the methods below.
6. **Sub-section Discharge:** Compute $Q_i = v_i \cdot d_i \cdot w_i$ for each strip.
7. **Total Discharge:** Sum all sub-section discharges.

**Methods for Mean Velocity at a Vertical:**

| **Method** | **Formula** | **When Used** |
|---|---|---|
| **Two-Point Method** | $\bar{v} = (v_{0.2} + v_{0.8})/2$ | Standard for depths > 0.6 m |
| **Six-Tenths Method** | $\bar{v} = v_{0.6}$ | Shallow streams (depth < 0.6 m) |
| **Three-Point Method** | $\bar{v} = (v_{0.2} + v_{0.6} + v_{0.8})/4$ | Deep streams |
| **One-Point Method (Surface)** | $\bar{v} = 0.85 \times v_{0.0}$ | Quick estimates, with correction factor |

**Part (b):** [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Mean Velocity at each vertical (Two-Point Method):**

- Vertical 2: $\bar{v}_2 = (0.60 + 0.40)/2 = 0.50$ m/s
- Vertical 3: $\bar{v}_3 = (0.80 + 0.60)/2 = 0.70$ m/s
- Vertical 4: $\bar{v}_4 = (1.00 + 0.80)/2 = 0.90$ m/s
- Vertical 5: $\bar{v}_5 = (0.70 + 0.50)/2 = 0.60$ m/s

**Step 2: Sub-section widths (Mid-Section Method):**

- $w_2 = (3.0 - 0)/2 = 1.5$ m
- $w_3 = (4.5 - 1.5)/2 = 1.5$ m
- $w_4 = (6.0 - 3.0)/2 = 1.5$ m
- $w_5 = (7.5 - 4.5)/2 = 1.5$ m

**Step 3: Sub-section Discharges:**

- $Q_2 = 0.50 \times 1.2 \times 1.5 = 0.90$ m³/s
- $Q_3 = 0.70 \times 2.0 \times 1.5 = 2.10$ m³/s
- $Q_4 = 0.90 \times 2.4 \times 1.5 = 3.24$ m³/s
- $Q_5 = 0.60 \times 1.8 \times 1.5 = 1.62$ m³/s

**Step 4: Total Discharge:**

$$Q = 0.90 + 2.10 + 3.24 + 1.62 = 7.86 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Answer:** The total discharge is **7.86 m³/s**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Stage-Discharge Curve**. Discuss its uses, limitations, and the conditions under which it becomes unstable.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A dam is designed to have a 500-year flood as the design flood. If the design life of the dam is 100 years, calculate the risk of failure. If the design life is increased to 200 years, what is the new risk? Comment on the result.

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a):** [7 Marks]

**Stage-Discharge Curve (Rating Curve) — Detailed Explanation:**

A **stage-discharge curve** is a graphical relationship between the water level (stage) at a gauging station and the corresponding discharge. It is also called a **rating curve**.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$Q = C \times (G - G_0)^n$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Discharge
- $G$ = Gauge reading (stage)
- $G_0$ = Gauge reading at zero flow
- $C$ = Constant (depends on channel geometry and roughness)
- $n$ = Exponent (typically 1.5 to 2.5)

**Procedure to Develop:**
1. Conduct stream gauging at various stages (low, medium, high).
2. Record stage $G_i$ and corresponding discharge $Q_i$.
3. Plot $G_i$ (x-axis) vs. $Q_i$ (y-axis).
4. Fit a smooth curve (often a power function) through the points.

**Uses:**
- Continuous discharge estimation from stage records.
- Foundation for flood forecasting.
- Reservoir operation decisions.
- Hydrological modeling input.

**Limitations:**
- Requires periodic re-calibration.
- Affected by **shifting control** (bed changes).
- **Loop rating curves** occur during flood rise and fall.
- Not valid during extreme events outside calibration range.

**Conditions of Instability:**
- **Scour and fill** of bed material.
- **Aquatic vegetation growth** (changes roughness).
- **Ice cover** in winter.
- **Backwater effects** from downstream obstructions.
- **Tidal influences** in coastal rivers.
- **Sediment deposition** in the reach.

**Part (b):** [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Return period, $T = 500$ years
- Design life 1: $n_1 = 100$ years
- Design life 2: $n_2 = 200$ years

**Risk for n₁ = 100 years:**

$$R_1 = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)^{n_1} = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{500}\right)^{100}$$

Computing the term:

$$1 - \frac{1}{500} = 0.998$$

$$(0.998)^{100} = e^{100 \cdot \ln(0.998)} = e^{100 \cdot (-0.002002)} = e^{-0.2002} \approx 0.8187$$

Therefore:

$$R_1 = 1 - 0.8187 = 0.1813 = 18.13\%$$

**Risk for n₂ = 200 years:**

$$R_2 = 1 - (0.998)^{200} = 1 - e^{-0.4004} = 1 - 0.6700 = 0.3300 = 33.00\%$$

**Comment on the Result:**

When the design life is **doubled from 100 to 200 years**, the risk of failure **increases significantly from 18.13% to 33.00%** (an increase of about 15 percentage points, or roughly 82% relative increase).

**Key Insight:** For a fixed return period, the risk of failure is a strongly increasing function of design life. This demonstrates that:
1. Long-life structures (e.g., major dams) must be designed for **much higher return period floods** to maintain acceptable risk.
2. The concept of return period alone is insufficient; it must be combined with design life to assess true risk.
3. For critical infrastructure with very long design life, the PMF (or close to it) may be the only acceptable design flood.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the Mean-Section Method with Mid-Section Method:** Many students mix up the formulas. In the **Mid-Section Method**, each vertical is the center of its sub-section (width = half-distance to adjacent verticals on each side). In the **Mean-Section Method**, sub-sections are bounded by adjacent verticals (each sub-section has two boundary verticals, and the mean depth/velocity of the two is used).
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Two-Point Method Formula:** Some students use $v_{0.6}$ alone (which is the Six-Tenths Method) or incorrectly average. The correct two-point formula is $\bar{v} = (v_{0.2} + v_{0.8})/2$. Using $v_{0.2} + v_{0.6} + v_{0.8}$ divided by 3 is **WRONG**; it should be divided by 4 for the three-point method.
> 
> 3. **Misinterpreting Return Period:** Students often say "a 100-year flood occurs once in 100 years." This is INCORRECT. The correct statement is "the probability of occurrence in any year is 1%."
> 
> 4. **Confusing SPF and PMF Definitions:** PMF is NOT the largest observed flood; it is the theoretical upper limit based on PMP. SPF is a practical design flood, not just any historical flood.
> 
> 5. **Rating Curve Datum Confusion:** Forgetting that $G_0$ is the gauge reading at zero flow, not the bed level. If the gauge is set above the streambed, $G_0 > 0$.
> 
> 6. **Flow Duration Curve Mis-plotting:** Plotting rank versus flow without converting to exceedance probability. Always use $P = m/(N+1) \times 100\%$.
> 
> 7. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing depth in cm and velocity in m/s, or computing $Q$ in m³/min instead of m³/s. **Always show units at every step.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 Topics:**

- **SPF (Standard Project Flood):** Most severe but reasonable flood; used for spillways of large dams.
- **PMF (Probable Maximum Flood):** Theoretical maximum flood from PMP; used for high-hazard dams and nuclear projects.
- **Return Period (T):** Average time between events of equal or greater magnitude; $P = 1/T$ is the annual exceedance probability.
- **Risk Formula:** $R = 1 - (1 - 1/T)^n$ for design life n years.
- **Continuity Equation:** $Q = A \times \bar{V}$ — fundamental to all open-channel flow measurements.
- **Area-Velocity Method:** Direct measurement by dividing cross-section into verticals and summing sub-section discharges.
- **Two-Point Method:** $\bar{v} = (v_{0.2} + v_{0.8})/2$ — for depths > 0.6 m.
- **Six-Tenths Method:** $\bar{v} = v_{0.6}$ — for shallow streams.
- **Three-Point Method:** $\bar{v} = (v_{0.2} + v_{0.6} + v_{0.8})/4$ — for deep streams.
- **Mid-Section Method:** $Q = \sum v_i \cdot d_i \cdot w_i$, where $w_i$ is half the distance to adjacent verticals.
- **Mean-Section Method:** $Q = \sum [(v_i + v_{i+1})/2] \cdot [(d_i + d_{i+1})/2] \cdot (x_{i+1} - x_i)$.
- **Site Selection:** Straight reach, stable cross-section, well-defined channel, no backwater, accessible.
- **Rating Curve:** $Q = C(G - G_0)^n$ — relates stage to discharge; non-linear; needs periodic re-calibration.
- **Causes of Rating Curve Instability:** Scour/fill, vegetation, ice, backwater, tides, sediment.
- **Flow Duration Curve:** % of time equalled/exceeded vs. discharge; sorted descending.
- **Exceedance Probability Formula:** $P = m/(N+1) \times 100\%$ (Weibull plotting position).
- **FDC Slope:** Steep = flashy stream; Flat = steady, perennial stream.
- **FDC Uses:** Water supply, hydropower, irrigation, environmental flows, reservoir sizing.
- **FDC vs. Hydrograph:** FDC has probability on x-axis; hydrograph has time on x-axis.
- **Dependable Flow:** Flow available at a given reliability (e.g., 90% of the time).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always carry 2 decimal places in intermediate calculations, draw clear diagrams for site selection and curve construction, and remember to write units at every step. KTU examiners award full marks only when units are consistent throughout the solution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Irrigation - Necessity, Benefits and Ill Effects, Types: Flow and Lift Irrigation - Perennial and Inundation Irrigation, Soil-Water-Plant Relationships, Irrigation Efficiencies, Computation of Crop Water Requirement: Depth and Frequency of Irrigation, Duty and Delta, Duty - Factors Affecting and Method of Improving Duty, Computation of Crop Water Requirement by Using the Concept of Duty and Delta, Irrigation Structures - Storage Structures - Reservoirs - Types, Zones, Yield of Reservoir

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Irrigation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Irrigation (KTU Standard Definition):**
> *Irrigation is the artificial application of water to soil or land to assist in the growing of agricultural crops, the maintenance of landscapes, and the re-vegetation of disturbed soils, during periods of inadequate rainfall, to ensure sustained agricultural productivity.*

Mathematically, irrigation water supplied to a field compensates for the deficit:

$$I = ET_c - P_e - G_c - \Delta S$$

Where:
- $I$ = Irrigation water applied (mm)
- $ET_c$ = Crop evapotranspiration (mm)
- $P_e$ = Effective precipitation (mm)
- $G_c$ = Groundwater contribution (mm)
- $\Delta S$ = Change in soil moisture storage (mm)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Thirsty Plant and the Watering Can":**
> Imagine a farmer growing rice in Kerala during the dry pre-monsoon months (March–May). Nature has stopped providing rain, but the plant keeps transpiring and losing water through its leaves (much like a human sweating on a hot day). If the soil dries below a critical level, the plant wilts and dies. **Irrigation is the farmer's controlled "watering can"** that supplies precisely the amount of water the plant needs, exactly when it needs it. The challenge for the engineer is to design the pipeline (canal/distribution network), measure how much water each crop "drinks" (crop water requirement), and ensure the supply matches the demand — all while minimizing losses (water that leaks from the can before reaching the plant).

---

## 1.3 Necessity of Irrigation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Irrigation is Indispensable — KTU Board Perspective:**

- **Inadequacy of Rainfall:** India's average annual rainfall is approximately **1190 mm**, but with a coefficient of variation of ~30%, dry farming alone is unreliable. Kerala receives ~3000 mm but has strong seasonal variation.
- **Uneven Distribution of Rainfall:** Monsoon-dependent agriculture fails during dry spells.
- **Growing Multiple Crops in a Year (Rabi + Kharif):** Irrigation enables double or triple cropping.
- **Commercial & High-Value Crops:** Sugarcane, banana, paddy require assured water.
- **Drought Mitigation & Food Security:** Irrigation buffers climate variability.
- **Reclamation of Arid & Semi-Arid Lands:** Bringing deserts under cultivation (e.g., Indira Gandhi Canal in Rajasthan).
- **Employment & Rural Development:** Irrigation creates sustained agricultural employment.
- **Domestic, Industrial, and Hydropower Synergies:** Multipurpose reservoirs serve multiple sectors.

---

## 1.4 Benefits of Irrigation

| **S.No** | **Benefit Category** | **Engineering/Civil Perspective** |
|:--:|:--|:--|
| 1 | Increased Crop Yield | 2x–3x yield over rain-fed agriculture |
| 2 | Multiple Cropping | Enables Rabi + Kharif + Perennial cropping patterns |
| 3 | Famine & Drought Protection | Stabilizes food supply chain |
| 4 | Land Value Appreciation | Irrigated land fetches 3–5x market price |
| 5 | Navigation & Waterways | Irrigation canals double as inland waterways |
| 6 | Domestic & Industrial Water | Reservoir-fed water supply schemes |
| 7 | Hydropower Generation | Linked benefits from storage reservoirs |
| 8 | Recharge of Groundwater | Reduces over-exploitation of aquifers |
| 9 | Afforestation & Ecology | Supports vegetation along canal corridors |
| 10 | Pisciculture (Fisheries) | Reservoir-based aquaculture |

---

## 1.5 Ill Effects of Irrigation

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative Consequences — KTU Module 3 Common Pitfall:**
> Students often write only benefits. The Examiner **deducts 1–2 marks** if ill effects are not enumerated.

- **Waterlogging:** Over-irrigation raises the water table, suffocating root zones.
- **Soil Salinity & Alkalinity:** Capillary rise brings salts to the surface (e.g., Indira Gandhi Canal command area issues).
- **Seepage Losses in Canals:** Reduces downstream availability; causes local waterlogging.
- **Mosquito Menace & Public Health Hazards:** Stagnant water breeds vectors (malaria, dengue).
- **Breeding of Snails (Schistosomiasis):** Critical in tropical irrigation projects.
- **Salt Balance Disruption:** Imported irrigation water contains salts; with no drainage, they accumulate.
- **Increased Construction & Maintenance Cost:** Weirs, dams, canals, distribution systems.
- **Displacement of People:** Reservoir submergence displaces families.
- **Loss of Natural Wetlands & Biodiversity.**
- **Sediment Deposition in Canals** reduces carrying capacity over time.

---

## 1.6 Types of Irrigation

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Classification (KTU Syllabus-Aligned):**

### A. Flow Irrigation and Lift Irrigation

```
                        IRRIGATION
                            |
        +-------------------+-------------------+
        |                                       |
   FLOW IRRIGATION                        LIFT IRRIGATION
   (Gravity-fed, no pumping)               (Pumping required)
        |                                       |
   +----+----+                            +-----+-----+
   |         |                            |           |
Direct    Storage                    River Lift    Well Lift
Diversion (Reservoir-fed)            (Pumps)       (Tube wells,
                                                    Open wells)
```

**Flow Irrigation:**
- Water is diverted by gravity from a river or reservoir through a weir/barrage into a canal network.
- No energy is required for water conveyance from source to farm.
- Examples: Most large Kuttanad (Kerala) paddy canals; Upper Ganga Canal.

**Lift Irrigation:**
- Water is lifted using pumps (electric or diesel) to irrigate lands lying **above** the source water level.
- Suitable for high-lying areas in undulating terrain.
- Examples: Lift irrigation schemes in Palakkad and Idukki (Kerala).

### B. Perennial Irrigation and Inundation Irrigation

| **Feature** | **Perennial Irrigation** | **Inundation Irrigation** |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Water Source** | Reservoir (assured year-round supply) | River (flood/seasonal flow) |
| **Cropping Pattern** | All-season cropping (Rabi, Kharif, Zaid) | Single cropping, mostly Kharif |
| **Timing** | Continuous supply | Only during flood/high-flow season |
| **Water Control** | Regulated through gates & outlets | Uncontrolled, river-driven |
| **Suitability** | Semi-arid/irrigated areas | Deltaic, flood-plain regions |
| **Example** | Bhakra Nangal Project | Kuttanad region below sea level (Kerala) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU One-Liner:**
> *Inundation irrigation = "flood-fed" temporary irrigation;* *Perennial irrigation = "reservoir-fed" continuous irrigation.*

### C. Other Types (Briefly, for completeness)
- **Surface Irrigation:** Furrow, border, basin, flooding.
- **Sprinkler Irrigation:** High-pressure sprays; suitable for sandy/undulating terrain.
- **Drip Irrigation:** Localized water delivery to root zone; 90% efficiency.
- **Sub-surface Irrigation:** Water supplied below the root zone through buried pipes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Soil–Water–Plant Relationship

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Soil-Water-Plant Triangle (Module 3 Core Concept):**

The availability of water to plants is governed by the interplay of **soil texture, soil structure, and plant root characteristics**.

### 2.1.1 Soil Moisture Constants

| **Term** | **Definition** | **Volumetric Range (Typical)** |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Saturation Capacity (S)** | All pore spaces filled with water | ~40–50% |
| **Field Capacity (FC)** | Water held after gravity drainage (≈ 1/3 atm suction) | ~15–30% |
| **Wilting Point (WP)** | Water held so tightly that plant roots cannot extract (≈ 15 atm) | ~5–15% |
| **Permanent Wilting Point** | Plant permanently wilts, cannot recover | Same as WP |
| **Available Water (AW)** | $AW = FC - WP$ | The plant-usable portion |
| **Unavailable Water** | Water below WP | Not extractable |
| **Hygroscopic Coefficient** | Water absorbed from atmosphere | Below WP |
| **Maximum Retentive Capacity** | Water retained against gravity | ≈ FC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note for KTU Board:**
> The **Available Moisture Range (AMR)** = FC − WP is the only water range that the plant can actually use. Irrigation must replenish soil moisture to **near FC** but not above (to avoid leaching and waterlogging).

### 2.1.2 Soil-Water Availability Stages

The diagram below maps soil moisture tension vs. plant availability:

| **Stage** | **Soil Moisture Range** | **Plant Status** |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Stage I (Saturation → FC) | Saturation to FC | Aeration limited; not ideal |
| Stage II (FC → Threshold) | FC to readily available | Easy extraction |
| Stage III (Threshold → WP) | Restriction begins | Stomata close, growth slows |
| Stage IV (Below WP)** | Permanent wilting | Plant dies |

> **Readily Available Moisture (RAM) ≈ 50% of AW** (a common design rule).

### 2.1.3 Plant Root Zone Concepts

- **Root Zone Depth (D):** Depth of soil from which roots extract water (varies: 30 cm for vegetables, 100–150 cm for sugarcane).
- **Maximum Root Depth:** $D_{max}$
- **Effective Root Depth:** Depth at which 80% of roots are concentrated.

The total available water in the root zone is:

$$AW_{total} = (FC - WP) \times \rho_b \times D_{rz}$$

Where $\rho_b$ = bulk density of soil (g/cc) and $D_{rz}$ = root zone depth (mm).

---

## 2.2 Irrigation Efficiencies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 3 — 3-Mark Favorite Topic:** Memorize the *conduit-by-conduit* efficiency definitions.

### 2.2.1 Conveyance Efficiency ($\eta_c$)

$$\eta_c = \frac{W_f}{W_r} \times 100$$

Where:
- $W_f$ = water delivered at the farm/field inlet
- $W_r$ = water released from the canal reservoir/headworks

### 2.2.2 Application Efficiency ($\eta_a$)

$$\eta_a = \frac{W_s}{W_f} \times 100$$

Where:
- $W_s$ = water stored in the root zone (beneficial consumptive use)
- $W_f$ = water delivered to the field

### 2.2.3 Water Use Efficiency (WUE)

$$WUE = \frac{Crop\ Yield\ (kg/ha)}{Water\ Used\ (mm\ or\ m^3/ha)}$$

- **Agronomic WUE:** Above-ground biomass / ET.
- **Economic WUE:** Marketable yield / ET.

### 2.2.4 Overall Project Efficiency (Combined Efficiency)

$$\eta_o = \eta_c \times \eta_a \times \eta_m \times \eta_f$$

Where $\eta_m$ = moisture management efficiency and $\eta_f$ = field channel efficiency.

> **Typical KTU Values:** $\eta_c = 60–80\%$, $\eta_a = 40–60\%$, $\eta_o = 30–50\%$ (gravity), up to **90% for drip**.

### 2.2.5 Distribution Uniformity (DU)

$$DU = \frac{Average\ lowest\ quartile\ depth}{Average\ depth\ applied} \times 100$$

> A **DU > 80%** is desirable for sprinkler systems.

### 2.2.6 Consumptive Use Efficiency

$$\eta_{cu} = \frac{CU_{beneficial}}{CU_{total}} \times 100$$

---

## 2.3 Duty and Delta

### 2.3.1 Delta (Δ)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Delta (Δ) is the **total depth of water** required by a crop during the entire base period (or crop period), expressed in **centimeters (cm)** or **meters (m)**.*

- It represents **the volume of water per unit area** delivered to a crop.
- Delta is the **crop demand parameter** (water-depth based).

### 2.3.2 Duty (D)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Duty (D) is the **area of land** (in hectares) that can be irrigated by a continuous supply of water at the rate of **1 cumec (1 m³/s)** throughout the base period. It is expressed in **hectares per cumec (ha/cumec)**.*

- Duty is the **channel/command parameter** (area-based).
- **Higher duty = more efficient** (less water needed per hectare).

### 2.3.3 Base Period (B)

> The period (in days) from the first watering at the time of sowing to the last watering before harvesting is called the **base period** or **crop period**.

### 2.3.4 Relationship Between Duty and Delta

> [!NOTE]
> **The Key KTU Derivation — must be memorized for Part B:**

Consider a canal carrying a discharge of $1$ cumec continuously for a period of $B$ days.

**Step 1:** Total volume of water supplied by the canal in $B$ days:

$$V = 1 \times (B \times 86400)\ \text{cubic meters}$$

$$V = 86400\, B\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2:** This water irrigates an area equal to $D$ hectares (by definition of duty).

**Step 3:** Volume of water required to irrigate $D$ hectares with delta $\Delta$ (in meters):

$$V = \Delta \times (D \times 10{,}000)\ \text{m}^3$$

$$V = 10{,}000\, D\, \Delta\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4:** Equating both volumes (water supplied = water used):

$$86400\, B = 10{,}000\, D\, \Delta$$

**Step 5:** Solving for the required relationship:

$$\Delta = \frac{8.64\, B}{D}\ \text{(meters)}$$

Or, when $\Delta$ is in cm:

$$\Delta = \frac{864\, B}{D}\ \text{(cm)}$$

Or, when expressed with time $T$ in days:

$$\Delta\, (\text{meters}) = \frac{8.64\, B}{D}$$

> **MNEMONIC:** *"8.64 is for cumec-to-hectare magic."*

### 2.3.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module 3 Formula Reference:**

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Volume supplied by $1$ cumec in $t$ days | $V$ | $V = 86400\, t$ | $\text{m}^3$ |
| Volume to irrigate $A$ hectares with $\Delta$ (m) | $V$ | $V = 10{,}000\, A\, \Delta$ | $\text{m}^3$ |
| **Delta (m) from duty** | $\Delta$ | $\Delta = \frac{8.64\, B}{D}$ | $\text{m}$ |
| **Delta (cm) from duty** | $\Delta$ | $\Delta = \frac{864\, B}{D}$ | $\text{cm}$ |
| **Duty from delta** | $D$ | $D = \frac{8.64\, B}{\Delta}$ | $\text{ha/cumec}$ |
| **Discharge required (Q)** | $Q$ | $Q = \frac{A}{D}$ | $\text{cumecs}$ |
| **Time to irrigate area A** | $t$ | $t = \frac{2.356\, \Delta}{R}$ | $\text{days}$ |
| Reservoir Yield | $Y$ | $Y = \text{Mean annual inflow} - \text{evaporation loss}$ | $\text{m}^3$ or $\text{Mm}^3$ |

> **Cross-check (KTU Trick Question):** Some books use $T$ in *days* and $B$ in *base period days*. Always check the convention used in your problem statement.

---

## 2.4 Factors Affecting Duty and Methods of Improving Duty

### 2.4.1 Factors Affecting Duty

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **12 KTU-Board Favourite Factors:**

1. **Type of Crop** — Rice (low duty, ~500 ha/cumec) vs. Wheat (high duty, ~1800 ha/cumec).
2. **Climate and Season** — Hot/dry season requires more water (lower duty).
3. **Soil Type** — Sandy soils have higher percolation (lower duty).
4. **Topography** — Undulating land needs more water (lower duty).
5. **Method of Irrigation** — Flood < Furrow < Sprinkler < Drip (in terms of duty improvement).
6. **Efficiency of Conveyance** — Lined canals improve duty.
7. **Distance from Source** — Long canals → more seepage (lower duty).
8. **Canal Lining Status** — Lined canals (lower seepage) → higher duty.
9. **Skill of the Farmer** — Better water management → higher duty.
10. **Ground Water Table** — High WT → less water needed (higher duty).
11. **Base Period** — Long base period crops (sugarcane) → different duty.
12. **Intensity of Rainfall** — Effective rainfall reduces duty requirement.

### 2.4.2 Methods of Improving Duty

| **Method** | **Mechanism** |
|:--|:--|
| Canal lining | Reduces seepage losses |
| Closing gaps in canal banks | Prevents unauthorized leakage |
| Land grading & leveling | Avoids water ponding in low spots |
| Proper field channels | Reduces field conveyance losses |
| Efficient irrigation methods | Sprinkler/drip > surface |
| Crop rotation with low water crops | Reduces demand |
| Avoiding over-irrigation | Saves water |
| Constructing efficient outlets | Regulates flow accurately |
| Field-to-field irrigation → Direct | Reduces conveyance loss |

---

## 2.5 Reservoir Types, Zones, and Yield

### 2.5.1 Types of Reservoirs (Storage Structures)

```
                       RESERVOIRS
                            |
        +-------------------+-------------------+
        |                                       |
   SURFACE (SURFACE)                     UNDERGROUND
   (Impounding surface runoff)           (Bank filtration,
        |                                  Aquifer storage)
        |
  +-----+-----+-----+
  |     |     |     |
  POND TANK LAKE  MAJOR
  (small  (med)   RESERVOIR
   irrigation)         (large dams)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Specific Classification (Module 3):**

1. **Storage / Impounding Reservoirs:** Created by constructing a dam across a river/stream (e.g., Idukki Dam).
2. **Diversion / Non-Storage Reservoirs:** No dam; a weir/barrage diverts water to canals (e.g., Lower Ganga Barrage).
3. **Flood-Control Reservoirs:** Designed for flood moderation.
4. **Distribution Reservoirs:** Storage in a distribution network (water supply tanks).
5. **Detention / Balancing Reservoirs:** Re-regulate diurnal flow from upstream.

### 2.5.2 Zones of a Reservoir

| **Zone** | **Description** | **Use** |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Normal Pool Level (NPL) / Full Reservoir Level (FRL)** | Maximum water surface under normal operating conditions | Design water surface |
| **Maximum Water Level (MWL)** | Highest level under design flood | Top of flood storage |
| **Minimum Drawdown Level (MDDL)** | Lowest level to which water can be drawn | Bottom of live storage |
| **Live Storage (Effective Storage)** | Volume between FRL and MDDL | Useful for irrigation/water supply |
| **Dead Storage** | Volume below MDDL | Sediment accumulation |
| **Flood / Surcharge Storage** | Volume between FRL and MWL | Temporary flood absorption |
| **Buffer Storage** | Small additional volume above FRL | Emergency reserve |
| **Valley Storage** | Storage below original stream bed | Always present |

> **KEY RELATION:**
> $$V_{total} = V_{live} + V_{dead} + V_{flood}$$

### 2.5.3 Yield of a Reservoir

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Yield is the volume of water that can be withdrawn from a reservoir in a specified time interval (annually, daily, etc.).*

**Types of Yield:**

| **Yield Type** | **Definition** |
|:--|:--|
| **Average Yield** | Total inflow over a long period (e.g., 50 years) |
| **Safe (Firm) Yield** | Quantity of water that can be supplied with **assured availability** during the **worst drought** in a historical period |
| **Secondary Yield** | Available in addition to firm yield during normal/above-normal years |
| **Draft / Design Yield** | Yield used for project design |

**Safe Yield Equation (Approximate):**

$$Y_{safe} = \bar{I}_{min} - E_v - S_e - I_{loss}$$

Where:
- $\bar{I}_{min}$ = minimum mean annual inflow
- $E_v$ = evaporation loss from reservoir
- $S_e$ = seepage loss
- $I_{loss}$ = infiltration / percolation

**Sediment Yield / Trap Efficiency:**

$$\text{Trap Efficiency} = \frac{Sediment\ deposited}{Sediment\ inflowed} \times 100$$

> **Brune's Curve:** Trap efficiency decreases as capacity/inflow (C/I) ratio decreases.
> **Churchill's Curve:** Used for fine sediments.

**Flood Routing & Yield Estimation:**

The **Rippl's Mass Curve Method** is the classical KTU technique for determining the safe yield of a reservoir. The principle: *plot cumulative inflow vs. cumulative demand; the steepest tangent from peak demand gives the required reservoir capacity.*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Duty–Delta Relationship (Complete Derivation)

> [!NOTE]
> **This derivation is asked almost every KTU End-Semester Examination — Module 3.**

**Given:**
- Discharge in canal = $Q$ cumec
- Base period = $B$ days
- Duty = $D$ hectares/cumec
- Delta = $\Delta$ meters

**Step 1 — Compute Total Volume Supplied by the Canal:**

Water supplied = Discharge $\times$ Time

Since $Q = 1$ cumec and the canal runs continuously for $B$ days:

$$V = 1 \times B \times 24 \times 60 \times 60\ \text{m}^3$$

$$V = 86400\, B\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Area Irrigated:**

By the definition of duty, $1$ cumec of continuous flow irrigates an area of $D$ hectares:

$$A = D\ \text{hectares} = D \times 10{,}000\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Volume of Water Stored in the Root Zone:**

For a delta of $\Delta$ meters applied over an area of $A$ m²:

$$V = \Delta \times A = \Delta \times D \times 10{,}000\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Equate Supply and Demand:**

By conservation, water supplied by the canal = water stored in the field:

$$86400\, B = 10{,}000\, D\, \Delta$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Delta:**

$$\Delta = \frac{86400\, B}{10{,}000\, D}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta = \frac{8.64\, B}{D}\ \text{meters}}$$

**Step 6 — Alternative Forms:**

For delta in centimeters:

$$\Delta = \frac{864\, B}{D}\ \text{cm}$$

For duty in ha/cumec:

$$D = \frac{8.64\, B}{\Delta}\ \text{ha/cumec}$$

**Step 7 — Dimension Check:**

$$
\text{Check: } \frac{86400\ \text{m}^3/\text{day} \times \text{day}}{10{,}000\ \text{m}^2} = \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{m}^2} = \text{m}
$$

✅ Units are consistent (meters of depth).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Crop Water Requirement (Depth & Frequency of Irrigation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard 14-Mark Derivation:**

The net irrigation depth per application is:

$$d_n = \frac{(FC - WP) \times D_{rz} \times \rho_b \times p}{100}$$

Where:
- $FC$ = Field Capacity (% by weight)
- $WP$ = Wilting Point (% by weight)
- $D_{rz}$ = Root zone depth (mm)
- $\rho_b$ = Bulk density of soil (g/cc)
- $p$ = Allowable depletion fraction (0.5 for most crops, 0.6 for deep-rooted)

**Gross Depth of Irrigation (accounting for application efficiency):**

$$d_g = \frac{d_n}{\eta_a}$$

**Frequency of Irrigation (Interval, $F$ in days):**

$$F = \frac{d_n \times 1000}{CU_c \times \eta_{cu}}$$

Where $CU_c$ = daily consumptive use of crop (mm/day).

**Total Water Requirement for Crop Season:**

$$V = \sum d_n \times A = \Delta \times A$$

**Step-by-Step Numerical Example:**

> *A wheat crop in Punjab has FC = 28%, WP = 14%, $\rho_b = 1.4$ g/cc, $D_{rz} = 600$ mm, $p = 0.5$, $CU_c = 5$ mm/day, and $\eta_a = 0.65$. Find (a) net depth, (b) gross depth, (c) irrigation interval, and (d) total water for 1 ha.*

**Solution (a) — Net Depth $d_n$:**

$$d_n = \frac{(28 - 14) \times 600 \times 1.4 \times 0.5}{100}$$

$$d_n = \frac{14 \times 600 \times 0.7}{100} = \frac{5880}{100} = 58.8\ \text{mm}$$

**Solution (b) — Gross Depth $d_g$:**

$$d_g = \frac{58.8}{0.65} = 90.46\ \text{mm}$$

**Solution (c) — Frequency $F$:**

$$F = \frac{58.8}{5} = 11.76 \approx 12\ \text{days}$$

**Solution (d) — Total Volume for 1 hectare:**

$$V = 90.46\ \text{mm} \times 1\ \text{ha} = 0.09046\ \text{m} \times 10{,}000\ \text{m}^2$$

$$V = 904.6\ \text{m}^3 \approx 0.904\ \text{ML}$$

> **KTU Marker Tip:** Always state **"round off to 3 significant figures"** in the final answer.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Discharge Required to Irrigate a Given Area (Full-Working)

**Problem Template (KTU Standard):**
*Given an area of $A$ hectares to be irrigated in $t$ days with a delta of $\Delta$ (meters), find the discharge $Q$ required.*

**Step 1:** Total volume of water needed:

$$V = \Delta \times A \times 10{,}000\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2:** This water must be supplied in $t$ days:

$$V = Q \times t \times 86400\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Equate:

$$Q \times t \times 86400 = \Delta \times A \times 10{,}000$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $Q$:

$$Q = \frac{\Delta \times A \times 10{,}000}{86400 \times t} = \frac{\Delta \times A}{8.64 \times t}\ \text{cumecs}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\Delta \times A}{8.64 \times t}\ \text{cumecs}}$$

**Numerical Example:**

> *A field of 2000 hectares is to be irrigated with a delta of 0.5 m. The base period is 120 days. Find (a) the duty, (b) the discharge required.*

**Solution (a) — Duty:**

$$D = \frac{8.64 \times B}{\Delta} = \frac{8.64 \times 120}{0.5} = 2073.6\ \text{ha/cumec}$$

**Solution (b) — Discharge:**

$$Q = \frac{0.5 \times 2000}{8.64 \times 120} = \frac{1000}{1036.8} = 0.965\ \text{cumec}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Reservoir Yield using Rippl's Mass Curve (Working Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Past Year Favourite — 14-Mark Question on "Determine Safe Yield from a Mass Curve."**

**Steps:**

1. **Prepare the Hydrological Data:** Tabulate inflow (MCM) for each year (typically 30–50 years).

2. **Plot the Mass Curve (Rippl's Curve):** Cumulative inflow vs. time (in years or months).

3. **Plot the Demand Line:** Draw a straight line representing the constant demand rate (the slope of the line equals the safe yield).

4. **Superimpose Demand Lines:** Draw multiple parallel demand lines tangent to the mass curve at various dry-period troughs.

5. **Find the Steepest Possible Demand Line:** The demand line that just touches the mass curve at all critical points represents the **maximum safe yield** (i.e., reservoir never empties).

6. **Compute Reservoir Capacity:** The maximum vertical distance between the demand line and the mass curve (at the driest period) = required **reservoir capacity**.

**Mathematical Condition:**

$$Q_d \leq \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i}{n} = \text{mean annual yield}$$

For a draft of $Q_d$ (in MCM/year), the cumulative surplus over the design period must equal the cumulative deficit.

---

## 3.5 Numerical: Reservoirs – Sediment Storage (Trap Efficiency)

**Example:** A reservoir has a capacity of $250\ \text{Mm}^3$ and an annual inflow of $500\ \text{Mm}^3$. Annual sediment inflow is $2\ \text{Mm}^3$. Find the expected trap efficiency and the dead storage needed for 50 years.

**Step 1:** Compute Capacity-Inflow Ratio:

$$C/I = \frac{250}{500} = 0.5$$

**Step 2:** From **Brune's Curve** (standard table), for $C/I = 0.5$:

$$\text{Trap Efficiency} \approx 0.93\ (93\%)$$

**Step 3:** Sediment trapped per year:

$$S_t = 0.93 \times 2 = 1.86\ \text{Mm}^3/\text{year}$$

**Step 4:** Sediment in 50 years:

$$S_{50} = 1.86 \times 50 = 93\ \text{Mm}^3$$

**Step 5:** This becomes the **dead storage** volume to be allocated.

> **KTU Tip:** Brune's curve is used for predominantly **silt**-sized material; Churchill's curve is preferred for **clay**-dominated sediments.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Cross-Question Mapping

| **KTU Exam Question Type** | **Key Formula to Use** | **Trap** |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Compute delta from duty | $\Delta = 8.64\, B / D$ (m) | Forgetting $8.64$ vs $864$ unit confusion |
| Compute duty from delta | $D = 8.64\, B / \Delta$ | Wrong unit of $\Delta$ |
| Discharge to irrigate A ha in t days | $Q = \Delta A / 8.64 t$ | Mixing up area in ha vs m² |
| Net irrigation depth | $d_n = (FC - WP) D \rho_b p / 100$ | Forgetting the factor $p$ |
| Gross irrigation depth | $d_g = d_n / \eta_a$ | Using $\eta_c$ instead of $\eta_a$ |
| Irrigation interval | $F = d_n / CU_c$ | Using gross depth instead of net |
| Reservoir safe yield | Rippl's mass curve | Not extrapolating to the worst year |
| Reservoir dead storage | Brune's curve $C/I$ | Misreading the curve at 0.4 vs 0.5 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define (i) Duty, and (ii) Delta, in irrigation engineering. State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Duty (D):** *Duty is the area of land in hectares that can be irrigated by a continuous flow of 1 cumec (1 m³/s) of water throughout the base period. Unit: hectares per cumec (ha/cumec).*

> **Delta (Δ):** *Delta is the total depth of water required by a crop to mature, from sowing to harvest, over the entire base period. Unit: cm or m.*

> **Relationship:**
> $$\Delta = \frac{8.64\, B}{D}\ \text{meters}\ \ \ \text{or}\ \ \ \Delta = \frac{864\, B}{D}\ \text{cm}$$

Where $B$ is the base period in days.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Perennial and Inundation Irrigation.**

**Model Answer (Tabular Form Expected):**

| **Aspect** | **Perennial Irrigation** | **Inundation Irrigation** |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Source | Impounded reservoir (assured supply) | Seasonal river floods |
| Supply Period | Continuous, year-round | Only during flood/high-flow |
| Cropping | Multiple crops (Rabi, Kharif) | Usually single Kharif crop |
| Control | Fully regulated via outlets | Uncontrolled, river-driven |
| Suitability | Semi-arid / canal command areas | Deltaic / floodplain regions |
| Example | Bhakra Nangal Project | Kuttanad, Kerala |

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] *A certain crop has a base period of 120 days and a delta of 0.50 m. Determine the duty of water for the crop. If the area to be irrigated is 4000 hectares, find the discharge required at the head of the canal.*

**(b)** [7 Marks] *Discuss the various factors affecting duty of water in an irrigation canal command.*

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Given:**
- Base period, $B = 120$ days
- Delta, $\Delta = 0.50$ m
- Area, $A = 4000$ ha

**Step 1:** Compute Duty:

$$D = \frac{8.64 \times B}{\Delta} = \frac{8.64 \times 120}{0.50} = \frac{1036.8}{0.50} = 2073.6\ \text{ha/cumec}$$

**Step 2:** Compute Discharge Required:

$$Q = \frac{A}{D} = \frac{4000}{2073.6} = 1.929\ \text{cumec} \approx 1.93\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

> **Or via direct formula:**
> $$Q = \frac{\Delta \times A}{8.64 \times B} = \frac{0.50 \times 4000}{8.64 \times 120} = \frac{2000}{1036.8} = 1.929\ \text{cumec}$$

✅ **Answer:** Duty = **2073.6 ha/cumec**; Discharge = **1.93 cumec**.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Factors Affecting Duty (Write Any 7 for Full 7 Marks):**

1. **Type of Crop:** Water-intensive crops (paddy) reduce duty; dry-foot crops (wheat) increase duty.
2. **Climate:** Hotter/drier conditions increase crop demand → lower duty.
3. **Soil Type:** Sandy soils with high permeability have lower duty.
4. **Topography:** Undulating land requires more water (lower duty).
5. **Method of Irrigation:** Drip/sprinkler > surface; hence duty improves with modern methods.
6. **Canal Lining:** Lined canals reduce seepage → higher duty.
7. **Distance from Source:** Long canals have higher transmission losses → lower duty.
8. **Ground Water Table:** A high water table reduces irrigation demand → higher duty.
9. **Farmer's Skill:** Better-trained farmers waste less water → higher duty.
10. **Rainfall & Season:** Effective rainfall reduces net duty required.
11. **Base Period:** Longer base period distributes demand over more time.

> **Diagrams Suggested:** Table of factors (as in Section 2.4.1).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] *A watershed has a 50-year record of annual streamflow. The average annual inflow into a proposed reservoir is 240 MCM. The minimum annual inflow recorded in the critical 5-year drought period is 90 MCM. If the annual evaporation loss is estimated at 10 MCM and seepage at 5 MCM, compute (i) Safe Yield, and (ii) the Dead Storage if Brune's trap efficiency at $C/I = 0.6$ is 0.94, with annual sediment inflow of 1.2 MCM for 50 years.*

**(b)** [7 Marks] *Explain the various zones of a storage reservoir with a neat sketch, and the difference between Live Storage and Dead Storage.*

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Given:**
- Mean annual inflow (long-term) = $240\ \text{MCM}$
- Mean annual inflow in critical drought period = $90\ \text{MCM}$
- Evaporation loss = $10\ \text{MCM/yr}$
- Seepage loss = $5\ \text{MCM/yr}$
- Trap efficiency = $0.94$
- Annual sediment inflow = $1.2\ \text{MCM}$

**Step (i) — Safe Yield:**

The **safe yield** is the maximum quantity of water that can be supplied in the **worst drought period** (5-year critical period) without the reservoir emptying.

For 5 years, cumulative inflow:

$$I_{5} = 90 \times 5 = 450\ \text{MCM}$$

Total losses over 5 years:

$$L_{5} = (10 + 5) \times 5 = 75\ \text{MCM}$$

Net usable water (over 5 years):

$$Y_{5} = 450 - 75 = 375\ \text{MCM}$$

Annual safe yield:

$$Y_{safe} = \frac{375}{5} = 75\ \text{MCM/year}$$

**Step (ii) — Dead Storage:**

Annual sediment trapped:

$$S_t = 1.2 \times 0.94 = 1.128\ \text{MCM/year}$$

Dead storage for 50 years:

$$V_{dead} = 1.128 \times 50 = 56.4\ \text{MCM}$$

✅ **Answers:** Safe yield = **75 MCM/year**; Dead Storage = **56.4 MCM**.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Zones of a Storage Reservoir (Diagrammatic Description):**

```
                        ^  Water Level
            MWL  -------|  | Maximum Water Level (under design flood)
            FRL  -------|  | Full Reservoir Level (Normal Pool)
            MDDL -------|  | Minimum Drawdown Level
            Bed  -------|__|
                 |       |
                 |  Flood Surcharge Storage (MWL to FRL)
                 |
                 |  Live / Effective Storage (FRL to MDDL)
                 |
                 |  Dead Storage (MDDL to Bed)
                 |  (Sediment accumulation)
                 |  Valley Storage (below original stream bed)
```

**Comparison Table:**

| **Feature** | **Live Storage** | **Dead Storage** |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Position | Between FRL and MDDL | Below MDDL |
| Usability | Available for irrigation/water supply | Not normally usable |
| Purpose | Active supply buffer | Sediment accommodation |
| Draw-off | Possible through outlet | Not possible in normal operation |
| Refill | Every monsoon | Rarely refilled (only in extreme floods) |
| Design Life | Unlimited (renewable) | Reduces over time |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Writing delta in cm but using 8.64 (which is for meters). → **-1 mark**
> 2. **Confusing $B$ and $T$:** Base period is the *crop period*; $T$ is the *time of irrigation*. → **-1 mark**
> 3. **Forgetting to convert ha → m²:** $1\ \text{ha} = 10{,}000\ \text{m}^2$. Skipping this gives $Q$ off by 4 orders. → **-2 marks**
> 4. **Missing "Readily Available Moisture" concept** in Part A — Examiner deducts if not stated.
> 5. **Trap Efficiency in Reservoir Question:** Students forget to multiply by trap efficiency before calculating dead storage. → **-1 mark**
> 6. **No Units in Final Answer:** Always write units in a box.
> 7. **Confusing Discharge Formulas:** $Q = A/D$ vs $Q = \Delta A / 8.64 B$ — they are equivalent; pick one and stick to it.
> 8. **Skipping Step 5 in Duty-Delta Derivation:** Examiners want all algebraic steps visible.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3:**

### **A. Irrigation Basics**
- ✅ Irrigation = artificial water application to crops.
- ✅ Necessity: rainfall inadequacy, drought, multiple cropping, food security.
- ✅ 5 key benefits: yield, cropping, drought protection, hydropower, navigation.
- ✅ 5 ill effects: waterlogging, salinity, seepage, health hazards, sedimentation.

### **B. Types of Irrigation**
- ✅ Flow vs Lift: gravity vs pumping.
- ✅ Perennial vs Inundation: reservoir vs flood.
- ✅ Surface (Furrow, Border, Basin, Flooding), Sprinkler, Drip, Sub-surface.

### **C. Soil-Water-Plant**
- ✅ Saturation, FC, WP, Available Water, RAM, Unavailable Water.
- ✅ Net depth $d_n = (FC - WP) D_{rz} \rho_b p / 100$.
- ✅ Gross depth $d_g = d_n / \eta_a$.
- ✅ Frequency $F = d_n / CU_c$.

### **D. Efficiencies**
- ✅ $\eta_c$ (conveyance), $\eta_a$ (application), $\eta_{cu}$ (consumptive use), $\eta_o$ (overall).
- ✅ $\eta_o = \eta_c \times \eta_a \times \eta_m \times \eta_f$.
- ✅ WUE = Yield / Water used.

### **E. Duty & Delta (MUST MEMORIZE)**
- ✅ Delta = $\frac{8.64\, B}{D}$ (m) = $\frac{864\, B}{D}$ (cm).
- ✅ Duty = $\frac{8.64\, B}{\Delta}$ ha/cumec.
- ✅ Discharge $Q = A/D$ cumec = $\Delta A / 8.64 B$ cumec.
- ✅ Total volume $V = \Delta A \times 10{,}000$ m³.
- ✅ 12 factors affecting duty; 8+ methods of improvement.

### **F. Reservoirs**
- ✅ Types: Storage, Diversion, Flood-control, Distribution, Detention.
- ✅ Zones: MWL, FRL, MDDL, Live Storage, Dead Storage, Flood Surcharge, Valley Storage.
- ✅ Safe yield via Rippl's Mass Curve.
- ✅ Trap efficiency via Brune's curve ($C/I$ ratio).
- ✅ Dead storage = sediment trapped $\times$ design life (50–100 years).

### **G. Common Numerical Patterns in KTU ESE**
- ✅ Compute duty given delta & base period.
- ✅ Compute delta given duty & base period.
- ✅ Compute discharge for a given area.
- ✅ Compute net/gross depth of irrigation.
- ✅ Compute reservoir safe yield from drought-period inflow.
- ✅ Compute dead storage using Brune's trap efficiency.
- ✅ **Always** show units in the **final boxed answer**.

> **MNEMONIC — "8.64 A DAY":** The number **8.64** is the conversion constant for *cumec × day → hectare-meter*. If you remember "8.64 a day", you can derive the entire Duty-Delta family.
