# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate Active:** Every algebraic step is shown. No jumps, no shortcuts. Read line by line.

## 3.1 Darcy's Law – Laboratory Setup Derivation

**Setup:** A horizontal cylindrical column of cross-section $A$ packed with sand, with two manometer taps separated by length $L$. Steady-state flow $Q$ is maintained.

**Step 1: Define the hydraulic gradient.**
The hydraulic head loss $\Delta h$ over length $L$ produces gradient:

$$i = \frac{\Delta h}{L} = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{L}$$

**Step 2: Assume linear proportionality (Darcy's experimental observation).**
The discharge $Q$ is proportional to the area $A$ and the gradient $i$:

$$Q \propto A \cdot i$$

**Step 3: Introduce the proportionality constant $K$.**

$$Q = K \cdot A \cdot i = K \cdot A \cdot \frac{dh}{dL}$$

**Step 4: Express discharge velocity.**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = K \cdot i = K \cdot \frac{dh}{dL}$$

> This is the **fundamental differential form** of Darcy's Law, valid locally at any point in the porous medium.

## 3.2 Steady Radial Flow into a Fully Penetrating Well in a **Confined** Aquifer (Dupuit–Thiem Equation)

**Setup:** A fully penetrating well in a confined aquifer of thickness $b$, pumped at constant $Q$. Two observation wells at radii $r_1$ and $r_2$ register heads $h_1$ and $h_2$.

### Step 1: Apply continuity of flow.
At any radius $r$ from the well axis, the flow is radial. The cylindrical surface through which flow passes has area:

$$A = 2\pi r b$$

### Step 2: Apply Darcy's Law locally.
The hydraulic gradient in the radial direction (Dupuit assumption) is:

$$i = \frac{dh}{dr}$$

Hence the discharge through the cylindrical surface is:

$$Q = K \cdot A \cdot i = K \cdot (2\pi r b) \cdot \frac{dh}{dr}$$

### Step 3: Separate variables.
Rearranging, all $r$ terms on the left, all $h$ terms on the right:

$$Q \cdot \frac{dr}{r} = 2\pi K b \cdot dh$$

### Step 4: Integrate between limits.
Integrate from radius $r_1$ (where head $= h_1$) to radius $r_2$ (where head $= h_2$):

$$\int_{r_1}^{r_2} Q \cdot \frac{dr}{r} = \int_{h_1}^{h_2} 2\pi K b \cdot dh$$

$$Q \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = 2\pi K b \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$$

### Step 5: Solve for $Q$.

$$Q = \frac{2\pi b K (h_2 - h_1)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

### Step 6: Introduce transmissivity $T = K b$.

$$\boxed{\;Q = \frac{2\pi T (h_2 - h_1)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}\;}$$

**Special Case – Single Well (no observation wells):**
If only the well radius $r_w$ (drawdown $s_w$) and radius of influence $R$ (drawdown $\approx 0$) are known:

$$h_1 = h_0, \quad h_2 = h_0 - s_w, \quad r_1 = R, \quad r_2 = r_w$$

$$Q = \frac{2\pi T s_w}{\ln(R/r_w)}$$

## 3.3 Steady Radial Flow into a Fully Penetrating Well in an **Unconfined** Aquifer

**Setup:** Identical to the confined case, but the upper boundary is a free water table. Saturated thickness varies with radius.

### Step 1: Apply continuity.
At radius $r$, the saturated thickness equals the head $h$ above the impermeable base (taking the datum at the base). The flow cross-section is:

$$A = 2\pi r h$$

### Step 2: Apply Darcy's Law.
The radial gradient is $dh/dr$ (Dupuit approximation). Hence:

$$Q = K \cdot (2\pi r h) \cdot \frac{dh}{dr}$$

### Step 3: Separate variables.
$h$ appears on the right, not $h^2$ yet, so we move $h$ to the right:

$$Q \cdot \frac{dr}{r} = 2\pi K \cdot h \cdot dh$$

### Step 4: Integrate between limits.

$$\int_{r_1}^{r_2} Q \cdot \frac{dr}{r} = \int_{h_1}^{h_2} 2\pi K \cdot h \cdot dh$$

$$Q \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = 2\pi K \cdot \frac{h_2^2 - h_1^2}{2} = \pi K (h_2^2 - h_1^2)$$

### Step 5: Solve for $Q$.

$$\boxed{\;Q = \frac{\pi K (h_2^2 - h_1^2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}\;}$$

### Step 6: Special case – single well.

Using $h_1 = h_0$ (at $r_1 = R$) and $h_2 = h_w$ (at $r_2 = r_w$):

$$Q = \frac{\pi K (h_0^2 - h_w^2)}{\ln(R/r_w)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference from Confined Aquifer:** Notice the **$h^2$ term in the unconfined equation** versus the linear $h$ term in the confined equation. This is the most-tested KTU board distinction!

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Confined Aquifer – Typical KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A fully penetrating well in a confined aquifer of thickness 30 m pumps at 1500 m³/day. Two observation wells at 30 m and 90 m from the pumping well record steady-state drawdowns of 5.0 m and 2.0 m, respectively. Find the transmissivity and hydraulic conductivity. Also estimate the drawdown at the pumping well of radius 0.15 m, assuming the radius of influence is 300 m.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Apply Dupuit–Thiem formula for $T$ (using the two observation wells).**

$$Q = \frac{2\pi T (h_1 - h_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

where $h_1 - h_2 = s_1 - s_2 = 5.0 - 2.0 = 3.0$ m, $r_1 = 30$ m, $r_2 = 90$ m, $Q = 1500$ m³/day.

$$T = \frac{Q \cdot \ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi (h_1 - h_2)} = \frac{1500 \times \ln(90/30)}{2\pi \times 3.0} = \frac{1500 \times \ln 3}{18.8496}$$

$$\ln 3 = 1.0986 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = \frac{1500 \times 1.0986}{18.8496} = \frac{1647.94}{18.8496} = 87.43 \;\; \text{m}^2/\text{day}$$

**Step 2: Find $K$.**

$$K = \frac{T}{b} = \frac{87.43}{30} = 2.914 \;\; \text{m/day}$$

**Step 3: Estimate drawdown at the well ($r_w = 0.15$ m).**

Use the **single-well form** with $R = 300$ m and outer reference (drawdown at $R$ = 0):

$$s_w = \frac{Q \cdot \ln(R/r_w)}{2\pi T} = \frac{1500 \times \ln(300/0.15)}{2\pi \times 87.43} = \frac{1500 \times \ln(2000)}{549.34}$$

$$\ln 2000 = 7.6009 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s_w = \frac{1500 \times 7.6009}{549.34} = \frac{11401.4}{549.34} = 20.75 \;\; \text{m}$$

> **Final Answer:** $T = 87.43 \;\text{m}^2/\text{day}$, $K = 2.914 \;\text{m/day}$, $s_w = 20.75 \;\text{m}$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Define the following terms: (a) Specific Yield, (b) Hydraulic Conductivity, (c) Transmissivity.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Specific Yield ($S_y$):** It is defined as the ratio of the volume of water that will drain from a saturated rock or soil under the influence of gravity to the total volume of the formation.

$$S_y = \frac{V_{\text{drained}}}{V_{\text{total}}}$$

It is dimensionless. Typical values: 0.10 to 0.30 for sands and gravels; very low (< 0.05) for clays.

**(b) Hydraulic Conductivity ($K$):** The volume of water that will flow per unit time through a unit cross-sectional area of a porous medium under a unit hydraulic gradient, at the prevailing temperature. Units: m/day or cm/s.

**(c) Transmissivity ($T$):** The rate at which water is transmitted through a unit width of an aquifer under a unit hydraulic gradient. It is the product of hydraulic conductivity and the saturated thickness of the aquifer:

$$T = K \cdot b$$

Units: m²/day.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**State Darcy's Law and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Darcy's Law states that for laminar flow through a saturated porous medium, the discharge velocity is directly proportional to the hydraulic gradient.

$$V = K \cdot i = K \cdot \frac{dh}{dL}$$

Or equivalently for discharge:

$$Q = K \cdot A \cdot \frac{dh}{dL}$$

**Significance of each term:**

- $V$ = discharge (apparent) velocity [m/day] – velocity based on total cross-section, not just pore area.
- $Q$ = volumetric discharge [m³/day] – total water flowing per unit time.
- $K$ = hydraulic conductivity [m/day] – property of both the medium and the fluid.
- $i = dh/dL$ = hydraulic gradient [dimensionless] – head loss per unit length.
- $A$ = gross cross-sectional area [m²] – total area perpendicular to flow.

**Limitations:** Valid only for laminar flow (Re < 1 to 10) and homogeneous media.

---

## 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the steady-state radial flow equation for a fully penetrating well pumping from a **confined aquifer**, and obtain an expression for the discharge in terms of drawdowns at two observation wells. (7 marks)

**(b)** A fully penetrating well in a confined aquifer of thickness 25 m yields 1200 m³/day at a steady drawdown of 12 m. If the radius of influence is 250 m and the well radius is 0.20 m, calculate the transmissivity, hydraulic conductivity, and the actual (seepage) velocity at the well face. Assume effective porosity $n_e = 0.25$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Define the radial flow geometry.** Consider a fully penetrating well pumping at constant rate $Q$ from a confined aquifer of uniform thickness $b$. By Dupuit's assumptions, flow is radial and horizontal.

**Step 2: At radius $r$ from the well axis**, the area of the cylindrical flow surface is:

$$A = 2\pi r b$$

**Step 3: The hydraulic gradient** in the radial direction is $dh/dr$ (Dupuit). Applying Darcy's Law:

$$Q = K \cdot (2\pi r b) \cdot \frac{dh}{dr}$$

**Step 4: Separate variables:**

$$Q \cdot \frac{dr}{r} = 2\pi K b \cdot dh$$

**Step 5: Integrate** between observation well 1 ($r_1, h_1$) and observation well 2 ($r_2, h_2$):

$$Q \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r} = 2\pi K b \int_{h_1}^{h_2} dh$$

$$Q \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = 2\pi K b (h_2 - h_1)$$

**Step 6: Solve for $Q$:**

$$\boxed{\;Q = \frac{2\pi K b (h_2 - h_1)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} = \frac{2\pi T (h_2 - h_1)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}\;}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $Q = 1200$ m³/day, $b = 25$ m, $s_w = 12$ m, $R = 250$ m, $r_w = 0.20$ m, $n_e = 0.25$.

**Step 1: Calculate Transmissivity $T$.** For a single well (drawdown = $s_w$ at $r_w$, zero at $R$):

$$Q = \frac{2\pi T s_w}{\ln(R/r_w)}$$

$$T = \frac{Q \cdot \ln(R/r_w)}{2\pi s_w} = \frac{1200 \times \ln(250/0.20)}{2\pi \times 12}$$

$$\ln(1250) = 7.1309 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = \frac{1200 \times 7.1309}{75.398} = \frac{8557.1}{75.398} = 113.5 \;\; \text{m}^2/\text{day}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Hydraulic Conductivity $K$.**

$$K = \frac{T}{b} = \frac{113.5}{25} = 4.54 \;\; \text{m/day}$$

**Step 3: Actual (seepage) velocity at the well face.**

$$V_a = \frac{V}{n_e} = \frac{Q}{2\pi r_w b \, n_e} = \frac{1200}{2\pi \times 0.20 \times 25 \times 0.25}$$

$$V_a = \frac{1200}{7.854} = 152.79 \;\; \text{m/day}$$

> **Final Answer:** $T = 113.5 \;\text{m}^2/\text{day}$, $K = 4.54 \;\text{m/day}$, $V_a = 152.79 \;\text{m/day}$ at the well face.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the steady-state radial flow equation for a fully penetrating well in an **unconfined aquifer** and hence obtain an expression for discharge in terms of drawdowns at two observation wells. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 0.40 m diameter well fully penetrates an unconfined aquifer of original saturated thickness 20 m. After a long period of pumping at 1000 m³/day, the steady-state drawdowns in two observation wells located at 30 m and 100 m from the pumping well are 4.0 m and 2.0 m, respectively. Determine the hydraulic conductivity of the aquifer. Also compute the drawdown at the well face if the radius of influence is 200 m. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: At radius $r$**, the saturated thickness equals the head $h$ above the datum (impermeable base). Cylindrical area:

$$A = 2\pi r h$$

**Step 2: Apply Darcy's Law** with radial gradient $dh/dr$:

$$Q = K \cdot (2\pi r h) \cdot \frac{dh}{dr}$$

**Step 3: Separate variables:**

$$Q \cdot \frac{dr}{r} = 2\pi K \cdot h \cdot dh$$

**Step 4: Integrate** between obs. wells ($r_1, h_1$) and ($r_2, h_2$):

$$Q \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = 2\pi K \cdot \frac{h_2^2 - h_1^2}{2} = \pi K (h_2^2 - h_1^2)$$

**Step 5: Solve for $Q$:**

$$\boxed{\;Q = \frac{\pi K (h_2^2 - h_1^2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}\;}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $r_w = 0.20$ m, $H_0 = 20$ m, $Q = 1000$ m³/day, $r_1 = 30$ m, $s_1 = 4.0$ m, $r_2 = 100$ m, $s_2 = 2.0$ m, $R = 200$ m.

**Step 1: Compute heads at observation wells.**
$h_1 = H_0 - s_1 = 20 - 4 = 16$ m
$h_2 = H_0 - s_2 = 20 - 2 = 18$ m

**Step 2: Apply Dupuit's unconfined equation:**

$$Q = \frac{\pi K (h_2^2 - h_1^2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

$$1000 = \frac{\pi K (18^2 - 16^2)}{\ln(100/30)} = \frac{\pi K (324 - 256)}{\ln(3.333)}$$

$$\ln(3.333) = 1.2040 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1000 = \frac{\pi K \times 68}{1.2040} = \frac{213.628 K}{1.2040} = 177.43 K$$

$$K = \frac{1000}{177.43} = 5.636 \;\; \text{m/day}$$

**Step 3: Drawdown at well face using radius of influence.**
At $r = R = 200$ m, $h = H_0 = 20$ m; at $r = r_w = 0.20$ m, $h = h_w$.

$$Q = \frac{\pi K (H_0^2 - h_w^2)}{\ln(R/r_w)}$$

$$1000 = \frac{\pi \times 5.636 \times (400 - h_w^2)}{\ln(200/0.20)} = \frac{17.704 \times (400 - h_w^2)}{\ln(1000)}$$

$$\ln(1000) = 6.9078 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1000 \times 6.9078 = 17.704 (400 - h_w^2)$$

$$6907.8 = 17.704 (400 - h_w^2) \quad \Rightarrow \quad 400 - h_w^2 = 390.20$$

$$h_w^2 = 9.80 \quad \Rightarrow \quad h_w = 3.13 \;\; \text{m}$$

**Step 4: Drawdown at well face:**

$$s_w = H_0 - h_w = 20 - 3.13 = 16.87 \;\; \text{m}$$

> **Final Answer:** $K = 5.636 \;\text{m/day}$, $s_w = 16.87 \;\text{m}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls – Do NOT Lose These Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $h$ with $H$:** In the unconfined equation, $h$ is the *saturated thickness* at radius $r$ (= head above impermeable base), NOT the water-table elevation above a horizontal datum. Forgetting $H_0 - s$ when computing $h_1, h_2$ is the #1 error.
> 2. **Linear vs Quadratic:** Confined equation uses $h_2 - h_1$ (linear). Unconfined uses $h_2^2 - h_1^2$ (quadratic). Writing the wrong form = full 7 marks lost.
> 3. **Logarithm Base:** All KTU well equations use **natural log ($\ln$)**. If you mistakenly use $\log_{10}$, your answer is off by a factor of 2.303.
> 4. **Units of $T$:** Often written as m²/day, NOT m/day (which is $K$).
> 5. **Forgetting to subtract:** When the question gives drawdowns $s$ but the equation needs heads $h$, you must convert: $h = H_0 - s$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist – Read Before Every Exam:**

- **Saturated Formations (4 types):** Aquifer (yields + transmits), Aquitard (yields a little, transmits very little), Aquiclude (yields but doesn't transmit), Aquifuge (neither yields nor transmits).
- **Key Aquifer Properties:** Porosity $n$, Specific Yield $S_y$, Specific Retention $S_r$ (with $S_y + S_r = n$), Hydraulic Conductivity $K$, Transmissivity $T = K b$, Storativity $S$.
- **Darcy's Law:** $Q = K i A$ and $V = K i$. Valid for **laminar flow** in saturated porous media.
- **Dupuit's Assumptions (7 standard ones):** Homogeneous, isotropic, fully penetrating, steady, radial, small gradient ($i = dh/dr$), constant $Q$.
- **Confined Well Equation (Dupuit–Thiem):** $Q = \dfrac{2\pi T (h_2 - h_1)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ – uses *linear* head difference.
- **Unconfined Well Equation (Dupuit):** $Q = \dfrac{\pi K (h_2^2 - h_1^2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ – uses *square* of head.
- **Single-Well Forms (using $R$ and $r_w$):** Confined: $Q = 2\pi T s_w / \ln(R/r_w)$. Unconfined: $Q = \pi K (H_0^2 - h_w^2) / \ln(R/r_w)$.
- **Radius of Influence ($R$):** The distance beyond which drawdown is essentially zero. Often determined empirically (e.g., Sichardt's formula $R = 3000 \, s_w \sqrt{K}$).
- **Logarithm:** Always use $\ln$ (natural log) in well equations; $\log_{10}$ requires a 2.303 conversion.
- **Drawdown Definition:** $s = h_0 - h$ (original head minus current head). $s > 0$ during pumping.
- **Actual vs Discharge Velocity:** $V_a = V / n_e$ (pore velocity > discharge velocity).
- **Storativity contrast:** Confined: $S \sim 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$. Unconfined: $S \sim S_y \sim 0.05$ to $0.30$.
- **Always state units in the final answer.** KTU evaluators explicitly look for $T$ in m²/day and $K$ in m/day.
- **Always re-check whether the question gives drawdowns or heads.** Convert using $h = H_0 - s$ before plugging into the formula.
- **Limitation of Dupuit's equations:** They assume horizontal flow; near the well face, the assumption breaks down and Jacob's correction or partial penetration corrections are needed (typically beyond KTU Module 4 scope but good to know).
- **Engineering Use Cases:** Well field design, dewatering projects, contamination studies, artificial recharge planning, dam seepage analysis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Wells, Types of Tube Wells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET501 - HYDROLOGY AND WATER RESOURCES ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Wells, Types of Tube Wells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wells — Formal Definition

A **well** is a vertical or near-vertical hydraulic structure, constructed by excavating, drilling, or driving into the earth's subsurface, specifically designed to extract **groundwater** from underlying aquifers for the purpose of domestic, agricultural, industrial, or municipal water supply. According to **KTU 2024 syllabus terminology**, a well is the most elementary unit of groundwater withdrawal and serves as the primary interface between the **saturated zone (phreatic zone)** of an aquifer and the surface environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A well is essentially a "controlled opening" into an aquifer. The **type of well** selected for a given site is dictated by the **geological strata, depth to water table, discharge requirement, aquifer characteristics (transmissivity, specific yield, permeability), and economic feasibility.**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Straw in a Glass" Mental Model

Imagine the **earth's subsurface** as a giant **sponge sandwich** — layers of clay, sand, gravel, and rock stacked on top of each other. The deeper, coarser, more porous layers (sandy gravel) act like a **water-soaked sponge**, while the clay layers act like **plastic wrap** trapping the water. A well is nothing but a **"straw"** inserted into this sandwich:

- If the water table is **shallow (1–10 m)**, a simple **dug well** (like scooping a hole with your hands) works.
- If the water is **deeper (20–300 m)**, we need a **drilled tube well** (like a long, thin syringe needle reaching the deep sponge layer).
- If the water is **under pressure** (sandwiched between two clay layers), a **flowing artesian well** releases water automatically without pumping — like puncturing a pressurized juice pack with a straw.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The choice of well is not arbitrary. It depends on **(a) depth of aquifer**, **(b) type of aquifer (confined/unconfined)**, **(c) required yield (liters per minute)**, and **(d) subsoil geological profile**.

## 1.3 General Classification of Wells (Master Overview)

Wells are broadly classified based on **method of construction**, **depth**, and **aquifer tapped**:

| S.No. | Classification Basis | Types of Wells |
|:-----:|:---------------------|:----------------|
| 1 | By Method of Construction | Open Well (Dug), Driven Well, Bored Well, Drilled (Tube) Well, Jetted Well |
| 2 | By Depth | Shallow Well, Deep Well |
| 3 | By Aquifer Tapped | Unconfined Aquifer Well, Confined Aquifer Well (Artesian) |
| 4 | By Method of Lift | Flowing Well, Pumped Well |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following **bold metrics** are universally used in KTU examinations while describing wells:

- **Depth of well (D)**: Measured in **meters (m)** or **meters below ground level (m bgl)**.
- **Diameter of well (Ø)**: For dug wells — **1.0 m to 10 m**; for tube wells — **100 mm to 600 mm**.
- **Discharge / Yield (Q)**: Expressed in **litres per second (Lps)** or **cubic metres per hour (m³/hr)** or **gallons per minute (gpm)**.
- **Specific yield (Sy)**: Dimensionless ratio (typically **0.01 to 0.30**).
- **Drawdown (s)**: Drop in water level due to pumping, in **meters (m)**.
- **Standard tube well depths**: Shallow ≤ **30 m**, Deep = **30–300 m**, Very Deep > **300 m**.

---

## 1.5 Types of Open Wells (Dug Wells)

An **open well** (also called a **dug well** or **sump well**) is a large-diameter well, typically of **cylindrical or square cross-section**, constructed by manual or mechanical excavation of the overburden until the water-bearing stratum is reached.

### 1.5.1 Shallow Open Well
- **Depth:** **5 m to 15 m** (rarely up to **20 m**).
- **Lining:** Masonry (stone or brick) or reinforced concrete — known as **well steining**.
- **Taps:** The water table (water table aquifer / unconfined aquifer).
- **Yield:** Low to moderate — typically **50 to 500 Lpm** (litres per minute).
- **Construction:** Hand-dug, with subsequent lining from top to bottom.

### 1.5.2 Deep Open Well
- **Depth:** **15 m to 30 m**, sometimes extending beyond **30 m**.
- **Construction:** Requires dewatering during excavation using a temporary centrifugal or submersible pump.
- **Taps:** Penetrates into a **second water-bearing stratum** below the first confining layer.
- **Special feature:** Incorporates a **borehole at the bottom** of the open well to enhance yield — this combination is called a **"combined well"** or **"dug-cum-bore well."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Combined Well (Dug-cum-Bore Well):** A traditional dug well with a drilled borehole (typically **100–200 mm dia.**) extending **10–30 m below the well bottom**, into a deeper aquifer. This is the most common rural water supply structure in Kerala, where the dug portion serves as a storage sump and the borehole taps deeper water.

---

## 1.6 Types of Tube Wells — Master Classification

A **tube well** is a small-diameter, deep, drilled well fitted with a **metal pipe (casing pipe)**, a **screen/strainer at the bottom**, and a **pump** (submersible or centrifugal with suction pipe) for lifting water.

Tube wells are classified on **three primary bases**:

### Classification A — Based on Method of Construction

| Type | Construction Method | Typical Depth | Soil Suitability |
|:-----|:---------------------|:---------------|:------------------|
| **Driven Tube Well** | Driven into ground by hammering a pointed pipe | **6 m to 30 m** | Soft, loose sandy strata |
| **Bored Tube Well** | Auger boring (hand or machine) | **15 m to 60 m** | Soft clays, medium-dense soils |
| **Jetted Tube Well** | High-pressure water jet | **15 m to 60 m** | Loose, unconsolidated sands |
| **Drilled Tube Well** | Rotary or percussion drilling | **30 m to 600 m+** | All formations including hard rock |

### Classification B — Based on Type of Strainer/Screen

| Type | Description | Use |
|:-----|:-------------|:----|
| **Strainer Type Tube Well** | Wire-mesh or perforated strainer on a pipe | Most common — for sandy aquifers |
| **Cavity Type Tube Well** | Open bottom pipe with cavity formed by pumping | For coarse gravel / boulder aquifers |
| **Slotted Type Tube Well** | Longitudinal slots cut into casing | For coarse to medium sand |
| **Perforated Type Tube Well** | Circular holes drilled in casing | For firm, stable formations |
| **Gault Perforated Type Tube Well** | Shallow, narrow slots punched in pipe | For fine to medium sand |

### Classification C — Based on Type of Aquifer

| Type | Aquifer | Artesian Condition |
|:-----|:---------|:--------------------|
| **Flowing Artesian Well** | Confined aquifer | Piezometric head > Ground Level |
| **Non-Flowing Artesian Well** | Confined aquifer | Piezometric head < Ground Level |
| **Water Table (Phreatic) Well** | Unconfined aquifer | No artesian pressure |

---

## 1.7 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Significance:** Questions on "Types of Wells and Tube Wells" frequently appear as:
> - **2-mark / 3-mark direct questions** asking for definitions or sketches.
> - **7-mark or 14-mark descriptive questions** demanding comparison tables, labelled diagrams, and site suitability analysis.
> - This topic forms the **conceptual foundation** for Module 4's well hydraulics (Theim, Dupuit, Theis equations).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Anatomy of a Tube Well — Component-by-Component

A modern tube well is not merely a "pipe in the ground." It is an engineered assembly of carefully designed components:

### 2.1.1 Vertical Sectional Components (Top to Bottom)

1. **Well Cap / Top Cover** — Prevents contamination from surface runoff and debris.
2. **Casing Pipe (Conductor / Housing Pipe)** — MS (Mild Steel) or PVC pipe that lines the upper non-aquifer portion of the borehole. It prevents **collapse of loose strata** above the water-bearing zone and stops **polluted surface water** from entering.
3. **Strainer / Screen** — The most critical component. It is a **perforated or slotted section** placed at the level of the water-bearing stratum, allowing water to enter while keeping out sand and gravel.
4. **Bottom Plug / Bail Plug** — A sealed cap at the bottom of the assembly, preventing silt entry from below.
5. **Gravel Pack** (optional but recommended) — **6 mm to 20 mm pea gravel** packed in the annular space between the strainer and the borehole wall, which:
   - Increases effective well diameter.
   - Prevents fine sand migration.
   - Improves hydraulic efficiency and reduces head loss.

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic — "How a Tube Well Works"

The operational sequence can be broken into **6 sequential steps**:

1. **Drilling** — A borehole of **150 mm to 600 mm diameter** is drilled to a depth intersecting the water-bearing stratum.
2. **Casing Installation** — The MS/PVC casing pipe is lowered into the borehole up to the top of the aquifer.
3. **Strainer Placement** — The strainer assembly is attached below the casing and lowered into the aquifer zone.
4. **Gravel Packing** — The annular space around the strainer is filled with graded gravel.
5. **Development** — The well is pumped at high rates (often with surging/airlift) to remove drilling mud, fine sand, and create a natural graded filter around the strainer — this is called **well development**.
6. **Pump Installation** — A submersible or turbine pump is installed for water extraction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Development" Matters:** An undeveloped well has a **clogged strainer** and a **mud-coated borehole wall**, resulting in **drastically reduced specific capacity (Q/s)**. Development can improve yield by **2 to 5 times**.

---

## 2.2 Comparative Analysis of Tube Well Types (Engineering Decision Matrix)

> The following **operational logic steps** form the **engineering rationale** for selecting each well type:

### 2.2.1 Strainer Type Tube Well
- **Why chosen:** Highest open area (15% to 40% of surface area), minimal head loss, low entrance velocity.
- **How it works:** Water enters through precisely sized openings — slot width chosen to retain **85% to 90%** of the surrounding aquifer sand.
- **Best for:** Sandy aquifers, high-yield requirements.
- **Limitation:** Expensive, prone to corrosion in mineralized water.

### 2.2.2 Cavity Type Tube Well
- **Why chosen:** Cheapest construction, no strainer required, ideal for coarse formations.
- **How it works:** The well is a simple open-ended pipe. When pumped, the upward flow of water **carries out fine sand** from the bottom, gradually creating an **underground cavity** (typically **1.5 m to 2.5 m diameter**). The cavity acts as a natural storage reservoir.
- **Best for:** Boulders, coarse gravel, cavernous formations.
- **Limitation:** Not suitable for fine sand (cavity will not form); risk of subsidence above the cavity.

### 2.2.3 Slotted Type Tube Well
- **Why chosen:** Simple fabrication, moderate cost.
- **How it works:** Slots of **1.5 mm to 6 mm width** are cut directly into the MS casing.
- **Best for:** Medium sand aquifers; temporary wells.
- **Limitation:** Lower open area (typically 8–12%), rapid corrosion, slot width changes under stress.

### 2.2.4 Perforated Type Tube Well
- **Why chosen:** Easy field fabrication.
- **How it works:** Circular holes of **6 mm to 25 mm diameter** are drilled into the casing pipe.
- **Best for:** Firm, stable formations (clay-bound sand, soft rock).
- **Limitation:** Very low open area (3–6%), high entrance velocity causing sand pumping.

### 2.2.5 Gault Perforated Type Tube Well
- **Why chosen:** Optimal slot shape for fine sand, prevents sand pumping.
- **How it works:** Shallow, V-shaped or louvered slots are punched in the pipe.
- **Best for:** Fine to medium sand, where conventional slots clog easily.
- **Limitation:** Low structural strength, suitable for shallow wells.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Typical Well Type |
|:--------------------|:-------------------|
| **Rural domestic water supply (Kerala)** | Dug-cum-bore well or shallow tube well with strainer |
| **Urban municipal supply** | Deep drilled tube well with submersible pump |
| **Irrigation in Indo-Gangetic plains** | Deep tube wells (100–200 m) with strainer |
| **Coastal alluvial aquifers (Karnataka coast)** | Cavity type tube wells |
| **Industrial water supply** | Drilled deep tube wells with multiple strainers |
| **Arid region (Rajasthan)** | Deep artesian flowing wells |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 4 — Wells & Tube Wells)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> While this topic is largely **descriptive**, the following **foundational formulas and definitions** are essential for higher-order questions and are foundational to **well hydraulics** (Theim, Dupuit, Theis) covered later in Module 4.

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|:-:|:---------------------|:-----------------------|:-------|:---------------------------|
| 1 | Specific Yield, $S_y$ | $S_y = \dfrac{V_d}{V_t}$ | Dimensionless | $V_d$ = volume of water drained; $V_t$ = total volume of aquifer |
| 2 | Specific Retention, $S_r$ | $S_r = \dfrac{V_w}{V_t}$ | Dimensionless | $S_y + S_r = 1$ (porosity, $n$ for unconfined aquifers) |
| 3 | Yield of a Well, $Q$ | $Q = A \cdot v = 2 \pi r h k \dfrac{dh}{dr}$ | m³/s | From Darcy's Law applied to a cylindrical flow surface |
| 4 | Specific Capacity, $C_s$ | $C_s = \dfrac{Q}{s}$ | Lpm/m | $Q$ = discharge; $s$ = drawdown — indicates well efficiency |
| 5 | Open Area of Strainer, $A_o$ | $A_o = \dfrac{Q}{V_e}$ | m² | $V_e$ = entrance velocity, typically limited to **≤ 0.03 m/s** for sand control |
| 6 | Entrance Velocity, $V_e$ | $V_e = \dfrac{Q}{A_o} = \dfrac{C \cdot A}{A_o}$ | m/s | $C$ = clogging factor (0.5 to 0.7); $A$ = total open area |
| 7 | Hydraulic Conductivity, $K$ | $K = \dfrac{Q \cdot \ln(R/r)}{2 \pi D (H - s)}$ | m/s | Used in confined aquifer well flow (Dupuit-Thiem) |
| 8 | Steady-State Drawdown (Confined) | $s = \dfrac{Q}{2 \pi T} \ln \left( \dfrac{R}{r} \right)$ | m | $T$ = transmissivity; $R$ = radius of influence; $r$ = well radius |
| 9 | Steady-State Drawdown (Unconfined) | $s = \dfrac{Q}{\pi K} \left[ \dfrac{1}{r} - \dfrac{1}{R} \right]$ | m | For unconfined aquifer with $H$ ≫ $s$ |
| 10 | Transmissivity, $T$ | $T = K \cdot b$ | m²/s | $b$ = aquifer thickness/saturated thickness |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Isolation Reminder:** Absolute value bars and natural log arguments in the above table use parentheses or `\ln(...)` form to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. In actual KTU exam scripts, vertical bars are used normally.

---

## 2.5 Decision Logic for Well Selection (Engineering Flow)

When an engineer is tasked with designing a well for a specific site, the following **decision hierarchy** is followed:

1. **Step 1 — Hydrogeological Survey:** Determine depth of water table, aquifer type, soil profile, expected yield.
2. **Step 2 — Yield Estimation:** Compute required $Q$ based on demand (domestic: **40 lpcd**; irrigation: **1 to 1.5 L/s per hectare**).
3. **Step 3 — Method Selection:** 
   - Depth < **20 m** + demand < **500 Lpm** → **Open well / Dug-cum-bore well**.
   - Depth **20–60 m** + sandy strata → **Tube well with strainer**.
   - Depth **60–300 m** + confined aquifer → **Deep drilled tube well**.
   - Coarse boulder formation → **Cavity type tube well**.
4. **Step 4 — Strainer Design:** Compute open area to keep $V_e \le 0.03$ m/s.
5. **Step 5 — Pump Selection:** Match pump capacity to $Q$ and total head.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Memorize the **entrance velocity limit of 0.03 m/s** — it is one of the most frequently asked constants in Module 4 well design problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since "Types of Wells and Tube Wells" is primarily a **descriptive and classification-based** topic, the derivations below focus on the **foundational hydraulic relationships** (Darcy's Law applied to radial flow to a well) and **strainer open-area calculation** that KTU examiners frequently test under "well design" sub-questions.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Discharge from a Fully Penetrating Well in an Unconfined Aquifer (Dupuit's Assumption)

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a fully penetrating well in a **homogeneous, isotropic, unconfined aquifer** of infinite extent. The well is pumped at a constant discharge $Q$, creating a **cone of depression** (drawdown curve) in the water table. At radial distance $r$ from the well centre, the saturated thickness (height of water above the impermeable base) is $h$. At the well face ($r = r_w$), $h = h_w$ (depressed water level). At the radius of influence $r = R$, $h = H$ (original static water table).

### 3.1.2 Governing Equation — Darcy's Law for Radial Flow

For steady-state, radial flow towards the well, Darcy's Law gives the discharge through a cylindrical surface of radius $r$ and height $h$:

$$Q = A \cdot v = (2 \pi r h) \cdot (K \cdot i)$$

where:
- $A = 2 \pi r h$ is the surface area of the cylindrical control volume at radius $r$.
- $v = K \cdot i$ is the Darcy velocity.
- $i = \dfrac{dh}{dr}$ is the hydraulic gradient (positive outward, but flow is inward).
- $K$ is the hydraulic conductivity.

### 3.1.3 Substituting and Rearranging

Because the flow is **inward** (towards the well), the gradient is negative. Taking magnitudes:

$$Q = 2 \pi r h K \frac{dh}{dr}$$

**Step 1 — Separate variables:**

$$Q \cdot \frac{dr}{r} = 2 \pi K h \, dh$$

**Step 2 — Integrate between the limits:**
- At $r = r_w$, $h = h_w$ (water level at the well).
- At $r = R$, $h = H$ (water level at radius of influence).

$$Q \int_{r_w}^{R} \frac{dr}{r} = 2 \pi K \int_{h_w}^{H} h \, dh$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate integrals:**

$$Q \cdot [\ln r]_{r_w}^{R} = 2 \pi K \cdot \left[ \frac{h^2}{2} \right]_{h_w}^{H}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute limits:**

$$Q \cdot \ln \left( \frac{R}{r_w} \right) = \pi K \left( H^2 - h_w^2 \right)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Q:**

$$Q = \frac{\pi K \left( H^2 - h_w^2 \right)}{\ln \left( \dfrac{R}{r_w} \right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Dupuit's Assumption:** This derivation assumes that the **hydraulic gradient $i = dh/dr$** is constant over the depth $h$ at any radius $r$ (i.e., equipotentials are vertical and flow lines are horizontal). This is reasonably accurate only when the drawdown is small compared to the saturated thickness.

**Step 6 — Express in terms of drawdown:**
- Let $s = H - h_w$ (drawdown at the well).
- Then $h_w = H - s$.
- Substitute: $H^2 - h_w^2 = H^2 - (H-s)^2 = 2 H s - s^2$.

**Step 7 — Final form (for $s \ll H$, $s^2$ is negligible):**

$$Q = \frac{2 \pi K H s}{\ln \left( \dfrac{R}{r_w} \right)}$$

This is the **fundamental discharge-drawdown equation for a well in an unconfined aquifer** — the **Dupuit equation** (1863).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Open Area of a Strainer (Well Design)

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A tube well with a strainer is to be designed for a discharge $Q$ in a sandy aquifer. The strainer is a pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D_s$ with perforations/openings. The goal is to determine the **minimum open area** $A_o$ such that the **entrance velocity** $V_e$ does not exceed the safe limit (0.03 m/s for fine sand, 0.06 m/s for coarse sand).

### 3.2.2 Governing Principle

The discharge entering the strainer equals:

$$Q = C \cdot A_o \cdot V_e$$

where:
- $Q$ = well discharge (m³/s).
- $A_o$ = total open area of strainer (m²).
- $V_e$ = entrance velocity through the openings (m/s).
- $C$ = **clogging factor** (accounts for partial blockage by sand, encrustation, biological growth).
  - $C = 0.5$ for severely encrusting water.
  - $C = 0.7$ for moderately encrusting water.
  - $C = 1.0$ for clean, non-encrusting water.

### 3.2.3 Derivation

**Step 1 — Express $A_o$ in terms of $Q$:**

$$A_o = \frac{Q}{C \cdot V_e}$$

**Step 2 — Express in more practical units (Q in Lpm, $A_o$ in cm²):**

Convert: $Q_{m^3/s} = \dfrac{Q_{Lpm}}{60000}$.

$$A_o = \frac{Q_{Lpm}}{60000 \cdot C \cdot V_e} \quad [m^2]$$

**Step 3 — Convert to cm² (× 10,000):**

$$A_o = \frac{Q_{Lpm} \times 10000}{60000 \cdot C \cdot V_e} = \frac{Q_{Lpm}}{6 \cdot C \cdot V_e} \quad [cm^2]$$

**Step 4 — Compute total strainer surface area:**

For a cylindrical strainer of length $L$ and diameter $D_s$:

$$A_{strainer} = \pi \cdot D_s \cdot L \quad [m^2]$$

**Step 5 — Compute the percentage of open area required:**

$$\% \text{ Open Area} = \frac{A_o}{A_{strainer}} \times 100 = \frac{Q_{Lpm}}{6 \cdot C \cdot V_e \cdot \pi D_s L} \times 100$$

### 3.2.4 Standard Permissible Entrance Velocities (KTU Reference Table)

| Aquifer Material | $V_e$ (m/s) | $V_e$ (cm/s) |
|:------------------|:-------------|:--------------|
| Fine sand | **0.03** | 3 |
| Medium sand | **0.045** | 4.5 |
| Coarse sand | **0.06** | 6 |
| Gravel | **0.09** | 9 |
| Boulders | **0.15** | 15 |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Numerical:** If $Q = 1500$ Lpm, $C = 0.7$, $V_e = 0.03$ m/s, then $A_o = \dfrac{1500}{6 \times 0.7 \times 0.03} = \dfrac{1500}{0.126} \approx 11{,}905$ cm². A strainer of 200 mm dia and 8 m length gives $A_{strainer} = \pi \times 0.2 \times 8 \approx 5.03$ m² = 50,265 cm². Required open area % = $\dfrac{11905}{50265} \times 100 \approx 23.7\%$. A **brass wire-mesh strainer** with 25–30% open area would suffice.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Capacity of a Well

**Definition:** Specific capacity is the discharge per unit drawdown — a direct indicator of **well efficiency**.

$$C_s = \frac{Q}{s}$$

**Significance:**
- Higher $C_s$ → more efficient well (greater yield per meter of drawdown).
- Typical $C_s$ for a properly designed well: **100 to 500 Lpm/m** in good aquifers.
- Declining $C_s$ over time indicates **well ageing** (scaling, biofouling, sand pumping).

**Step-by-step relation with hydraulic parameters:**

For an unconfined aquifer (from Dupuit's equation):

$$C_s = \frac{Q}{s} = \frac{2 \pi K H}{\ln(R/r_w)}$$

For a confined aquifer (Thiem equation):

$$C_s = \frac{Q}{s} = \frac{2 \pi T}{\ln(R/r_w)}$$

where $T = K \cdot b$ is the **transmissivity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** $C_s$ depends only on **aquifer properties** ($K$, $H$, $T$) and **geometric factors** ($R$, $r_w$) — not on the pump. So $C_s$ is a **hydrogeological characteristic** of the well-aquifer system, not of the pump.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Strainer Open-Area Calculation

**Given:** A tube well is to yield $Q = 2400$ Lpm from a fine sand aquifer. The strainer is **200 mm in diameter** and **10 m long**. Assume clogging factor $C = 0.7$. Permissible entrance velocity for fine sand $V_e = 0.03$ m/s.

**Find:** (a) Required open area of strainer. (b) Percentage of open area needed. (c) Verify if a wire-mesh strainer with 25% open area is suitable.

### Step 1 — Compute Open Area Required (m²)

$$A_o = \frac{Q}{C \cdot V_e} = \frac{2400 \text{ Lpm}}{0.7 \times 0.03 \text{ m/s}}$$

Convert $Q$ to m³/s: $Q = \dfrac{2400}{60000} = 0.04$ m³/s.

$$A_o = \frac{0.04}{0.021} = 1.905 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Convert to cm²

$$A_o = 1.905 \times 10000 = 19{,}050 \text{ cm}^2$$

### Step 3 — Total Strainer Surface Area (m²)

$$A_{strainer} = \pi \cdot D_s \cdot L = \pi \times 0.20 \times 10 = 6.283 \text{ m}^2 = 62{,}832 \text{ cm}^2$$

### Step 4 — Required % Open Area

$$\% \text{ Open} = \frac{19{,}050}{62{,}832} \times 100 = 30.32\%$$

### Step 5 — Verification

Wire-mesh strainer open area available = **25%**.

$$25\% < 30.32\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NOT SUITABLE}$$

**Conclusion:** The wire-mesh strainer with 25% open area is **insufficient**. Either:
- Increase strainer length to $L = \dfrac{19{,}050}{0.25 \times 100 \times \pi \times 20} = 12.13$ m, OR
- Use a higher open-area strainer (e.g., **louvered/perforated pipe** with 35–40% open area), OR
- Reduce $Q$ to match the 25% open area: $Q_{new} = 0.25 \times 62{,}832 \times 0.7 \times 0.03 = 329.9$ m³/s ... wait, that is wrong dimensionally.

**Recalculation check (final line):**

$$Q_{new} = A_o \times C \times V_e = 15{,}708 \text{ cm}^2 \times 0.7 \times 0.03 \text{ m/s}$$

Convert: $15{,}708 \text{ cm}^2 = 1.5708 \text{ m}^2$.

$$Q_{new} = 1.5708 \times 0.7 \times 0.03 = 0.03298 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1979 \text{ Lpm}$$

So a 25% open-area strainer can deliver up to ~**1980 Lpm**, just below the required 2400 Lpm. Hence, the original conclusion stands: **wire-mesh strainer is marginally insufficient** — the engineer should either use a higher open-area strainer or increase length to **12.13 m**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Define a tube well. List any four types of tube wells based on the type of strainer used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **tube well** is a small-diameter, deep, drilled well (typically **100 mm to 600 mm diameter**) that taps a water-bearing stratum located at considerable depth below the ground surface. It consists of a **casing pipe**, a **bottom strainer/screen**, and a **pump** for water extraction.

The **four types of tube wells based on strainer type** are:

1. **Strainer type tube well** — uses wire-mesh or perforated metal screen (open area 15–40%).
2. **Cavity type tube well** — has an open bottom; pumping creates a natural underground cavity.
3. **Slotted type tube well** — slots (1.5–6 mm) cut directly into the casing pipe.
4. **Perforated type tube well** — circular holes (6–25 mm) drilled into the casing.
5. (Bonus) **Gault perforated type tube well** — uses V-shaped/louvered slots for fine sand.

*(Any 4 correctly named types — ½ mark each; definition — 1 mark)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]`
**What is a "dug-cum-bore well"? Mention its two main advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **dug-cum-bore well** (also called a **combined well**) is a hybrid water supply structure in which a conventional **open/dug well** is constructed down to the first water-bearing stratum, and a **borehole** (typically 100–200 mm diameter) is drilled **10–30 m below the bottom of the dug well** to tap a deeper, more productive aquifer.

**Two main advantages:**

1. **Dual Source Reliability:** The dug well acts as a storage sump, ensuring continuous supply even during pump failure or power cuts. Water can be drawn manually from the dug portion.
2. **Higher Yield with Low Cost:** The borehole taps a deeper aquifer with higher transmissivity, yielding **3 to 10 times more water** than a plain dug well, at a fraction of the cost of a full deep tube well.

*(Definition 1 mark; each advantage 1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE 2024 Scheme Part B questions for Module 4 carry **14 marks** with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Below are **TWO independent choices** for practice.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Series-1, Modified]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **constructional features of a tube well**. List the functions of (i) casing pipe, (ii) strainer, and (iii) gravel pack. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **strainer type** and **cavity type** tube wells on the basis of construction, suitability, yield, cost, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A (a) [7 marks]

The **constructional features of a tube well** (from top to bottom) are:

1. **Well Cap / Top Cover (½ mark):** Prevents entry of surface contaminants and debris.
2. **Casing Pipe (1 mark):** A **MS (Mild Steel) or PVC pipe** of **100–400 mm diameter**, lowered from the ground surface to the top of the water-bearing stratum. Its **function** is to:
   - Prevent collapse of loose overburden strata.
   - Seal off contaminated shallow aquifers.
   - House the rising main (delivery pipe) of the pump.
3. **Strainer / Screen (1 mark):** A **perforated/slotted section** placed at the level of the water-bearing zone. Its **function** is to:
   - Allow water to enter the well.
   - Retain the surrounding sand and gravel (sand control).
   - Maintain structural continuity of the well.
4. **Gravel Pack (1 mark):** **Graded pea gravel (6–20 mm)** placed in the **annular space** between the strainer and the borehole wall. Its **function** is to:
   - Increase effective well radius.
   - Prevent fine sand migration.
   - Reduce head loss at the entrance.
5. **Bottom Plug / Bail Plug (½ mark):** Sealed cap preventing silt entry from below.
6. **Pump (½ mark):** Submersible or centrifugal-with-suction pump installed to lift water.
7. **Schematic Diagram (1½ marks):** (Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the labelled Mermaid schematic.)

#### Model Solution — Part A (b) [7 marks]

| Parameter | Strainer Type Tube Well | Cavity Type Tube Well |
|:-----------|:------------------------|:-----------------------|
| **Construction (1 mark)** | Wire-mesh or perforated metal strainer attached to bottom of casing | Open-ended pipe, no strainer; cavity formed by pumping |
| **Suitability (1½ marks)** | Sandy aquifers of any grain size; most versatile | Coarse gravel, boulder, or cavernous formations |
| **Yield (1½ marks)** | High — 1000 to 5000 Lpm depending on aquifer and strainer area | Moderate — limited by cavity size |
| **Cost (1½ marks)** | Higher due to expensive strainer material | Lowest — no strainer cost |
| **Limitations (1 mark)** | Strainer encrustation, corrosion, clogging | Risk of subsidence; not feasible in fine sand (cavity will not form) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Series-2, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain with a **labelled diagram** the working of a **cavity type tube well**. Under what conditions is it preferred? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tube well is to be designed for a discharge of **3000 Lpm** from a **medium sand aquifer**. The proposed strainer is **250 mm in diameter** and **12 m long**, with a clogging factor of **0.7**. Permissible entrance velocity for medium sand is **0.045 m/s**. Determine: (i) Required open area of the strainer, (ii) Total strainer surface area, (iii) Percentage of open area required, (iv) Whether a brass wire-mesh strainer with **30% open area** is suitable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B (a) [7 marks]

**Working of a Cavity Type Tube Well (5 marks + 2 marks for diagram):**

A cavity type tube well consists of a **plain MS casing pipe** open at the bottom (no strainer), driven or drilled into a **coarse gravel / boulder formation**. When pumping begins:

1. The high-velocity water entering the open bottom **carries out fine sand and silt** from the surrounding formation.
2. Gradual removal of fines creates an **underground cavity** (typically **1.5–2.5 m diameter and 2–3 m deep**) around the well bottom.
3. The cavity acts as a **natural storage reservoir**, increasing the effective radius of the well.
4. Coarse material (gravel, boulders) remains in place around the cavity, providing a natural "strainer" effect.
5. Water enters the well radially through the cavity wall, sustaining high discharge.

**Preferred Conditions (2 marks):**

- Aquifer composed of **coarse gravel, boulders, or highly permeable alluvial deposits**.
- Formation is **stable enough** to support the cavity roof without subsidence.
- Cost is a constraint — cavity type wells are **30–50% cheaper** than strainer type wells.
- Fine sand is **absent** (otherwise the cavity will not form and may cause sand pumping).

#### Model Solution — Part B (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $Q = 3000$ Lpm = $\dfrac{3000}{60000} = 0.05$ m³/s.
- $C = 0.7$ (clogging factor).
- $V_e = 0.045$ m/s (medium sand).
- $D_s = 250$ mm = $0.25$ m.
- $L = 12$ m.

**Step (i): Required open area of strainer (2 marks)**

$$A_o = \frac{Q}{C \cdot V_e} = \frac{0.05}{0.7 \times 0.045} = \frac{0.05}{0.0315}$$

$$A_o = 1.587 \text{ m}^2 = 15{,}873 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step (ii): Total strainer surface area (1.5 marks)**

$$A_{strainer} = \pi \cdot D_s \cdot L = \pi \times 0.25 \times 12 = 9.425 \text{ m}^2 = 94{,}248 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step (iii): Required % open area (1.5 marks)**

$$\% \text{ Open} = \frac{15{,}873}{94{,}248} \times 100 = 16.84\%$$

**Step (iv): Suitability check (2 marks)**

Wire-mesh strainer open area = **30%**.

Since 30% > 16.84% (required), the **brass wire-mesh strainer is SUITABLE** (and provides a comfortable safety margin of nearly 2×).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark Loss):**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Students frequently forget to convert **Lpm to m³/s** (divide by 60,000). This single error loses **2–3 marks** in one sub-part.
> 2. **Clogging factor C forgotten:** Many students omit the factor **C = 0.7**, leading to an over-designed strainer. Always check the problem statement.
> 3. **Confusing "strainer surface area" with "well discharge area":** $A_{strainer} = \pi D_s L$ is the **outer surface of the cylindrical strainer**, NOT the cross-sectional area of the well.
> 4. **Final conclusion not stated:** Always end with "SUITABLE / NOT SUITABLE with safety margin." Examiners award the final mark for this engineering judgment.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this section twice before the exam:**

- [x] **Open wells** are large-diameter (1–10 m), shallow (5–30 m), hand-dug structures; **tube wells** are small-diameter (100–600 mm), deep (20–300 m+), machine-drilled structures.
- [x] A **dug-cum-bore well** is a hybrid: open well on top + borehole drilled 10–30 m below the well bottom.
- [x] **Tube wells by strainer type:** Strainer, Cavity, Slotted, Perforated, Gault Perforated.
- [x] **Tube wells by aquifer type:** Water-table (unconfined) and Artesian (confined) — the latter may be **flowing** or **non-flowing**.
- [x] **Cavity type tube wells** are preferred for **coarse gravel/boulder aquifers**; cavity forms naturally by pumping out fines.
- [x] **Strainer type tube wells** are the most versatile and yield the highest discharge for sandy aquifers.
- [x] **Slotted type** uses longitudinal slots; **perforated** uses circular holes; **Gault** uses V-shaped/louvered slots.
- [x] **Standard permissible entrance velocities:** Fine sand **0.03 m/s**; Medium sand **0.045 m/s**; Coarse sand **0.06 m/s**; Gravel **0.09 m/s**; Boulders **0.15 m/s**.
- [x] **Open area formula:** $A_o = \dfrac{Q}{C \cdot V_e}$ (m²), with $Q$ in m³/s and $C = 0.5$ to $1.0$.
- [x] **Total strainer area:** $A_{strainer} = \pi D_s L$.
- [x] **Specific capacity:** $C_s = \dfrac{Q}{s}$ — a measure of well efficiency (typical good range: **100–500 Lpm/m**).
- [x] **Dupuit equation (unconfined):** $Q = \dfrac{\pi K (H^2 - h_w^2)}{\ln(R/r_w)}$.
- [x] **Gravel pack** = pea gravel 6–20 mm, placed in the annular space, increases effective radius and reduces sand pumping.
- [x] **Well development** is essential to achieve design yield — it removes drilling mud and fines.
- [x] **Artesian well** = confined aquifer with piezometric head above ground; **flowing artesian** = head is above ground level, well flows naturally.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:**
> 1. Always **draw a labelled diagram** for any 7-mark or 14-mark question on this topic — it secures **1.5 to 2 marks** even if the description is incomplete.
> 2. Use **comparison tables** wherever the question says "compare and contrast" — examiners award structured presentation.
> 3. Memorize the **five standard entrance velocities** — they are a **favourite KTU question**.
> 4. Always state **engineering judgment** in numerical problems (suitable / not suitable with reason).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Well Losses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCET501 - HYDROLOGY AND WATER RESOURCES ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Well Losses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In open wells and tube wells penetrating confined or unconfined aquifers, when water is pumped out, the **drawdown (s)** observed in the pumped well is never purely the result of the aquifer's own resistance to flow. A significant component of the total drawdown is consumed by turbulence and friction generated **inside the well itself** — at the screen openings, along the screen length, through the gravel pack, and in the suction pipe up to the pump intake.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Well Loss (s_w):** The head loss that occurs as water converges radially toward the well and then moves **through the well screen, gravel pack, and inside the casing up to the pump intake**. Mathematically, it is the non-linear (quadratic) component of the total drawdown in a discharging well.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formation Loss (s_f):** The head loss due to laminar (linear) flow through the aquifer matrix itself, from the radius of influence to the well face. This is governed by Darcy's law and is the **linear** component of drawdown.

The total drawdown in a pumped well is therefore expressed as:

$$s = s_f + s_w = BQ + CQ^2$$

where:
- $s$ = total drawdown in the pumping well (m)
- $s_f = BQ$ = formation (aquifer) loss (m)
- $s_w = CQ^2$ = well loss (m)
- $Q$ = pumping discharge (m³/s or L/s)
- $B$ = formation loss coefficient (s/m² or s²/m⁵) — depends on aquifer properties
- $C$ = **well loss coefficient** (s²/m⁵) — depends on well construction

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Straw Through a Honey Jar" Intuition

Imagine you are trying to drink thick honey from a large jar through a narrow straw. Two things happen:

1. **The honey's own viscosity** resists flowing through the bulk honey from far away toward the straw tip — this is the **formation loss** (it scales linearly with how fast you suck).

2. **The narrow straw itself** has its own friction, and air bubbles, partial blockages, and turbulence inside the straw add extra resistance that scales **not linearly but as the square** of the speed you suck — this is **well loss**.

If you double the suction speed, the honey-flow resistance inside the straw increases by **four times**, not two — that is the signature of a quadratic ($Q^2$) loss term. In a real well, the gravel pack, the screen slot openings, incrustation (mineral deposits), and the casing itself are the "straw" through which the aquifer "honey" must squeeze.

## 1.3 Key Metrics and Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Specific Capacity of a well** ($Q/s$): Discharge per unit drawdown, expressed in **L/s/m** or **m³/hr/m**. A higher specific capacity means an efficient well.
> - **Well Efficiency (η_w)**: Ratio of theoretical drawdown (formation loss only, by Jacob) to actual drawdown.
> - **Critical / Maximum Yield**: The discharge at which the well efficiency becomes uneconomically low (typically when well loss > formation loss).
> - **Step-Drawdown Test**: The standard field test used to separate $BQ$ from $CQ^2$ components using multi-step constant-discharge pumping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Well Loss matters in KTU examinations:** It directly controls well design economics. A poorly designed well screen can cause 50% or more of the pumped drawdown to be wasted as well loss, meaning you must drill deeper, install a higher-capacity pump, or pay more electricity bills. The Well Loss Coefficient **C** is the diagnostic number for this.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Origin of Well Loss — Why It Exists

As groundwater converges radially toward a well from a vast aquifer, the flow is essentially **laminar (Darcian)** because the Reynolds number in the porous matrix is small (typically $Re < 1$ to $10$). This laminar resistance produces the **formation loss**, which is proportional to $Q$ — the classical Theis / Jacob result.

However, at the **well boundary** itself, the flow regime suddenly changes:

1. Water must accelerate from a wide radial cross-section to the confined area of the well screen slots.
2. It must turn 90° from horizontal radial flow to vertical flow inside the casing.
3. It must pass through the **screen openings**, where the local velocity becomes much higher than in the aquifer.
4. It must squeeze through the **gravel pack** surrounding the screen.
5. It encounters any **incrustation, corrosion, or partial blockage** of the screen.

The Reynolds number inside and around the screen becomes large, flow becomes **turbulent (non-Darcian)**, and the head loss follows a **quadratic law** with discharge — exactly analogous to pipe flow where $h_f \propto V^2$ (Darcy-Weisbach).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

- **Step 1 — Assume total drawdown has two independent components:** formation (linear) and well (quadratic).
- **Step 2 — Formulate:** $s = BQ + CQ^2$.
- **Step 3 — Recognize that $B$ comes from aquifer parameters:** $B$ depends on transmissivity $T$, storativity $S$, well radius $r_w$, radius of influence $R$, and time $t$. From the Jacob equation:

$$B = \frac{2.303}{4\pi T} \log_{10}\left(\frac{2.25 T t}{r_w^2 S}\right) = \frac{0.183}{T} \log_{10}\left(\frac{2.25 T t}{r_w^2 S}\right)$$

- **Step 4 — Recognize that $C$ depends purely on well construction:** screen slot size, open area %, gravel pack gradation, incrustation level, and pump setting. It is **NOT** a property of the aquifer.
- **Step 5 — Conduct a step-drawdown test:** Pump the well at $Q_1$ for a steady time, record $s_1$. Change to $Q_2$, record $s_2$. Repeat for at least 3–4 steps ($Q_3$, $Q_4$).
- **Step 6 — Linearize the equation:** Divide by $Q$ to get $s/Q = B + CQ$.
- **Step 7 — Plot $s/Q$ vs $Q$:** A straight line with intercept $B$ and slope $C$.
- **Step 8 — Calculate well efficiency:** $\eta_w = \dfrac{BQ}{BQ + CQ^2} = \dfrac{B}{B + CQ}$ (or as a percentage).
- **Step 9 — Design the well:** Adjust screen open area, gravel pack, or develop the well to **reduce C**, then re-test.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the complete formula table for Well Losses. All equations are board-exam ready.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $s = BQ + CQ^2$ | Total drawdown = formation loss + well loss | m |
| 2 | $s_f = BQ$ | Formation (linear) loss component | m |
| 3 | $s_w = CQ^2$ | Well (quadratic) loss component | m |
| 4 | $B = \dfrac{0.183}{T}\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{2.25 T t}{r_w^2 S}\right)$ | Formation loss coefficient (Jacob) | s/m² |
| 5 | $C = \dfrac{s_w}{Q^2}$ | Well loss coefficient (test-derived) | s²/m⁵ |
| 6 | $\dfrac{s}{Q} = B + CQ$ | Linearized form for plotting | s/m² |
| 7 | $\eta_w = \dfrac{s_f}{s} = \dfrac{BQ}{BQ + CQ^2} = \dfrac{B}{B + CQ}$ | Well efficiency (decimal) | — |
| 8 | $\eta_w (\%) = \dfrac{BQ}{BQ + CQ^2} \times 100$ | Well efficiency (percentage) | % |
| 9 | Specific Capacity $= \dfrac{Q}{s} = \dfrac{1}{B + CQ}$ | Discharge per unit drawdown | L/s/m |
| 10 | $\dfrac{s}{Q^2} = \dfrac{B}{Q} + C$ | Alternative linearization (s/Q² vs 1/Q) | s²/m⁵ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical distinction for KTU exams:** $B$ depends on **aquifer** properties ($T$, $S$, $r_w$, $t$), but $C$ depends **only on well construction** (screen, gravel, development). This is the most asked conceptual question in viva.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design Optimization:** A new well's C can be predicted from lab models of screen hydraulic behavior; combined with B (from aquifer tests), engineers predict total drawdown before installation.
- **Rehabilitation Decisions:** If an old well's C increases over time (incrustation), the engineer knows to chemically treat or re-develop the well rather than deepen it.
- **Energy Cost:** Well loss contributes to **Lift (Total Dynamic Head)** that the pump must overcome. Lowering C directly cuts electricity bills.
- **Regional Groundwater Management:** Kerala's high-yield tube wells in the coastal alluvial aquifers are routinely tested for $C$ to maintain sustainable abstraction norms.
- **Boundary in Tender Documents:** KTU-affiliated consulting projects often specify a maximum allowable $C$ value (e.g., $C < 200$ s²/m⁵) as a construction quality benchmark.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Total Drawdown Equation (Hantush-Bierschenk / Rorabaugh)

The total drawdown $s(r,t)$ at radius $r$ from a well pumping at constant $Q$ in a confined aquifer is given by the Theis equation, which at large $u$ (small $r$, or large $t$) reduces to the Jacob approximation:

$$s(r,t) = \frac{Q}{4\pi T}\left[\ln\left(\frac{2.25 T t}{r^2 S}\right)\right]$$

This equation assumes **purely laminar** flow everywhere. However, the actual flow near the well is non-laminar due to the local acceleration described in Section 2.1.

To account for the **non-linear head loss near the well**, Rorabaugh (1953) proposed adding an empirical quadratic term. The justification is as follows:

**Step 1 — Write the Jacob equation in linear form:** For a pumping well, $r = r_w$:

$$s_f = \frac{2.303\, Q}{4\pi T}\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.25 T t}{r_w^2 S}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Define a lumped linear coefficient:**

$$B = \frac{0.183}{T}\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.25 T t}{r_w^2 S}\right) \quad\Rightarrow\quad s_f = BQ$$

This is the **formation loss** — the laminar component that scales linearly with $Q$.

**Step 3 — Account for non-laminar losses near the well.** Empirical and theoretical analyses (based on extending Forchheimer's law to porous media) show that near-well losses follow a **quadratic** form. In analogy with Darcy-Weisbach pipe friction, the extra head is proportional to $V^2$, and since $V \propto Q$, we get $s_w \propto Q^2$:

$$s_w = CQ^2$$

**Step 4 — Apply superposition (the two losses are independent and additive):**

$$s = s_f + s_w = BQ + CQ^2$$

This is the **Rorabaugh equation** (1953) — the cornerstone of well-loss analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If $C = 0$, the well is "ideal" (100% efficient). If $C$ is large, the well is poorly developed, partially clogged, or poorly designed.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation of the Step-Drawdown Test Procedure

The **step-drawdown test** is used in the field to determine $B$ and $C$ experimentally.

**Step 1 — Run the test.** Pump the well at constant $Q_1$ until drawdown stabilizes at $s_1$. Then increase to $Q_2$ (without stopping the pump), wait for stability, record $s_2$. Repeat for $Q_3$, $Q_4$.

**Step 2 — Write the equation for each step:**

$$s_1 = BQ_1 + CQ_1^2$$
$$s_2 = BQ_2 + CQ_2^2$$
$$s_3 = BQ_3 + CQ_3^2$$
$$s_4 = BQ_4 + CQ_4^2$$

**Step 3 — Linearize by dividing each equation by its Q:**

$$\frac{s_1}{Q_1} = B + CQ_1$$
$$\frac{s_2}{Q_2} = B + CQ_2$$
$$\frac{s_3}{Q_3} = B + CQ_3$$
$$\frac{s_4}{Q_4} = B + CQ_4$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the linear form** $y = mx + c$ where:

$$y = \frac{s}{Q},\quad x = Q,\quad m = C,\quad c = B$$

**Step 5 — Plot $s/Q$ (y-axis) vs $Q$ (x-axis).** A straight line results.

- **Slope of the line** = $C$ (well loss coefficient).
- **Y-intercept** of the line = $B$ (formation loss coefficient).

**Step 6 — Compute well efficiency at any operating $Q$:**

$$\eta_w = \frac{BQ}{BQ + CQ^2} = \frac{B}{B + CQ}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units check:** If $Q$ is in L/s and $s$ is in m, then $B$ is in s/m² and $C$ is in s²/m⁵. KTU exams frequently test this dimensional analysis.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (End-to-End Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Typical KTU-style 14-mark question):**
> A step-drawdown test on a tube well gave the following steady-state data:
>
> | Step | $Q$ (L/s) | $s$ (m) |
> |------|-----------|---------|
> | 1    | 10        | 2.1     |
> | 2    | 18        | 4.5     |
> | 3    | 25        | 7.0     |
> | 4    | 30        | 9.1     |
>
> Determine (a) the formation loss coefficient $B$ and well loss coefficient $C$, (b) the drawdown and well efficiency at $Q = 20$ L/s, and (c) the well loss at $Q = 20$ L/s.

### Part (a) — Compute $s/Q$ for each step:

**Step 1:** $\dfrac{s_1}{Q_1} = \dfrac{2.1}{10} = 0.210$ s/m²

**Step 2:** $\dfrac{s_2}{Q_2} = \dfrac{4.5}{18} = 0.250$ s/m²

**Step 3:** $\dfrac{s_3}{Q_3} = \dfrac{7.0}{25} = 0.280$ s/m²

**Step 4:** $\dfrac{s_4}{Q_4} = \dfrac{9.1}{30} \approx 0.303$ s/m²

### Step 2 — Fit a straight line $y = B + C\cdot Q$ using least-squares:

$$n = 4,\quad \sum Q = 83,\quad \sum (s/Q) = 1.043$$

$$\sum Q^2 = 100 + 324 + 625 + 900 = 1949$$

$$\sum Q\cdot(s/Q) = \sum s = 2.1 + 4.5 + 7.0 + 9.1 = 22.7$$

The slope $C$ and intercept $B$ from least-squares:

$$C = \frac{n\sum Q \cdot (s/Q) - \sum Q \cdot \sum (s/Q)}{n\sum Q^2 - (\sum Q)^2}$$

$$C = \frac{4(22.7) - (83)(1.043)}{4(1949) - (83)^2} = \frac{90.8 - 86.569}{7796 - 6889} = \frac{4.231}{907}$$

$$C \approx 0.00466 \text{ s}^2/\text{m}^5$$

$$B = \frac{\sum (s/Q) - C\sum Q}{n} = \frac{1.043 - (0.00466)(83)}{4} = \frac{1.043 - 0.387}{4} = \frac{0.656}{4} = 0.164 \text{ s/m}^2$$

### Part (b) — Drawdown and efficiency at $Q = 20$ L/s:

**Total drawdown:**

$$s = BQ + CQ^2 = (0.164)(20) + (0.00466)(20)^2 = 3.28 + (0.00466)(400) = 3.28 + 1.864 = 5.144 \text{ m}$$

**Well efficiency:**

$$\eta_w = \frac{BQ}{s} = \frac{3.28}{5.144} \times 100\% \approx 63.77\%$$

### Part (c) — Well loss at $Q = 20$ L/s:

$$s_w = CQ^2 = 0.00466 \times 400 = 1.864 \text{ m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** At $Q = 20$ L/s, out of 5.14 m of total drawdown, **1.86 m (≈36%) is wasted as well loss**. This is unacceptably high for a properly designed well (acceptable $\eta_w \geq 70\%$). The well needs redevelopment or screen replacement.

---

## 3.4 Algebraic Alternative — Closed-Form Solution Using Two Steps

If the examiner asks to solve using only two steps (a common KTU Part A variation), use:

$$s_1 = BQ_1 + CQ_1^2 \quad\text{...(i)}$$
$$s_2 = BQ_2 + CQ_2^2 \quad\text{...(ii)}$$

Solve simultaneously:

\begin{aligned}
s_1 Q_2 - s_2 Q_1 &= CQ_1^2 Q_2 - CQ_2^2 Q_1 = CQ_1 Q_2(Q_1 - Q_2) \\
C &= \frac{s_1 Q_2 - s_2 Q_1}{Q_1 Q_2 (Q_1 - Q_2)} \\
B &= \frac{s_1 - CQ_1^2}{Q_1}
\end{aligned}

Substituting the data from steps 1 and 2:

$$C = \frac{(2.1)(18) - (4.5)(10)}{(10)(18)(10 - 18)} = \frac{37.8 - 45}{-1440} = \frac{-7.2}{-1440} = 0.005 \text{ s}^2/\text{m}^5$$

$$B = \frac{2.1 - (0.005)(100)}{10} = \frac{2.1 - 0.5}{10} = \frac{1.6}{10} = 0.16 \text{ s/m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> The slight differences between least-squares and two-step values are normal — least-squares is statistically more reliable. KTU typically accepts either if shown with work.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram — Origin of Well Loss Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Pumping Drawdown s] --> B{Where is Head Lost?}
    B --> C[Formation Loss s_f = BQ]
    B --> D[Well Loss s_w = CQ squared]
    C --> C1[Radial flow through aquifer matrix]
    C --> C2[Laminar Darcy flow]
    C --> C3[Depends on T, S, t, r_w]
    D --> D1[Flow through screen slots]
    D --> D2[Flow through gravel pack]
    D --> D3[Flow inside casing to pump]
    D --> D4[Non-laminar turbulent loss]
    D --> D5[Depends on screen design and development]
    C1 --> E[Linear in Q]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D1 --> F[Quadratic in Q]
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    D4 --> F
    D5 --> F
    E --> G[s = BQ + CQ squared]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Well Efficiency n_w = BQ slash s]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Step-Drawdown Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Discharge Pump with Variable Throttle] --> W[Pumped Well]
    W --> A1[Aquifer Matrix - Laminar Flow]
    W --> A2[Well Screen - Turbulent Loss]
    W --> A3[Gravel Pack - Convergent Loss]
    W --> A4[Inside Casing - Friction Loss]
    A1 --> LSQ1[Step 1 Q1 s1]
    A2 --> LSQ1
    A3 --> LSQ1
    A4 --> LSQ1
    LSQ1 --> PLOT[s slash Q vs Q plot]
    LSQ2[Step 2 Q2 s2] --> PLOT
    LSQ3[Step 3 Q3 s3] --> PLOT
    LSQ4[Step 4 Q4 s4] --> PLOT
    PLOT --> SLOPE[Slope = C]
    PLOT --> INTC[Y intercept = B]
    INTC --> EFF[Calculate Well Efficiency]
    SLOPE --> EFF
    EFF --> DEC{Acceptable n_w 70 percent or more}
    DEC -->|Yes| USE[Well Approved for Operation]
    DEC -->|No| REDO[Re develop or Re design Well]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Block — Components of Drawdown Around a Pumping Well

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SURF[Ground Surface] --> SWL[Static Water Level]
    SWL -. Pre pumping .-> WL1
    WL1[Pumping Water Level in Well] --> DDL[Drawdown s in Well]
    WL1 --> AQL[Water Level in Aquifer at Well Face]
    AQL --> FL[Formation Loss BQ Linear]
    AQL --> WL2[Water Level at Radius of Influence R]
    FL --> WL2
    WL1 --> WLS[Well Loss CQ squared]
    WLS --> SCREEN[Screen and Gravel Pack]
    SCREEN --> CASING[Inside Casing and Pump Intake]
    DDL --> SUM[Total s = BQ + CQ squared]
    FL --> SUM
    WLS --> SUM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** The total drawdown $s$ (from static water level to pumped water level) is the sum of (1) the formation loss occurring in the aquifer between radius of influence and the well face, plus (2) the well loss occurring from the well face through the screen and up to the pump intake.
